title: testing(metamorphic): MST algorithm equivalence (boruvka == kruskal == prim total weight)
description: Metamorphic property: for any connected weighted undirected graph, the total edge weight of the minimum spanning tree must be identical regardless of which algorithm computes it (boruvka, kruskal, prim). Add tests/python/test_mst_algorithm_equivalence.py exercising the three new MST edge iterators (boruvka_mst_edges, kruskal_mst_edges, prim_mst_edges from br-wjv9o) on a parameterized matrix of random graphs (seeded gnp_random_graph at varying n/p, plus path_graph, cycle_graph, complete_graph, balanced_tree). Assert sum of edge weights matches across all 3 algorithms, and equals nx baseline. Catches regressions in any of the iterators silently dropping edges or picking suboptimal ones.
close_reason: Already shipped by another agent: tests/python/test_mst_algorithm_equivalence.py exists with 28 metamorphic test cases (boruvka == kruskal == prim total weight on gnp/path/cycle/K_n/binary_tree fixtures, plus disconnected, tie-breaking, and finite-weight sanity sub-cases) and they all pass. No additional work needed — the metamorphic property is locked in.

title: REVIEW: [HIGH] condensation used wrong SCC labeling (source-first instead of sink-first)
description: fnx.condensation(G) labeled SCCs via the Rust _condensation_raw internal order, which numbered source SCCs first. nx numbers SCCs in strongly_connected_components() order (sinks first). Consequence: C.graph['mapping'], C.nodes[i]['members'], and condensation edges had integer labels flipped relative to networkx, silently breaking downstream code that indexes SCCs by label. Fixed by rebuilding condensation on top of fnx's own strongly_connected_components iterator so labels stay bit-compatible.
close_reason: Fixed: condensation now iterates strongly_connected_components(G) and assigns indices in emission order, matching networkx's convention. Tested on 5-node 2-SCC DAG; mapping, edges, and members all match nx exactly; scc= arg path and empty-graph path also match.

title: REVIEW: [MEDIUM] node_connectivity on MultiGraph with parallel edges returned simple-graph value
description: fnx.node_connectivity(MultiGraph) called into _raw_node_connectivity which collapses parallel edges. On e.g. K3 with extra parallel edges between two pairs, networkx returns min-degree=3 (parallel edges inflate the degree bound) while fnx returned 2 (simple-graph κ=n−1). Fixed by delegating multigraph inputs to networkx to preserve parity. Simple Graph/DiGraph continue to use the Rust fast path.
close_reason: Fixed: node_connectivity now delegates when G.is_multigraph(). Verified on K3-parallel, K3 MG no-parallel, MDG with parallel edges — all match nx.

title: [code-review] dominating_set(start_with) port still 4.84x slower than nx — wrapper overhead in G[u] access
description:
br-r37-c1-lj6bo just shipped a native port for dominating_set(start_with=...). Profile on BA200 (500 calls, 278ms total) shows G[u] access dominates:

  ncalls  cumtime  function
  35500   0.142 s  _graph_getitem_from_adj
  35500   0.078 s  __getitem__ (AtlasView)
  35500   0.072 s  __iter__   (AtlasView)
  35500   0.057 s  _atlas
  35500   0.045 s  _private_adj_mapping

~50% of total time is in the AtlasView wrapper around G[u]. Same wrapper-bypass opportunity that closed the find_cliques gap (br-r37-c1-lgyq8) and square_clustering (br-r37-c1-7t95c).

Apply same fix: when graph has no nx-private-storage and isn't a multigraph, replace set(G[start_with]) and set(G[v]) with set(_GRAPH_NEIGHBORS(G, start_with)) and set(_GRAPH_NEIGHBORS(G, v)). The set-comprehension form preserves nx's iteration order, but here we're just consuming the set so order doesn't matter for correctness — only the SET contents need to match nx.

Estimated speedup: 1.5-2x based on profile (the per-call overhead of AtlasView is a fixed cost). Combined with the prior delegate→native port (14x), final speedup vs delegate would be ~25-30x.

P1 because the dominating-set call is a frequently-used primitive and the bypass pattern is now mature (3 prior applications).
close_reason: Shipped. dominating_set(start_with) now bypasses AtlasView via raw _GRAPH_NEIGHBORS / _DIGRAPH_NEIGHBORS for non-multigraph non-private-storage cases. Closed perf gap from 4.84x to 1.13x (within nx parity). Total speedup vs original delegate: ~45x. 256 tests pass; correctness verified on K4/P5/BA20/K33/DiGraph.

title: REVIEW: [MEDIUM] edge_connectivity returned float and ignored MultiGraph parallel edges
description: Two related defects in edge_connectivity: (1) simple Graph path returned a float (e.g. 2.0) via the max-flow pipeline while nx returns an int cardinality (2). (2) MultiGraph path collapsed parallel edges: on K3 with extra parallel edges fnx returned 2.0 vs nx's 3. Fixed by coercing int-valued floats to int before returning, and delegating multigraph inputs to networkx.
close_reason: Fixed: MG/MDG delegate to nx; simple Graph path casts integral float back to int. Verified across K3 simple, DG, MG with/without parallel, MDG — all match nx in value and type.

title: [code-review] with_ledger Option<R> return is silently discarded by callers; no #[must_use]
close_reason: Shipped. with_ledger now annotated #[must_use], so silent discard of the Option<R> return triggers an unused_must_use compile warning. 4 fire-and-forget callers updated to explicit let _ = with_ledger(...). Build + test pass on rch. Lifts prior g80ih fix's defensive guarantee from runtime to compile time.

title: dag_to_branching missing @not_implemented_for('multigraph') guard
description:
Follow-up to br-r37-c1-6nhsw. nx.dag_to_branching is decorated with BOTH @not_implemented_for('multigraph') AND @not_implemented_for('undirected'). The previous fix added the undirected guard but missed the multigraph one.

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.MultiDiGraph([(1, 2), (2, 3)])
  >>> fnx.dag_to_branching(g)        # silently succeeds
  >>> nx.dag_to_branching(nx.MultiDiGraph([(1, 2), (2, 3)]))
  NetworkXNotImplemented: not implemented for multigraph type

Fix: add @not_implemented_for('multigraph') equivalent guard before the existing undirected/cycle checks.

title: [reality-check] add nx.op.reverse
description:
fnx 16.3ms vs nx 2.49ms (6.6x slower) on BA200 directed. Edge-iteration + DiGraph::add_edge per edge. Port nx.algorithms.operators.unary.reverse to a Rust pass that builds the reversed adjacency in O(V+E).

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
close_reason: Implemented order-preserving native Rust reverse path for DiGraph/MultiDiGraph and restored copy=True to the native O(V+E) route; validated with cargo test -p fnx-python.

title: dijkstra_predecessor_and_distance pred dict iteration order drifts from nx
description:
fnx.dijkstra_predecessor_and_distance returns the predecessor dict with keys in distances iteration order, but nx returns it in insertion order during traversal (which differs because pred entries are populated when an edge relaxation finds a tentative path, not when the node is finalized).

Repro:
  edges = [(a,b,1),(b,c,2),(c,d,1),(a,d,5),(b,d,3)] (weighted)
  fnx.dijkstra_predecessor_and_distance(g, 'a')[0] -> {'a':[], 'b':['a'], 'c':['b'], 'd':['b','c']}
  nx .dijkstra_predecessor_and_distance(g, 'a')[0]  -> {'a':[], 'b':['a'], 'd':['b','c'], 'c':['b']}

The values are correct; only key order differs. nx populates pred[d] with 'b' when edge (b,d) is relaxed (before c is popped), then appends 'c' when (c,d) ties — so 'd' lands in the dict before 'c'.

Drop-in code that iterated the predecessor dict in nx's algorithm-discovery order broke. Fix: delegate dijkstra_predecessor_and_distance to nx (the local _single_source_dijkstra_path_basic_local helper initialises {node:[] for node in G} and would need a per-call adapter to track pred-insertion order).
close_reason: done

title: [code-review] _fnx.{diameter,radius,center,periphery,barycenter} silent-wrong-answer on directed input
description:
Audit (porting follow-up): the directed-collapse defect family I've been tracking (br-r37-c1-89n9d / wojl3 / ecqmz / kitjs / b4zwt / p7p7l). _fnx.transitivity was patched in br-r37-c1-p7p7l to require_undirected. The remaining 5 functions in the family still silently return wrong answers on directed input.

Source: crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs lines 3705 (diameter), 3726 (radius), 3747 (center), 3772 (periphery), 9725 (barycenter). Each unconditionally calls gr.undirected() and computes on the undirected projection, then falls into is_connected (which is undirected-only).

Repro on directed cycle5+chord (where directed-correct diameter is 4):
  >>> from franken_networkx import _fnx
  >>> G = _fnx.DiGraph(); G.add_edges_from([(i,(i+1)%5) for i in range(5)] + [(0,2)])
  >>> _fnx.diameter(G)   # 3 (undirected projection has shorter paths)
  >>> _fnx.radius(G)     # 2
  >>> _fnx.center(G)     # wrong subset
  >>> _fnx.periphery(G)  # wrong subset
  >>> _fnx.barycenter(G) # ['0', '2'] (correct nx is ['0'])

Sister function _fnx.transitivity already guards via require_undirected (br-r37-c1-p7p7l). Sister functions triangles, clustering, square_clustering, k_core also guard. The 5 distance functions are the odd ones out.

Fix plan (this tick): add require_undirected(&gr, name)? to each function. The Python wrappers fnx.{diameter, radius, center, periphery, barycenter} already route directed through native fnx.eccentricity / shortest_path_length paths (br-r37-c1-89n9d / wojl3 / ecqmz), so end users of the public API are unaffected. Direct callers of _fnx.* now get a clear NetworkXNotImplemented instead of a silently wrong value.

Build via rch exec -- cargo build -p fnx-python.

P1 because backend.py and any direct _fnx caller still see wrong answers; the wrapper-level masks helped end users but the underlying Rust function class is broken.
close_reason: Shipped. _fnx.{diameter, radius, center, periphery, barycenter} all now guard with require_undirected — directed input raises NetworkXNotImplemented matching the contract of every other undirected-only Rust function in fnx-python (transitivity, triangles, clustering, square_clustering, k_core). Public-API users unaffected (Python wrappers route directed through native fnx.eccentricity / shortest_path_length). Build passes. Cuts off the directed-collapse defect family at the source.

title: parity: is_minimal_d_separator missing kwargs + generate_random_paths weight default mismatch
description: Two remaining real signature defects: (1) is_minimal_d_separator(G, x, y, z) — fnx is missing the keyword-only included=None, restricted=None kwargs that nx accepts; users who pass them get TypeError. (2) generate_random_paths(G, sample_size, ..., weight=None, ...) — fnx default is None but nx default is weight, which causes sampled paths to ignore weights by default in fnx. Fix is_minimal_d_separator by routing through nx via _call_networkx_for_parity (mirrors find_minimal_d_separator fix in br-71glx). Fix generate_random_paths by changing weight default to weight to match nx.
close_reason: Verified already-fixed in main: is_minimal_d_separator now has (G, x, y, z, *, included=None, restricted=None) matching nx; generate_random_paths weight default is now weight matching nx. Both must have been aligned by another agent or earlier session commit between probe runs.

title: MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph constructor mishandles dict-of-lists and 3-level dict-of-dicts
description:
Two defects in fnx.MultiGraph and fnx.MultiDiGraph constructor parity with nx:

1) dict-of-lists symmetric input duplicates edges. For input {1:[2,3], 2:[1,3], 3:[1,2]} (the symmetric adjacency-list representation of K3), nx returns 3 edges via the 'seen-node' dedupe in nx.convert.from_dict_of_lists (which only adds (u,v) when v has not yet been processed). fnx adds all 6 edges. Graph (non-multi) is unaffected because Graph dedupes structurally.

2) dict-of-dicts 3-level input misuses inner dict. For input {1: {2: {'w': 1}}}, nx reads this as 'edge (1,2) with attr w=1' (auto-key 0). fnx instead reads it as 'edge (1,2) with key=w' (no attrs). nx detects 3-level vs 4-level by checking whether the inner-most dict's values are dicts; fnx always assumes 4-level for MultiGraph.

Both regressions affect drop-in compat for code that uses these constructors.

Repro:
  >>> nx.MultiGraph({1:[2,3], 2:[1,3], 3:[1,2]}).number_of_edges()  # 3
  >>> fnx.MultiGraph({1:[2,3], 2:[1,3], 3:[1,2]}).number_of_edges()  # 6
  >>> list(nx.MultiGraph({1:{2:{'w':1}}}).edges(keys=True, data=True))  # [(1,2,0,{'w':1})]
  >>> list(fnx.MultiGraph({1:{2:{'w':1}}}).edges(keys=True, data=True))  # [(1,2,'w',{})]

title: parity: ISMAGS class missing from isomorphism API
description: NetworkX exposes networkx.algorithms.isomorphism.ISMAGS(graph, subgraph, node_match=None, edge_match=None, cache=None) with is_isomorphic(), isomorphisms_iter(), subgraph_is_isomorphic(), subgraph_isomorphisms_iter(), and largest_common_subgraph(). FrankenNetworkX does not export ISMAGS. Add an ISMAGS wrapper that converts FrankenNetworkX graph objects via _networkx_graph_for_parity and delegates to NetworkX, plus parity tests for constructor signature and core methods.
close_reason: Completed in 2c0f4aa

title: parity: transitive_closure_dag silent on undirected; weakly_connected_components / topological_generations have wrong error wording
description: Continuation of br-r37-c1-yf4tf and br-r37-c1-4elbw. Four more drifts: (1) transitive_closure_dag silently runs on undirected input; nx raises NetworkXNotImplemented('not implemented for undirected type'). (2) topological_generations on undirected raises NetworkXError('Topological generations not defined on undirected graphs.'); nx raises NetworkXError('Topological sort not defined on undirected graphs.'). (3) weakly_connected_components on undirected raises 'weakly_connected_components is not defined for undirected graphs. Use connected_components instead.'; nx raises 'not implemented for undirected type'. (4) number_weakly_connected_components has the same divergence. Fix: align all four to nx's exact error wording.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 8c1b91c. weakly_connected_components, number_weakly_connected_components, topological_generations, and transitive_closure_dag now raise with nx's exact error wording. 8 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: dfs_predecessors / dfs_successors dict iteration order drifts from nx
close_reason: done

title: parity: biconnected_component_edges returns list, nx returns generator
description: fnx.biconnected_component_edges(G) returns a list while nx returns a generator. Same iter contract issue as br-r37-c1-682kr (8 traversal/path/clique iterators) and br-r37-c1-ohxpp (edge_boundary). Drop-in code doing isinstance(result, types.GeneratorType) breaks; short-circuit short-circuits like next(biconnected_component_edges(huge_graph)) materialise the entire result on fnx vs. one component on nx. Fix: convert wrapper to generator function.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 129af9c. biconnected_component_edges now returns a true generator. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [modes-of-reasoning] perf-vs-parity tradeoff
description: Mode report: Perf-vs-parity tradeoff lens. Finding: users and reviewers cannot currently see how much of a workload is on Rust fast paths vs NetworkX parity fallbacks. Evidence: docs/coverage.md:9-11 reports RUST_NATIVE=21, PY_WRAPPER=855, NX_DELEGATED=0, but a direct source count found 170 _call_networkx_for_parity(...) and 16 _call_networkx_submodule_for_parity(...) call sites in python/franken_networkx/__init__.py. Evidence: python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:3427-3447 and many shortest-path call sites route non-unit weighted graphs away from raw Rust for correctness. Evidence: artifacts/perf/slo_thresholds.json:6-87 measures family SLOs but not fast-path/delegated-path mix. Action: add a delegation ledger and benchmark split that reports, per public function and representative argument shape, whether execution is native Rust, Python wrapper, or NetworkX fallback; performance gates should include at least one common fallback-heavy parity case so speedups do not hide compatibility routing costs.
close_reason: Added generated NetworkX helper delegation ledger and weighted delegated shortest-path SLO probe.

title: tree_broadcast_center / tree_broadcast_time / maximal_matching missing multigraph guard (Rust-direct exports)
description:
Three Rust-direct re-exports lack the @not_implemented_for('multigraph') guard that nx applies. Scoped out of br-r37-c1-tqimg because they don't have Python wrappers — they're imported directly from the Rust extension.

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.MultiGraph([(1,2),(2,3)])
  >>> fnx.tree_broadcast_center(g)        # silently runs Rust impl
  >>> nx.tree_broadcast_center(nx.MultiGraph([(1,2),(2,3)]))
  NetworkXNotImplemented: not implemented for multigraph type

  Same for tree_broadcast_time and maximal_matching.

For tree_broadcast_*, the directed guard is already correctly enforced by the Rust binding (both fnx and nx raise on DiGraph). Only the multigraph guard is missing.

Fix: replace the bare from franken_networkx._fnx import maximal_matching (and the tree_broadcast pair) with thin Python wrappers in __init__.py that add the multigraph guard before delegating to the Rust impl.

title: find_cycle (undirected) edge-tuple direction drifts from nx
description:
fnx.find_cycle on an undirected graph yields edges in algorithm-canonical orientation, but nx yields edges in DFS-traversal direction (which depends on adj-iteration order).

Repro:
  edges = [('c','d'), ('a','b'), ('b','c'), ('d','e'), ('a','c')]
  fnx.find_cycle(g) -> [('c','a'), ('a','b'), ('b','c')]
  nx.find_cycle(gx) -> [('c','b'), ('b','a'), ('a','c')]

Edge tuples are reversed and the cycle is traversed in the opposite DFS direction. Fix: delegate find_cycle to nx for undirected graphs (the directed case already matches).
close_reason: done

title: [parity-gap] average_clustering fast path has float last-bit drift from nx
description:
Review finding from recent clustering fast-path work (71583c98). The public clustering(G) dict matches NetworkX, but average_clustering(G) routes through _raw_average_clustering and accumulates in a different order, causing observable exact-float drift from nx on insertion-ordered graphs.

Repro:
PYTHONPATH=python python3 - <<'PY'
import networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
edges = [('c','d'),('a','b'),('b','c'),('d','e'),('a','c')]
for name, mod in [('nx', nx), ('fnx', fnx)]:
    G = mod.Graph(); G.add_edges_from(edges)
    print(name, repr(mod.clustering(G)), repr(mod.average_clustering(G)))
PY

Expected fnx average_clustering to match nx exactly because clustering(G) already matches. Actual: nx returns 0.4666666666666667 while fnx returns 0.4666666666666666. A second graph [(0,1),(1,2),(2,0),(2,3),(3,4),(4,2)] diverges in the other direction: nx 0.8666666666666666 vs fnx 0.8666666666666668. Fix by preserving NetworkX's Python-level summation contract rather than using the raw aggregate fast path for public parity.
close_reason: Fixed: average_clustering now uses the public clustering() result and Python insertion-order summation, matching NetworkX exact float outputs; regression test covers two last-bit drift repros.

title: strongly_connected_components order disagrees with nx on multi-component DiGraphs
description:
fnx.strongly_connected_components currently materialises Rust's SCC output and applies reversed() to match nx. The reversed() flip happens to match small-symmetric cases but is wrong on inputs where Rust and nx disagree on component-discovery start node.

Repro:
  edges = [('c','d'),('a','b'),('b','c'),('d','a'),('c','a'),('e','f'),('f','e')]
  fnx -> [{'f','e'}, {'a','b','c','d'}]
  nx  -> [{'a','b','c','d'}, {'f','e'}]
  edges = [('a','b'),('b','a'),('c','d'),('d','c')]
  fnx -> [{'c','d'}, {'b','a'}]
  nx  -> [{'b','a'}, {'c','d'}]

Fix: delegate strongly_connected_components to nx for parity (Rust + reversed() heuristic is unsound).
close_reason: done

title: Make conformance dashboard golden output deterministic
description: testing-golden-artifacts found that scripts/generate_conformance_dashboard.py declares deterministic=true but writes datetime.now() into generated_at_utc and builds run_id from the current epoch timestamp. Re-running the generator changes artifacts/conformance/latest/conformance_dashboard_v1.json without any input change, which defeats exact golden comparison and freshness reviews. Fix by deriving stable metadata from input artifact content and add a regression test that generate_dashboard() returns identical output across repeated calls. UUID: 3854183f-664f-490a-9848-232d701ac063.
close_reason: Conformance dashboard now derives generated_at_utc from stable input artifacts and run_id from a content hash; added an exact repeated-generation regression test.

title: Parity drifts: edge_betweenness_centrality_subset, find_negative_cycle, triad_type
parity: all_pairs_dijkstra_path / all_pairs_dijkstra_path_length outer-key iteration order doesn't match nx
description: fnx.all_pairs_dijkstra_path and fnx.all_pairs_dijkstra_path_length yield outer-key (source) iteration in arbitrary Rust internal order (e.g. ['a','e','c','d','b']) instead of nx's node-insertion order (['c','d','a','b','e']). Drop-in code that iterates 'for source, dists in result.items(): ...' breaks. Same iteration-order family fixed for triangles/core_number/etc. Note: outer keys for all_pairs_shortest_path[_length] already match nx; only dijkstra variants need the fix.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 40d5417. all_pairs_dijkstra_path / _length outer keys now iterate in node-insertion order matching nx. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: DiGraph.reverse() / fnx.reverse() edge + adj iteration order drifts from nx
description:
fnx.reverse() (and DiGraph.reverse() method) on a DiGraph returns a copy whose edge-iteration order and per-node adj-iteration order differ from nx.

Repro:
  edges = [('a','b'),('b','c'),('c','d')]  # path
  fnx.reverse(dg).edges() -> [('c','b'),('d','c'),('b','a')]
  nx.reverse(dgx).edges() -> [('b','a'),('c','b'),('d','c')]

  edges = [(0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,0)]  # cycle
  fnx -> [(1,0),(3,2),(0,3),(2,1)]
  nx  -> [(0,3),(1,0),(2,1),(3,2)]

  edges = [('c','d'),('a','b'),('b','c'),('d','a'),('c','a')]
  fnx adj['a'] = ['d', 'c']
  nx adj['a']  = ['c', 'd']

nx iterates nodes in insertion order and emits reversed (v→u) edges in that order; fnx's Rust-side reverse traverses internal adjacency in a different order.

The copy=False path matches. The copy=True path (default for both function and method) drifts.

Fix: delegate the copy=True path to nx so the resulting DiGraph's edge + adj iteration matches nx's contract.
close_reason: done

title: [code-review] is_planar / core_number / square_clustering missing from golden snapshot
description:
br-r37-c1-gttlp (is_planar fast path), br-r37-c1-fbons (core_number self-loop guard perf), and br-r37-c1-7t95c (square_clustering AtlasView bypass) all shipped recent fixes with unit-test, fuzz, metamorphic, and conformance-lock coverage — but NO golden-snapshot coverage. Subtle changes (e.g. precision drift, dict-iteration order changes, or set-membership reordering) could pass functional tests but produce a visibly different output bytes that a human reading the snapshot would catch.

Also missing from the existing golden: the canonical Kuratowski-pair fixtures Petersen and K5, even though the corresponding planar fixes flagged them as critical test cases.

Concrete fix plan (this tick):
  1. Extend GRAPH_FACTORIES with petersen and k5 fixtures.
  2. Add is_planar / core_number / square_clustering to the per-graph payload (undirected only — nx contracts say is_planar / core_number raise on directed via @not_implemented_for).
  3. Regenerate via UPDATE_GOLDENS=1.

P1 because the recent fixes are perf-sensitive (br-r37-c1-7t95c was a 16% perf gain via the bypass) and a future micro-regression could re-route through the slow path silently. The golden snapshot freezes the EXACT output bytes.
close_reason: Shipped. Golden snapshot extended with petersen and k5 fixtures (canonical Kuratowski-pair non-planar) plus is_planar / core_number / square_clustering algorithm payload entries. 7051 lines (was 5558). Locks the recent br-r37-c1-{gttlp, fbons, 7t95c} fix outputs byte-exactly — complements the existing fuzz / metamorphic / conformance coverage.

title: k_truss subgraph node order drifts from nx
description:
fnx.k_truss builds the resulting subgraph from Rust output which iterates nodes in canonical/sort order, while nx iterates them in input-graph node order.

Repro:
  edges = [('c','d'),('a','b'),('b','c'),('d','e'),('a','c')]
  fnx.k_truss(g, 2).nodes() -> ['a','b','c','d','e']
  nx.k_truss(gx, 2).nodes()  -> ['c','d','a','b','e']

Drop-in code that iterated R.nodes() / R.edges() / R.adj of the k-truss subgraph in nx's input-graph-order broke. Fix: delegate k_truss to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity + _from_nx_graph for output rehydration.
close_reason: done

title: Graph.copy() does deep copy — nx documents and implements shallow copy
description:
fnx's Graph.copy() (and DiGraph/MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph) deep-copies node/edge/graph attributes via copy.deepcopy. nx documents and implements shallow copy: G.copy() returns a graph whose nodes/edges/graph dicts are *new* dicts but whose values (e.g. nested lists/dicts inside node attrs) are shared with the original.

Repro:
  >>> G = fnx.Graph(); G.add_node(1, attrs={'list': [1,2,3]})
  >>> H = G.copy()
  >>> H.nodes[1]['attrs'] is G.nodes[1]['attrs']  # fnx: False, nx: True
  >>> H.nodes[1]['attrs']['list'].append(99)
  >>> G.nodes[1]['attrs']['list']  # fnx: [1,2,3], nx: [1,2,3,99]

This is documented in nx.Graph.copy: 'an independent shallow copy of the graph and attributes. That is, if an attribute is a container, that container is shared by the original and the copy.'

Root cause: _copy_preserving_insertion_order in __init__.py uses deepcopy(attrs) and deepcopy(self.graph). Should mirror nx.Graph.copy's d.copy() / datadict.copy() / G.graph.update(self.graph) shallow-copy semantics.

Note: copy.deepcopy(G) (Python's built-in) MUST still do full deep copy — that path goes through __deepcopy__ which is correct. Only G.copy() needs the change.

title: TestSigmaParity / TestOmegaParity tests assert no-fallback but br-smallrng delegates
description:
9 pre-existing test failures in test_graph_metrics.py:
- TestSigmaParity::test_matches_networkx_without_fallback[path4, cycle4, complete4]
- TestSigmaParity::test_error_contract_matches_networkx_without_fallback[disconnected4]
- TestOmegaParity::test_matches_networkx_without_fallback[complete4]
- TestOmegaParity::test_error_contract_matches_networkx_without_fallback[path3, path4, cycle4, disconnected4]

The tests assert that fnx.sigma / fnx.omega compute the result WITHOUT delegating to nx (monkeypatched nx.sigma / nx.omega raises AssertionError if called). But br-smallrng made these wrappers delegate to nx — fnx's local loop with per-iteration derived seeds produced different numeric results than nx's stateful single-RNG approach, so the project chose delegation for exact parity.

Tests are stale — they assert the pre-br-smallrng contract.

Fix: drop test_matches_networkx_without_fallback entirely for sigma/omega (they now legitimately delegate), and remove the error_contract cases that hit the delegation path (only the cases that hit fnx's pre-checks survive).
close_reason: done

title: [reality-check] add nx.centrality.load_centrality
description:
fnx 234ms vs nx 62ms (3.8x slower) on BA200. Brandes-style O(VE) but currently routes via Python. Port nx.algorithms.centrality.load.load_centrality directly (analogous to the perf-pagerank pattern from a04084e0).

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
close_reason: Shipped in 32ba3622. Rewrote Rust load_centrality_generic to implement Newman's load (was computing Brandes' betweenness — semantically wrong). Bit-exact parity vs nx (max_diff=0.0). BA200: 0.26x → 4.14x. BA1000: 0.26x → 3.72x (16x improvement). 15 tests pass.

title: [parity-gap] all_shortest_paths must preserve NetworkX backend keyword signature
description:
Audit source: suspicious commit 54ecea7a. all_shortest_paths now returns a true generator, but franken_networkx.all_shortest_paths still lacks upstream's keyword-only backend/backend_kwargs signature. Repro: PYTHONPATH=python python3 - <<'PY'
import networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
ng = nx.path_graph(3); fg = fnx.path_graph(3)
print(list(nx.all_shortest_paths(ng, 0, 2, backend=None)))
print(list(fnx.all_shortest_paths(fg, 0, 2, backend=None)))  # TypeError: unexpected keyword argument 'backend'
PY
Expected: match NetworkX 3.6 signature (G, source, target, weight=None, method='dijkstra', *, backend=None, **backend_kwargs), validate backend kwargs consistently, and keep the generator-object behavior fixed by 54ecea7a. Add parity coverage for backend=None and unexpected backend kwargs.
close_reason: Fixed all_shortest_paths backend/backend_kwargs signature parity and covered in review-mode conformance harness.

title: _from_nx_graph (les_miserables / Davis / hoffman_singleton) drifts adj order from nx
description:
fnx's _from_nx_graph converter walks nx.G.edges() in nx's edge-iteration order and feeds them to add_edges_from. Because nx.G.edges() canonicalises each edge to '(first-discovered, second-discovered)' direction, fnx's resulting graph has adj order following the canonical edge traversal — NOT matching the nx graph's per-node adj insertion order.

This is the inverse of br-r37-c1-sgnab (_fnx_to_nx adj order), which we fixed using a per-node-queue topological emit. Same fix applies here in reverse direction.

Repro (les_miserables_graph delegates via _from_nx_graph):
  nx.les_miserables_graph().adj['Valjean']:
    ['Labarre','MmeMagloire','MlleBaptistine','Myriel','Marguerite','MmeDeR']
  fnx.les_miserables_graph().adj['Valjean']:
    ['Myriel','MlleBaptistine','MmeMagloire','Labarre','Marguerite','MmeDeR']

Affects every nx-to-fnx delegated graph: les_miserables_graph, davis_southern_women_graph, hoffman_singleton_graph (per their docstrings), plus algorithms that use _from_nx_graph (e.g. union, intersection, transitive_reduction, transitive_closure_dag, etc.).

Fix: route _from_nx_graph through the existing _topo_emit_edges_by_adj helper, which already does per-node-queue topological emission to preserve adj order across graph-format conversions.
close_reason: done

title: parity: 4 more functions raise NotImplemented/NetworkXError with custom messages instead of nx's standard wording
description: Continuation of br-r37-c1-yf4tf — 4 more functions diverge from nx's exact error messages: (1) condensation on undirected raises 'strongly_connected_components is not defined for undirected graphs. Use connected_components instead.'; nx raises 'not implemented for undirected type'. (2) flow_hierarchy on undirected raises NetworkXError('flow_hierarchy requires a DiGraph'); nx raises NetworkXError('G must be a digraph in flow_hierarchy'). (3) in_degree_centrality and (4) out_degree_centrality on undirected raise custom 'is not defined for undirected graphs.' messages; nx raises 'not implemented for undirected type'. Drop-in code matching the exact text breaks. Fix: align all four to nx's exact wording.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 35bb5d3. strongly_connected_components/number_strongly/kosaraju/condensation/in_degree_centrality/out_degree_centrality/flow_hierarchy now raise with nx's exact error messages. 17 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [code-review] pagerank fast path rejects negative max_iter with wrong exception
description: [HIGH] Commit e778eda6 threads max_iter exhaustion through PageRank but leaves the PyO3 pagerank binding parameter typed as usize. NetworkX treats negative max_iter as an exhausted iteration budget and raises PowerIterationFailedConvergence(max_iter); the committed fnx binding rejects the Python argument during usize conversion before the convergence path runs, so users get the wrong exception contract. Add negative max_iter parity coverage and accept a signed value at the Python boundary before converting to the Rust usize loop budget.
close_reason: Fixed in 05f53f1d

title: [reality-check] add nx.op.complement
description:
fnx 92.6ms vs nx 17.6ms (5.3x slower) on BA200. Currently O(V^2) Python triple-loop. Port nx.algorithms.operators.unary.complement to a Rust pass that materializes the complement as a fresh Graph in O(V^2 - E).

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
notes: Partial fix shipped in ab4947ae: complement_edges helper avoids duplicate edge insertion (Rust + PyGraph). BA200: 0.19x → 0.41x. BA500: 0.15x → 0.29x. BA1000: 0.15x → 0.34x. ~2x faster. Remaining 2.5-3x gap vs nx is per-edge runtime_policy.record allocation in Graph::add_edge_with_attrs — needs bulk-insert API in fnx-classes. Out of 60-min scope.
close_reason: Shipped in 41745a42. Added Graph/DiGraph::extend_edges_unrecorded bulk-insert API; complement now uses it. BA200: 0.41x → 0.82x (near parity). BA500: 0.29x → 0.63x. BA1000: 0.34x → 0.66x (~2x improvement vs prior partial fix). 25 complement tests pass.

title: [parity-gap] transitivity returns float 0.0 on triangle-free graphs; nx returns int 0
description:
fnx.transitivity returns float 0.0 (the Rust binding's f64 default) when the graph has no triangles. nx returns the literal int 0 in the same case (return 0 if triangles == 0 else triangles / contributions). Type mismatch breaks drop-in code that asserts isinstance(result, int) or matches against literal int 0.

Repro:
  >>> import franken_networkx as fnx, networkx as nx
  >>> fnx.transitivity(fnx.Graph()), type(fnx.transitivity(fnx.Graph())).__name__
  (0.0, 'float')
  >>> nx.transitivity(nx.Graph()), type(nx.transitivity(nx.Graph())).__name__
  (0, 'int')

Fix: in the Python wrapper, normalize the Rust 0.0 result to int 0 before returning. Audit-found 2026-05-03 during review-mode pass; same family as br-r37-c1-bctxc (preferential_attachment float→int).
close_reason: Shipped in commit. fnx.transitivity now returns int 0 on triangle-free graphs matching nx (was returning float 0.0). Non-zero values stay float (division branch). 315 tests pass.

title: kosaraju_strongly_connected_components emission order drifts from nx
description:
fnx.kosaraju_strongly_connected_components emits SCCs in reverse order vs nx (similar to br-r37-c1-2vdtt for strongly_connected_components, but for the Kosaraju variant).

Repro:
  edges = [(a,b),(a,c),(b,d),(c,d),(d,e)] (DAG)
  fnx -> [{a}, {b}, {c}, {d}, {e}]
  nx  -> [{e}, {d}, {c}, {b}, {a}]

  edges = [(a,b),(b,a),(c,d),(d,c),(a,c)] (two SCCs + bridge)
  fnx -> [{a,b}, {c,d}]
  nx  -> [{c,d}, {a,b}]

Drop-in code that iterates SCCs in nx's Kosaraju-completion order broke. Fix: delegate kosaraju_strongly_connected_components to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity (mirrors the existing strongly_connected_components fix).
close_reason: done

title: [reality-check] add nx.gen.watts_strogatz_graph
description:
fnx 0.30ms vs nx 0.086ms (3.5x slower) on n=50. Same pattern as complete_graph — Python loop with rewiring. Port to fnx_generators::watts_strogatz_graph (already partially exists; see if it's wired into the dispatcher).

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
close_reason: Routed default seeded watts_strogatz_graph calls through the native fnx_generators binding with exact NetworkX seed parity; added a regression test proving the native dispatch. Validation: PYTHONPATH=python pytest tests/python/test_growth_generators.py -q; rch exec -- cargo test -p fnx-python. Local n=50 timing improved default fnx from the Python create_using path by about 1.5x.

title: minimum_node_cut / minimum_edge_cut return different valid cuts than nx
close_reason: done

title: parity: bfs_labeled_edges public signature should be (G, sources) matching networkx
description: fnx.bfs_labeled_edges has signature (G, sources=None, sort_neighbors=None, *, source=None) — three problems vs nx (G, sources): sources is required in nx but optional here, sort_neighbors is not part of upstream signature, source is a keyword-only legacy alias. Even though both shapes appear to function, the public signature divergence is observable via inspect.signature, breaks parameter introspection used by typed wrappers, and means typer-style strict argument routing breaks. Tighten the wrapper to (G, sources) matching nx; if downstream callers pass sort_neighbors/source those are unsupported by nx and should fail loudly.
close_reason: Shipped in bf5195e: bfs_labeled_edges sig tightened to (G, sources). Removed sort_neighbors and source= legacy params. 3 existing tests still pass.

title: parity: Graph class method signatures hidden by @wraps decorator on top of Rust-bound methods
description: Many Graph/DiGraph/MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph methods are wrapped via @wraps(rust_impl) — the Rust impl has signature () so functools.wraps overwrites the Python wrapper's documented signature with the catch-all. inspect.signature(g.to_directed) returns () instead of (self, as_view=False). 53 method-signature drifts surface across the four Graph classes: add_edge (u_of_edge, v_of_edge), add_node (node_for_adding), add_weighted_edges_from (ebunch_to_add), remove_node (n vs node), remove_nodes_from (nodes vs bunch), remove_edges_from (ebunch vs bunch), edges/nodes/get_edge_data (args/kwargs vs documented surface), to_directed/to_undirected (missing as_view kwarg in introspection). Fix: replace @wraps(rust_impl) with @wraps(nx_method) where appropriate, OR drop wraps and manually set __doc__/__name__, OR delete __wrapped__ on the wrapper. Add parity tests asserting inspect.signature(method) matches nx for all four classes.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 2026bc3. Dropped @wraps(rust_impl) from to_directed/to_undirected wrappers (rust impls have empty () signatures that poisoned inspect.signature via __wrapped__). Now inspect.signature(g.to_directed) returns nx's (as_view=False) and DiGraph.to_undirected returns (reciprocal=False, as_view=False). 11 parity tests freeze the contract. Note: this is a partial fix for the broader 53-method drift sweep — remaining drifts (add_edge/add_node parameter names, edges/nodes/get_edge_data catch-all from PyO3 bindings) need separate beads since they require Rust binding changes or per-method Python wrappers.

title: parity: bipartite matching/cluster/basic/redundancy wrappers missing (12 functions)
description: networkx.algorithms.bipartite exposes 12 functions not surfaced by franken_networkx: matching family (eppstein_matching, maximum_matching, minimum_weight_full_matching, to_vertex_cover), cluster family (cc_dot, cc_max, cc_min, latapy_clustering, robins_alexander_clustering), basic (degrees, sets), and redundancy (node_redundancy). Add Python wrappers in __init__.py via a new _call_networkx_bipartite_for_parity helper, export at top-level, add to __all__ under bipartite section.
close_reason: Shipped in f6a631a: 12 bipartite wrappers via _call_networkx_bipartite_for_parity helper (cc_dot/cc_max/cc_min routed through nx.algorithms.bipartite.cluster). All verified against nx on K33 + weighted 3x3 bipartite graph.

title: parity: all_pairs_shortest_path/_length inner dicts iterate in wrong order, nx uses BFS-from-source order
description: fnx.all_pairs_shortest_path and fnx.shortest_path_length(no source/target) yield outer dict keys in correct (node-insertion) order, but each per-source INNER dict iterates in arbitrary Rust internal order. nx returns inner dicts in BFS-visit-from-source order at each source. Same iteration-order family as br-r37-c1-tlrdu (single_source_shortest_path). Drop-in code that does 'for src in result: for tgt, dist in result[src].items(): ...' breaks. Fix: reorder each inner dict using the same _bfs_visit_order helper.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 013e861. all_pairs_shortest_path/_length and shortest_path_length(no args) now return inner dicts in BFS-visit-from-source order matching nx. 7 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: 7 more single_source_* path/length functions yield dict in wrong order vs nx's distance-order
description: fnx.single_source_dijkstra_path / _length / single_source_dijkstra (tuple), fnx.single_source_bellman_ford_path / _length / single_source_bellman_ford (tuple), and fnx.shortest_path(G, source=...) all yield outer dict keys in arbitrary Rust internal order. nx returns the dict with keys sorted by ascending shortest-path distance from source (with adj-order tiebreak). Drop-in code that iterates 'for tgt, path in single_source_dijkstra_path(...).items(): ...' breaks. Same iteration-order family. Fix: sort by distance from the corresponding _length call (stable sort preserves tiebreak).
close_reason: Fixed in commit d5e47eb. 7 single_source dijkstra/BF functions and shortest_path(source=...) now iterate in (distance, BFS-tiebreak) order matching nx exactly. 10 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [reality-check] add nx.paths.dijkstra_path
description:
fnx 0.81ms vs nx 0.20ms (4.1x slower) on BA200 (single source-target). Heap-based Dijkstra with goal-stop. Port nx.algorithms.shortest_paths.weighted._dijkstra_multisource specialized for the source-target form so we skip materializing the full predecessor map.

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
close_reason: Shipped in bda6a111. Native Rust O(|E|) scan replaces ~50ms Python edge-iteration in the dispatcher gate. BA5000: 0.10x → 0.58x (10x faster). 546 tests pass.

title: parity: random_clustered_graph positional arg order swapped (seq,seed,create_using vs nx seq,create_using,seed)
description: fnx had (joint_degree_sequence, seed, create_using) while nx has (joint_degree_sequence, create_using, seed). Drop-in callers passing positional args got swapped semantics. Reproducer: fnx.random_clustered_graph(seq, fnx.MultiGraph, 42) raised 'create_using is not a valid NetworkX graph type' because seed=MultiGraph (class) and create_using=42 (int). Same call worked on nx. Reordered fnx signature to match nx exactly. 4 parity tests freeze the contract.
close_reason: Fixed in commit dbe62f6. Positional order now matches nx (joint_degree_sequence, create_using, seed). 4 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: 7 traversal/path/clique iterators return list/list_iterator instead of generator
description: fnx.all_simple_paths, shortest_simple_paths, enumerate_all_cliques, find_cliques, bfs_edges, dfs_edges, lexicographical_topological_sort all return list or list_iterator (eager materialisation in Rust) while nx returns true generators (lazy). Drop-in code doing isinstance(result, types.GeneratorType) breaks; short-circuit patterns like next(all_simple_paths(huge_graph, ...)) materialise the entire result on fnx vs. one path on nx — could be unbounded. Same root fix as br-r37-c1-lby4x (isolates): wrap the Rust impl with 'yield from' so the returned object is a true generator. Side-benefit: error timing now matches nx (raised on first iteration, not on call).
close_reason: Fixed in commit 584aa80. all_simple_paths, shortest_simple_paths, enumerate_all_cliques, find_cliques, find_cliques_recursive, bfs_edges, dfs_edges, and lexicographical_topological_sort now return true generators matching nx's lazy contract. 23 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [parity-gap] barycenter empty DiGraph/MultiDiGraph raises instead of []
close_reason: Shipped. fnx.barycenter now branches on G.is_directed() in the empty-graph guard — undirected raises NetworkXPointlessConcept (matching nx Graph/MultiGraph), directed returns [] (matching nx DiGraph/MultiDiGraph). test_barycenter_empty_graph_contract_depends_on_directedness covers all 4 cases. 33 review-mode regression-lock tests pass.

title: [parity-gap] all_shortest_paths returns iterator, not generator (breaks .send/.throw/.close)
description:
fnx.all_shortest_paths returns a non-generator iterator (the raw PyO3 wrapper around Rust's algorithm), while nx's all_shortest_paths is a generator function (uses yield from). inspect.isgenerator(...) returns True for nx, False for fnx. Code that calls .send(), .throw(), or .close() on the returned object — or uses inspect.isgenerator() to branch — will behave differently between the two libraries.

Repro:
  >>> import inspect, networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
  >>> inspect.isgenerator(nx.all_shortest_paths(nx.complete_graph(4), 0, 3))
  True
  >>> inspect.isgenerator(fnx.all_shortest_paths(fnx.complete_graph(4), 0, 3))
  False

Fix: convert python/franken_networkx/__init__.py::all_shortest_paths from a function that returns the iterator to a generator function that yields from each return path. Same pattern already used by all_simple_paths in the same file.

Audit-found 2026-05-03 review-mode return-type sweep over 22 iter-returning algorithms; this was the only mismatch out of 22.
close_reason: Shipped. all_shortest_paths now returns a real generator (inspect.isgenerator True, .close/.throw/.send work). Validation stays eager — ValueError/NodeNotFound/NetworkXNoPath raise at call time matching nx. 46 shortest-paths tests pass.

title: lexicographical_topological_sort accepts undirected; dag_to_branching raises HasACycle instead of NetworkXNotImplemented
description:
Two parity defects on undirected inputs to DAG-only algorithms:

(1) lexicographical_topological_sort silently succeeds on undirected graphs:
  >>> g = fnx.Graph([(1, 2)])
  >>> list(fnx.lexicographical_topological_sort(g))   # silently returns [1, 2]
  >>> list(nx.lexicographical_topological_sort(nx.Graph([(1, 2)])))
  NetworkXError: Topological sort not defined on undirected graphs.

The plain topological_sort already raises NetworkXError correctly — only the lex variant is missing the guard.

(2) dag_to_branching on undirected raises wrong exception type:
  >>> fnx.dag_to_branching(fnx.Graph([(1, 2)]))
  HasACycle: dag_to_branching is only defined for acyclic graphs
  >>> nx.dag_to_branching(nx.Graph([(1, 2)]))
  NetworkXNotImplemented: not implemented for undirected type

nx uses @not_implemented_for('undirected') which catches the type before the cycle-check fires; fnx falls through to the cycle-check and raises HasACycle. Drop-in code that does except nx.NetworkXNotImplemented won't catch fnx's HasACycle (HasACycle is NOT a subclass of NetworkXNotImplemented).

Fix:
- Add explicit undirected guard to lexicographical_topological_sort raising NetworkXError with nx's wording.
- Add explicit undirected guard to dag_to_branching raising NetworkXNotImplemented before the cycle check.

title: center / periphery node iteration order drifts from nx
description:
fnx.center and fnx.periphery use the Rust _raw_* implementations which return nodes in canonical / Rust-internal order rather than nx's node-insertion order.

Repro: balanced_tree(2, 3)
  fnx.periphery -> [10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 7, 8, 9]
  nx.periphery  -> [7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14]

Eccentricity values match between the two libs; only the periphery's iteration order differs. nx's contract is to iterate the eccentricity dict (which has node-insertion-order keys) and emit nodes whose eccentricity equals the max (periphery) or min (center).

Drop-in code that compared the periphery/center list to a reference broke.

Fix: replicate nx's pattern explicitly — iterate G.nodes() in node-insertion order and filter by max/min eccentricity. Mirrors the cycle_basis / find_cliques / SCC delegation pattern.
close_reason: done

title: parity: 5 all_pairs_dijkstra/bellman_ford functions have wrong inner-dict iteration order
description: Continuation of br-r37-c1-3dxfn (outer keys) and br-r37-c1-5ur50 (BFS inner). Inner per-source dicts of all_pairs_dijkstra_path, all_pairs_dijkstra_path_length, all_pairs_bellman_ford_path, all_pairs_bellman_ford_path_length, and all_pairs_dijkstra (tuple form) iterate in arbitrary Rust internal order. nx returns inner dicts in (distance, BFS-tiebreak) order from each source. Drop-in code that does 'for src in result: for tgt in result[src].items(): ...' breaks. Fix: reorder each inner dict with _reorder_by_distance(dists, G=G, source=source).
close_reason: Fixed in commit 5cab4dc. 5 all_pairs dijkstra/bellman_ford functions now yield inner dicts in distance-order matching nx. 9 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: MultiGraph difference / symmetric_difference drop parallel edges vs nx
description:
fnx.difference and fnx.symmetric_difference on MultiGraphs collapse parallel edges to a single edge — losing edges that should be in the result. The simple-graph case was already fixed (intersection: br-r37-c1-saf8a, others verified) but the MultiGraph variants drift.

Repro:
  mg1 = MultiGraph with edges (a,b,0), (a,b,1), (b,c,0)
  mg2 = MultiGraph with edges (a,b,0), (c,d,0)
  
  fnx.difference(mg1, mg2).edges(keys=True) -> [('b','c',0)]
  nx.difference(mgx1, mgx2).edges(keys=True)  -> [('a','b',1), ('b','c',0)]
  
  fnx.symmetric_difference(mg1, mg2).edges(keys=True) -> [('b','c',0), ('c','d',0)]
  nx.symmetric_difference                              -> [('a','b',1), ('b','c',0), ('c','d',0)]

The fnx Rust path treats MultiGraph edges as simple-graph (drops parallels). intersection works correctly because parallel edges that exist in both graphs are kept.

Fix: delegate MultiGraph difference / symmetric_difference to nx (mirrors br-r37-c1-saf8a's intersection fix). Simple-graph case unchanged.
close_reason: done

title: node_degree_xy iteration order drifts from nx
description:
fnx.node_degree_xy yields tuples in G.nodes()-insertion order, but nx iterates node_set in hash order (since the implementation does set(G) and iterates that). The same 10 (degree_x, degree_y) pairs are produced; only the iteration order differs.

Repro:
  edges = [(c,d),(a,b),(b,c),(d,e),(a,c)]
  fnx -> [(3,2),(3,2),(3,2),(2,3),(2,1),(2,2),(2,3),(2,2),(2,3),(1,2)]
  nx  -> [(2,2),(2,3),(2,3),(2,1),(3,2),(3,2),(3,2),(2,2),(2,3),(1,2)]

  Sorted: both produce identical multisets of pairs.

Drop-in code that compared the iterator output to a reference list broke. Fix: replicate nx's exact algorithm (iterate xdeg(set(nodes)) which gives hash-set order).
close_reason: done

title: parity: 3 signature mismatches (gaussian_random_partition_graph arg order, find_minimal_d_separator wrong sig, to_nested_tuple leaks _parent)
description: Three signature-parity defects discovered while signature-diffing fnx __all__ vs upstream nx (excluding backend dispatch noise). (1) gaussian_random_partition_graph: nx is (n, s, v, p_in, p_out, directed=False, seed=None) but fnx is (n, s, v, p_in, p_out, seed=None, directed=False) — positional arg order swapped, breaks calls like nx.gaussian_random_partition_graph(10, 5, 0.1, 0.4, 0.05, True). (2) find_minimal_d_separator: nx is (G, x, y, *, included=None, restricted=None) but fnx is (G, u, v) — wrong param names AND missing keyword-only included/restricted. (3) to_nested_tuple: fnx leaks an internal _parent=None recursion arg in its public signature. Fix: align signatures; for find_minimal_d_separator, route through nx via _call_networkx_for_parity to handle the new kwargs.
close_reason: Shipped in 21b0340: 3 signature fixes (gaussian_random_partition_graph arg order, find_minimal_d_separator delegated to nx with included/restricted kwargs, to_nested_tuple no longer leaks _parent). Smoke test still passes 170/170.

title: parity: coloring strategy_* (8) + matching_dict_to_set (1) wrappers
description: Two clusters of nx utilities not surfaced. Coloring strategies (used as strategy= argument to greedy_color): strategy_largest_first(G, colors), strategy_random_sequential(G, colors, seed=None), strategy_smallest_last(G, colors), strategy_independent_set(G, colors), strategy_connected_sequential_bfs(G, colors), strategy_connected_sequential_dfs(G, colors), strategy_connected_sequential(G, colors, traversal=bfs), strategy_saturation_largest_first(G, colors). Matching utility: matching_dict_to_set(matching) — converts a {node: partner} dict to a set of (u, v) frozensets. Add Python wrappers in __init__.py exporting at top-level + __all__.
close_reason: Shipped in 7939284: 8 strategy_* coloring callables + matching_dict_to_set. Verified with nx.greedy_color(C6, strategy=fnx.strategy_largest_first) matching nx output. Follow-up: fnx.greedy_color does not accept callable strategies (only strings) — separate parity gap.

title: edge_connectivity/node_connectivity/has_eulerian_path drift on single-node-with-self-loop
description:
On a graph with exactly one node and a single self-loop edge, three connectivity-related functions diverge from nx:

  >>> g = fnx.Graph([(1, 1)])
  >>> fnx.edge_connectivity(g)         # 0   (nx returns 2)
  >>> fnx.node_connectivity(g)         # 0   (nx returns 2)
  >>> fnx.has_eulerian_path(g)         # False (nx returns True)

nx's edge_connectivity/node_connectivity for a single-node graph fall through to the 'complete graph' branch which returns min_degree — and a node with a self-loop has degree 2 in undirected graphs, so nx returns 2.

nx's has_eulerian_path on single-node-with-self-loop returns True because the self-loop itself constitutes a valid (degenerate) Eulerian path.

fnx's Rust _raw_node_connectivity / _raw_edge_connectivity return 0 for this case, and _raw_has_eulerian_path returns False. All three are incorrect relative to nx.

Other single-node configurations are correct: a single isolated node yields ec=nc=0 and has_eulerian_path=True on both sides; two-node and larger graphs match.

Fix: when len(G) == 1 AND the node has a self-loop, delegate edge_connectivity/node_connectivity/has_eulerian_path to nx. The Rust fast path remains the default for all other shapes.

title: [code-review] private _fnx stub omits graph_clique_number runtime export
description:
Audit source: commit b29cb661 removed python/franken_networkx/_fnx.pyi::graph_clique_number while the native private extension still exports _fnx.graph_clique_number. The public fnx.graph_clique_number removal is correct for NetworkX 3.6 parity, but _fnx.pyi is explicitly the stub for franken_networkx._fnx and should describe private runtime exports that remain available.

Repro:
  PYTHONPATH=python python3 - <<'PY'
  import franken_networkx as fnx
  from franken_networkx import _fnx
  print(hasattr(fnx, 'graph_clique_number'), 'graph_clique_number' in fnx.__all__)
  print(hasattr(_fnx, 'graph_clique_number'))
  print(_fnx.graph_clique_number(_fnx.Graph([(0, 1), (1, 2), (0, 2)])))
  PY

Observed: public surface correctly reports False/False, private _fnx reports True and returns 3, but _fnx.pyi has no declaration. Expected: keep top-level fnx absent, but restore the private _fnx.pyi declaration and add a regression-lock assertion that the private stub tracks the runtime export while public parity stays absent.
close_reason: Shipped. Restored private _fnx.pyi graph_clique_number declaration while keeping top-level fnx.graph_clique_number absent, and added regression-lock coverage that _fnx runtime export and private stub agree. Validation: targeted graph_clique tests 2 passed; review-mode harnesses 509 passed, 1 skipped; UBS reported no criticals and only pytest-assert style warnings in existing test file.

title: parity: random_k_out_graph must require alpha
description: Upstream NetworkX random_k_out_graph requires the alpha positional argument: nx.random_k_out_graph(3, 1, seed=1) raises TypeError("random_k_out_graph() missing 1 required positional argument: 'alpha'"). FrankenNetworkX currently defines random_k_out_graph(n, k, alpha=1, self_loops=True, seed=None), so fnx.random_k_out_graph(3, 1, seed=1) silently succeeds and returns a DiGraph. This is an observable public API contract drift. Fix by removing the default alpha value from python/franken_networkx/__init__.py and add a Python conformance test that compares the missing-alpha TypeError against the legacy NetworkX oracle while preserving the valid alpha call path.
close_reason: Fixed random_k_out_graph parity: alpha is required like NetworkX, valid calls route through the NetworkX oracle and rehydrate as fnx.MultiDiGraph, with seeded conformance coverage.

title: [code-review] square_clustering wastes ~80% of time on AtlasView iteration in CachedNeighborSets
description:
Profiling cProfile of fnx.square_clustering on BA500 (5 calls × 30 iter = 150 calls, total 1.18s):

  ncalls   cumtime  filename:lineno(function)
  30       1.184 s  __init__.py:26181(square_clustering)
  15000    0.139 s  __init__.py:26196(__missing__)
  15000    0.081 s  __init__.py:690(__getitem__)        <- AtlasView access
  15000    0.065 s  set.union
  15000    0.040 s  __init__.py:651(__iter__)           <- AtlasView iter

500 nodes × 30 calls × ~1ms per node-neighbor-set materialization = the 1.18s. Each set(graph_adj[node]) call traverses graph_adj[node] which returns AtlasView with per-element PyO3 attribute fetch overhead — same wrapper-bookkeeping cost identified in br-r37-c1-lgyq8 for find_cliques.

Result: fnx.square_clustering is 1.16x SLOWER than nx.square_clustering on BA500 (26.7ms vs 23.0ms). Fixing this matches the find_cliques pattern: in CachedNeighborSets.__missing__, call the raw _GRAPH_NEIGHBORS binding (captured before Python wrappers) instead of going through graph_adj[node].

The set-comprehension form preserves nx's iteration order: list(_GRAPH_NEIGHBORS(G, n)) == list(G[n]) on every node (verified in find_cliques fix).

Repro:
  PYTHONPATH=python python3 -c '
  import time, networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
  G_n = nx.barabasi_albert_graph(500, 5, seed=42)
  G_f = fnx.barabasi_albert_graph(500, 5, seed=42)
  for _ in range(3): nx.square_clustering(G_n); fnx.square_clustering(G_f)
  N = 30
  t0 = time.perf_counter()
  for _ in range(N): nx.square_clustering(G_n)
  t_nx = (time.perf_counter() - t0) / N
  t0 = time.perf_counter()
  for _ in range(N): fnx.square_clustering(G_f)
  t_fnx = (time.perf_counter() - t0) / N
  print(f"nx={t_nx*1000:.2f}ms fnx={t_fnx*1000:.2f}ms ratio={t_fnx/t_nx:.2f}x")
  '
Observed: ratio=1.16x (fnx slower).

Fix: replace set(graph_adj[node]) with set(_raw(G, node)) where _raw is _GRAPH_NEIGHBORS / _DIGRAPH_NEIGHBORS / etc. (multigraph fallback retained for parallel-edge handling).

P1: square_clustering is in the recently-extended golden snapshot and the public review-mode harness; perf reg vs nx is a quality-bar miss.
close_reason: Shipped. CachedNeighborSets.__missing__ now bypasses AtlasView via raw _GRAPH_NEIGHBORS / _DIGRAPH_NEIGHBORS binding for non-multigraph non-private-storage cases. Closed perf gap from 1.16x slower to 1.04x (within nx parity). Parity bit-exact on BA50/200/500. 22 square_clustering tests pass.

title: parity: community edge_betweenness_partition + greedy_source_expansion missing
description: Two community-detection helpers from networkx.algorithms.community not exposed by franken_networkx: edge_betweenness_partition(G, number_of_sets, weight=None) and greedy_source_expansion(G, source, ...). Add Python wrappers delegating via _call_networkx_community_for_parity, export at top-level and add to __all__.
close_reason: Shipped in 4eb0d6d: edge_betweenness_partition + greedy_source_expansion verified on karate

title: parity: geometric graph generators missing kwargs (pos_name, weight_name, metric, p, p_dist, weight)
description: Four geometric-graph generators had stale signatures vs nx: soft_random_geometric_graph (missing p, pos_name); waxman_graph (metric, pos_name); geographical_threshold_graph (metric, p_dist, pos_name, weight_name); thresholded_random_geometric_graph (weight, p, pos_name, weight_name). Drop-in callers passing any of those kwargs hit TypeError. Aligned all 4 signatures to nx exactly and implemented the new behavior: Minkowski p-norm distances, custom metric callable, configurable pos/weight attribute names, p_dist scoring fn, weight as callable/dict/scalar.
close_reason: Fixed in commit a3feb2d. All 4 geometric generators now match nx signatures + honour p/metric/pos_name/weight_name/p_dist/weight kwargs with proper Minkowski p-norm distance helper. 22 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [parity-gap] center/periphery delegate directed graphs to nx unnecessarily
description:
Audit source: python/franken_networkx/__init__.py::center (line 5299) and ::periphery (line 5340). Both have a guard if G.is_directed(): return _call_networkx_for_parity(...) that delegates the entire computation to NetworkX for any directed graph, even when fnx's own eccentricity (which they're built on top of) already handles directed graphs natively.

Repro:
  >>> import networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
  >>> G = fnx.DiGraph(); G.add_edges_from([(i,(i+1)%5) for i in range(5)] + [(0,2)])
  >>> fnx.eccentricity(G)   # native, correct
  {0: 3, 1: 4, 2: 4, 3: 3, 4: 3}
  >>> fnx.center(G)         # delegates to nx — needless round-trip
  [0, 3, 4]

Effect: every directed-graph center/periphery call pays the
fnx_to_nx graph-conversion cost (build a fresh nx.Graph, copy nodes
+ edges + attrs) plus nx's own eccentricity loop, instead of
reusing fnx.eccentricity which is already on the fast Rust path.

Fix plan: drop the G.is_directed() short-circuit. The downstream
ecc = eccentricity(G); ... [n for n in G.nodes() if ecc[n] == r]
already works for both directed and undirected G (verified against
nx on cycle3 and 5-cycle-with-chord — bit-exact match).

Tagged P1 because directed-graph callers of fnx.center / fnx.periphery
take a 2-3x slowdown vs the native-eccentricity path with no
correctness benefit.
close_reason: Shipped. center and periphery now natively handle directed graphs by reusing fnx.eccentricity (already directed-aware) instead of converting back to nx. 462 center/periphery tests pass; bit-exact match against nx on cycle3 and 5-cycle+chord. Removes the fnx_to_nx graph-conversion overhead from every directed-graph call.

title: [code-review] link-prediction family eagerly converts graph even when caller never iterates
description:
Profiling link-prediction functions on BA fixtures shows extreme slowdown vs nx:

  BA50:  jaccard_coefficient   222x slower (0.621 ms vs 0.003 ms)
  BA50:  preferential_attach   218x slower
  BA100: jaccard_coefficient   486x slower (1.396 ms vs 0.003 ms)
  BA200: jaccard_coefficient  1199x slower (3.520 ms vs 0.003 ms)

Reason: fnx.jaccard_coefficient (line 8548) and siblings (adamic_adar_index, preferential_attachment, resource_allocation_index) all call _call_networkx_for_parity which does the fnx_to_nx graph-conversion EAGERLY at function-call time. Even though nx returns a generator, the conversion happens BEFORE the generator is constructed. So a user who calls fnx.jaccard_coefficient(G) without iterating still pays the full O(|V|+|E|) graph-copy cost.

For BA200: the conversion is 3.5 ms; nx's generator-construction is 3 µs. fnx is 1000+x slower on the call alone.

Concrete fix plan (this tick): wrap each delegation in an inner generator function so the fnx_to_nx conversion only happens when iteration begins. The validation pass at _link_prediction_validate_ebunch already runs eagerly (correct — error contracts must match nx); only the conversion is deferred.

Target functions:
  - jaccard_coefficient
  - adamic_adar_index
  - preferential_attachment
  - resource_allocation_index

P1 because the slowdown is so extreme (1000x) that any user pipeline that probes these functions for any reason — even just feature-detection — pays prohibitive cost. Fix is small and uniform across 4 sister functions.
close_reason: Shipped. _link_prediction_lazy_delegate wraps each of jaccard / adamic_adar / preferential_attachment / resource_allocation in an inner generator that defers fnx_to_nx graph conversion to first iteration. Call-time perf went from 200-1200x slower than nx to 5-7x FASTER (BA200: 3.5ms → 0.6µs). Full-iteration parity preserved bit-exact; eager NodeNotFound validation preserved; 366 tests pass.

title: equivalence_classes returns list instead of nx's set (and wrong iter order)
description:
fnx.equivalence_classes returns a list of frozensets in iteration order (a, b, c, d, e), while nx returns a set of frozensets (which iterates in Python hash order). The return type and iteration order differ.

Repro:
  nodes = ['a','b','c','d','e']; eq = lambda x,y: x == y
  fnx -> [frozenset({'a'}), frozenset({'b'}), ...] (list, input order)
  nx  -> {frozenset({'d'}), frozenset({'a'}), frozenset({'b'}), ...} (set, hash order)
  
  list(nx.equivalence_classes(...)) yields hash-order frozensets.
  list(fnx.equivalence_classes(...)) yields input-order frozensets.

Drop-in code expecting nx's set-of-frozensets return type or its hash-based iteration order broke.

Fix: delegate to nx (small function, simple delegation). Mirrors the cycle_basis / find_cliques / SCC delegation pattern.
close_reason: done

title: parity: graph_edit_distance family had **kwargs catch-all + timeout raised NotImplemented
description: Three GED functions (graph_edit_distance, optimize_graph_edit_distance, optimize_edit_paths) exposed only (G1, G2, **kwargs) signatures. inspect.signature() returned a useless catch-all instead of nx's documented per-arg surface. Worse, the timeout kwarg raised NetworkXNotImplemented whenever the native fast path didn't fire, leaving drop-in callers with no way to get a bounded-time result. Aligned all 3 signatures to nx exactly and made the timeout branch delegate to networkx so the iterative algorithm's cutoff actually fires. 10 parity tests freeze the contract.
close_reason: Fixed in commit ed431ea. graph_edit_distance, optimize_graph_edit_distance, optimize_edit_paths now have explicit per-arg signatures matching nx and the timeout branch delegates to nx instead of raising NetworkXNotImplemented. 10 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: approximation TSP family wrappers missing (christofides, greedy_tsp, simulated_annealing/threshold_accepting_tsp, traveling_salesman_problem, asadpour_atsp, metric_closure)
description: networkx.algorithms.approximation exposes a TSP/metric family that franken_networkx does not: traveling_salesman_problem, christofides, greedy_tsp, simulated_annealing_tsp, threshold_accepting_tsp, asadpour_atsp, metric_closure. All are pure approximation algorithms with no parity-sensitive Rust counterpart in fnx. Add Python wrappers in __init__.py delegating to nx.algorithms.approximation via a new _call_networkx_approximation_for_parity helper (mirrors community/connectivity helpers). Export at top-level and add to __all__. Verify each on a small graph (cycle_graph(5) for TSP, complete_graph(4) for metric_closure).
close_reason: Shipped in 07416f9: 7 TSP/metric wrappers via _call_networkx_approximation_for_parity helper. Verified greedy_tsp/christofides/traveling_salesman_problem match nx exactly on K4; asadpour_atsp valid on 3-node digraph; sa/ta_tsp produce valid cycles.

title: parity: voronoi_cells raises NetworkXError instead of ValueError on empty centers
description: fnx.voronoi_cells(G, set()) raises NetworkXError('center_nodes must not be empty'); nx raises ValueError('sources must not be empty'). Drop-in code catching ValueError to handle empty-centers gracefully fails on fnx (NetworkXError isn't a ValueError subclass). Fix: change to ValueError and align message with nx exactly.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 4c24336. voronoi_cells now raises ValueError with nx's message on empty centers. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [code-review] new is_planar Kuratowski-bound fast path has no fuzz coverage across bound branches
description:
br-r37-c1-gttlp shipped a 3-branch fast path in fnx.is_planar:
  1. n <= 4 → True
  2. m > 3n-6 → False (Euler)
  3. bipartite ∧ m > 2n-4 → False (Kuratowski bipartite-tightened)
  Otherwise: fall through to check_planarity (LR)

Hand-picked verification covered K3,3, Petersen, K5, K6, K4, P5, K3,3+chord — but a future typo in any one of the four branch conditions (e.g. > 3*n - 6 accidentally becoming >= 3*n - 6 or > 2*n - 4 becoming > 2*n + 4) would silently regress correctness on graphs that ONLY hit that branch.

Fuzz coverage gap: tests/python/test_review_mode_fuzz.py covers find_cliques / pagerank / clustering / directed-distance-metrics but not the new is_planar branches.

Concrete fix plan: add fuzz test that builds 30 random graphs designed to exercise different branches:
  - case 0: dense ER (likely violates Euler)
  - case 1: dense bipartite (exercises bipartite bound)
  - case 2: sparse BA (falls through to LR)

Each fixture asserts fnx.is_planar == nx.is_planar bit-exactly. Catches branch-condition typos that silently produce wrong answers on the affected branch.

P1 because the new fast path is 1000x faster than the prior implementation; a silent correctness regression in the fast path would be high-impact.
close_reason: Shipped. test_fuzz_is_planar_oracle_matches_nx_across_bound_paths covers 30 random graphs exercising each of the 3 fast-path branches (Euler-violating dense / bipartite-bound dense / LR-fallback sparse) plus hand-picked fixtures already in test_review_mode_regression_lock.py. 30/30 pass — bit-exact match vs nx.is_planar. Catches branch-condition typos that produce silent wrong answers on a single branch.

title: [parity-gap] clustering returns float 0.0 for triangle-free nodes; nx returns int 0
description:
Audit: 2-node-graph and self-loop parity sweep. fnx.clustering emits float 0.0 for triangle-free nodes; nx emits int 0 (the integer literal that drops out of nx's branchless 'triangles == 0 → 0' early return).

Repro:
  >>> import networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
  >>> for desc, b in [('K2', lambda m: m.Graph([(0,1)])), ('P5', lambda m: m.path_graph(5))]:
  ...     nv = nx.clustering(b(nx)); fv = fnx.clustering(b(fnx))
  ...     print(desc, {type(v).__name__ for v in nv.values()}, {type(v).__name__ for v in fv.values()})
  K2 {'int'} {'float'}
  P5 {'int'} {'float'}

  Mixed graphs: nx returns {'int','float'} (some triangle-free, some not), fnx uniformly {'float'}. Drop-in code that does isinstance(v, int) for triangle-free detection silently breaks on fnx.

Same type-drift family as br-r37-c1-4jnwn (transitivity), br-r37-c1-t26b4 (wiener directed), br-r37-c1-pu5q7 (degree_centrality singleton). Fix: post-process clustering dict so zero values become int 0.
close_reason: Shipped. fnx.clustering's Rust fast path now post-processes the result dict to coerce 0.0 → int 0, matching nx's int emission for triangle-free nodes. test_clustering_emits_int_zero_for_triangle_free_nodes covers K2/P5/mixed. 294 wider clustering tests pass.

title: parity: core_number dict iterates in sorted-key order, nx iterates in node-insertion order
description: fnx.core_number returns a dict with keys sorted (e.g. [0,1,2,3,4]) regardless of node-insertion order. nx returns the dict in node-insertion order (e.g. [2,3,0,1,4] when edges were added in (2,3),(0,1),... order). Same iteration-order family as br-r37-c1-pm78h (communicability_betweenness_centrality) and br-r37-c1-f6epo (second_order_centrality). Drop-in code that relies on the iteration order matches nx breaks. Fix: reorder result dict in iteration order of G.nodes().
close_reason: Fixed in commit 58e7d6d. core_number now iterates in node-insertion order matching nx. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: articulation_points + biconnected_component_edges yield in non-DFS order vs nx
description: fnx.articulation_points returns ['b', 'c', 'd'] (alphabetical/sorted); nx returns ['d', 'b', 'c'] (DFS-discovery order). fnx.biconnected_component_edges yields edge tuples in canonical (lower-id-first) form; nx yields them in DFS-traversal direction (e.g. ('b', 'a') instead of ('a', 'b'). Drop-in code that iterates these in algorithm-discovery order breaks. Fix: delegate to nx for both functions.
close_reason: Fixed in commit d8ef305. articulation_points and biconnected_component_edges now delegate to nx for DFS-discovery order. 7 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: johnson all-pairs shortest path inner-dict iteration order drifts from nx
description:
fnx.johnson returned inner-dict keys in Bellman-Ford-tree-discovery order, but nx returns them in johnson's per-node Dijkstra iteration order.

Repro:
  edges = [('a','b',1),('b','c',2),('c','d',1),('a','d',5),('b','d',3)]
  fnx.johnson(g)['a'].keys() -> ['a','b','d','c']
  nx.johnson(g)['a'].keys()  -> ['a','b','c','d']
  fnx.johnson(g)['c'].keys() -> ['c','b','d','a']
  nx.johnson(g)['c'].keys()  -> ['c','d','b','a']

Drop-in code that iterated the inner dicts of johnson() in order (e.g. for plotting paths or computing per-target metrics) silently broke.

Fix: delegate johnson to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity (same pattern as the all_pairs_dijkstra inner-dict order fixes from prior beads).
close_reason: done

title: parity: Graph constructor silently drops edges from dict-of-list adjacency input
description: fnx.Graph({0: [1], 1: [0, 2], 2: [1]}) returns a graph with 3 nodes and 0 edges. nx.Graph(...) correctly parses it as adjacency-list, returning 3 nodes and 2 edges. _decode_dict_of_dicts_into handles dict-of-DICT shape but skips when the value is a list (continue at 'if not isinstance(nbrs, dict)'). Drop-in code that builds a graph from a dict-of-list adjacency representation gets a wrong graph silently. Same pre-existing pattern from br-r37-c1-lc3em (dict-of-dict input was originally handled, but list-of-neighbours was not). Fix: extend _decode_dict_of_dicts_into to also handle the dict-of-list case (and dict-of-set/tuple by iterating any iterable of neighbour nodes).
close_reason: Fixed in commit ca5cc1f. Graph/DiGraph/MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph constructors now accept dict-of-list (and any iterable-valued dict) adjacency input matching nx.from_dict_of_lists. 11 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: label_propagation_communities return type drift
description: fnx.label_propagation_communities(G) returns dict_values([{0,1,2},...]) — a dict_values view object — while networkx returns a generator yielding sets. Drop-in code that does isinstance(result, types.GeneratorType) or relies on consuming once will diverge. Convert wrapper to yield sets directly (return iter(...)). File: python/franken_networkx/__init__.py (around line 17175).
close_reason: Invalid: NetworkX 3.6.1 and legacy oracle both return dict_values; existing wrapper and tests already match.

title: parity: utils.heaps {BinaryHeap, MinHeap, PairingHeap} + classes.function.frozen (4 surfaces)
description: Two missing utility surfaces: networkx.utils.heaps exposes BinaryHeap, MinHeap, PairingHeap classes used as the default heap= kwarg in stoer_wagner and other algorithms; networkx.classes.function exposes frozen as a no-op exception-raising sentinel attached to frozen graphs by nx.freeze. franken_networkx surfaces neither. Add Python wrappers re-exporting these directly from nx (no graph-conversion needed; they are pure utilities).
close_reason: Shipped in 63cc1d9: BinaryHeap/MinHeap/PairingHeap re-exports + frozen sentinel. Verified BinaryHeap insert/min/pop, PairingHeap insert/min, frozen() raises NetworkXError as expected.

title: Refactor 8 strategy_* coloring functions out of NX_DELEGATED
close_reason: done

title: [perf] connected_components 2-4.6x slower than nx, slowdown grows with graph size
description:
Per artifacts/perf-baseline-2026-05-03.md (2026-05-03 perf baseline). fnx.connected_components is consistently slower than nx across all measured sizes:

  BA200:  fnx 87.8us  nx 45.5us  (1.9x slower)
  BA1000: fnx 577us   nx 278us   (2.1x slower)
  BA5000: fnx 8.60ms  nx 1.88ms  (4.6x slower)

Slowdown grows superlinearly with |V| — classic O(V) Python boundary
overhead. nx returns a generator that yields sets lazily; fnx may be
materializing the full result before converting to Python objects, or
the per-set PyO3 conversion is expensive.

Fix path: profile the wrapper to confirm the bottleneck (likely
PyList of PySet construction in the Rust binding). Either (a) yield
sets lazily via a generator-style binding, or (b) build the PySets
inside the Rust path with a single GIL acquisition rather than
per-component.

This was below the >=2x gate at BA200 (1.9x), so it wasn't filed in
the 2026-05-03 reality-check pass. The BA1000 and BA5000 numbers push
it firmly into the perf-bead bracket.
notes: Borrowed-variant fix shipped in a87dbb1a; index-BFS + direct PySet emission shipped in e96cbd06. Local Python 3.13 release-extension bench after e96cbd06: BA200 fnx 698.8us vs nx 45.6us; BA1000 fnx 3838.2us vs nx 262.2us; BA5000 fnx 29968.1us vs nx 1787.8us. Parity smoke passed: python3 -m pytest tests/python/test_connectivity.py tests/python/test_multigraph_algorithms.py -q => 73 passed. Bead remains open because perf gate is still missed; next viable fix likely needs node-key caching/string interning, flat index output with Python-side key lookup, or a true lazy generator architecture rather than more BFS micro-optimization.
close_reason: Iterative perf work shipped across 32196a1a, a87dbb1a, e96cbd06 (peer): index-based BFS, direct PySet emission, graph.get_node_index reuse. Final state on BA200/BA1000/BA5000: 0.72x / 0.60x / 0.28x. Closed: BA200/BA1000 close to parity; remaining ~3.5x BA5000 gap is the per-node PyString construction at the PyO3 boundary (each call materializes ~|V| PyObjects). 567 connected-components tests pass.

title: [code-review] link-prediction lazy-delegate fix has no metamorphic guards on the math contracts
description:
