br-r37-c1-8e60l shipped a 1000x perf fix for the link-prediction family (jaccard, adamic_adar, preferential_attachment, resource_allocation_index) by deferring graph conversion. Functional tests verify nx differential parity, but no metamorphic guards lock the underlying mathematical contracts — so a future regression where both libraries drift together (e.g., a generator-protocol change that affects both) could pass diff tests while violating the math.

Concrete relations to add (independent of nx):

  jaccard_coefficient:
    1. Symmetric: jaccard(u, v) == jaccard(v, u). Definitional —
       set similarity uses unordered set intersection/union.
    2. In [0, 1]: normalized set ratio is bounded.

  preferential_attachment:
    3. pa(u, v) == deg(u) * deg(v). Holds by definition; fnx
       delegates to nx, so this verifies the delegation
       round-trip end-to-end.

P1 because the lazy-delegate fix is fresh (last 30 min) and lacks math-rooted regression coverage — a future generator-protocol change that broke iteration semantics could pass differential tests while violating jaccard's symmetry or bounds.
close_reason: Shipped. 3 metamorphic-property tests for link-prediction (jaccard symmetry, jaccard bounds, preferential_attachment definitional check). 12/12 pass — math contracts locked independent of nx. Complements br-r37-c1-8e60l's lazy-delegate functional tests.

title: [code-review] _fnx.transitivity directed-collapse: returns 1.0 on cycle3 instead of nx's 0
description:
Audit: extending the directed-collapse defect family. The Rust _fnx.transitivity binding has the same gr.undirected() collapse pattern that I've been finding across diameter/radius/center/periphery/barycenter.

Repro:
  >>> from franken_networkx import _fnx
  >>> G = _fnx.DiGraph(); G.add_edges_from([(0,1),(1,2),(2,0)])
  >>> _fnx.transitivity(G)
  1.0    # WRONG — undirected K3 projection has 1 triangle / 1 triplet
  >>> import networkx as nx
  >>> nx.transitivity(nx.DiGraph([(0,1),(1,2),(2,0)]))
  0      # nx counts directed triangles (u→v→w→u patterns); none on cycle3

The Python wrapper fnx.transitivity (line 26142+) already routes directed inputs through the Python triangle-iter path, so users of the public API see correct results. But the raw Rust binding _fnx.transitivity is still broken.

Affects: backend.py dispatch for transitivity (if directed), direct _fnx.transitivity callers.

Fix plan: same shape as br-r37-c1-89n9d / wojl3 / ecqmz — the wrapper already handles correctness; this bead documents that the underlying Rust function still needs a real fix (same scope as the broader directed-collapse cleanup).

This tick: add a Python regression-lock test that asserts fnx.transitivity (the wrapper) matches nx exactly on directed cycle3 / cycle5+chord / K4. The wrapper is correct today; the test prevents a future refactor that re-routes through _fnx from silently regressing.

P1 because the same family (raw Rust on directed) has produced 4 prior shipped bugs; locking the wrapper-level contract is the minimum guard.
close_reason: Shipped wrapper-level lock test. fnx.transitivity public-API contract on directed graphs (nx-bit-exact match) now locked across cycle3/cycle5+chord/directed K4. Underlying _fnx.transitivity gr.undirected() collapse defect remains for separate Rust-side fix in the broader directed-collapse cleanup family.

title: resistance_distance silently accepts directed input; nx raises NetworkXNotImplemented
description:
fnx.resistance_distance silently computes a value on DiGraph and MultiDiGraph inputs. nx is decorated with @not_implemented_for('directed') and raises NetworkXNotImplemented.

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.DiGraph([(1,2),(2,3)])
  >>> fnx.resistance_distance(g)
  {1: {1: 0.0, 2: 0.33..., 3: 0.66...}, 2: {...}, 3: {...}}
  >>> nx.resistance_distance(nx.DiGraph([(1,2),(2,3)]))
  NetworkXNotImplemented: not implemented for directed type

The mathematical concept of resistance distance is defined for undirected graphs (electrical networks); applying it to a digraph by treating each directed edge as a resistor produces a numerically-meaningless asymmetric matrix.

This was the only function found in an audit of all 106 nx functions decorated with @not_implemented_for('directed') that had a fnx parity gap.

Fix: add if G.is_directed(): raise NetworkXNotImplemented('not implemented for directed type') at the top of fnx.resistance_distance.

title: [code-review] filter private names from franken_networkx.community.__all__
description:
Audit source: suspicious commit a7905970. New python/franken_networkx/community.py builds __all__ from set(globals()) | set(dir(networkx.algorithms.community)), which exports private/module internals such as _fnx and __builtins__. Repro: PYTHONPATH=python python3 - <<'PY'
import franken_networkx as fnx
print(sorted(x for x in fnx.community.__all__ if x.startswith('_'))[:12])
# currently includes __builtins__, __cached__, __doc__, __file__, __loader__, __name__, __package__, __path__, __spec__, _fnx
PY
Expected: __all__ should mirror upstream public community exports plus intentional fnx overrides, not private names or imported implementation modules. Fix: derive from upstream __all__/public names and add only label_propagation_communities.
close_reason: Filtered franken_networkx.community.__all__ to upstream public names plus fnx override; private implementation names no longer export.

title: parity: preferential_attachment returns float counts and reversed pair order vs nx
description: fnx.preferential_attachment yields (u, v, count) triples with: (1) count as float (2.0) instead of nx's int (2) — preferential attachment is just a degree product so always integer-valued. (2) some pairs in reversed order: fnx returns ('d', 'a', 2.0) where nx returns ('a', 'd', 2). Drop-in code that asserts isinstance(p, int) or that (a, d, 2) in result fails. Fix: delegate to nx for both type and pair-order parity (the underlying degree-product computation is trivial; Rust impl gives no real perf benefit).
close_reason: Fixed in commit c52f0e3. preferential_attachment delegates to nx for int scores and matching pair order. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: has_eulerian_path raises NotImplemented on directed graphs; nx accepts directed
description: fnx.has_eulerian_path(DiGraph) raises NetworkXNotImplemented('not implemented for directed type'). nx supports both directed and undirected — a directed graph has an Eulerian path iff at most one vertex has (out_degree - in_degree) = 1, at most one has -1, all others 0, AND the underlying undirected graph is connected. fnx.is_eulerian on directed graphs already works correctly; only has_eulerian_path is over-restricted. Drop-in code that calls has_eulerian_path(digraph) breaks. Fix: delegate to nx for directed inputs (or add native directed support).
close_reason: Fixed in commit 8fe2bcb. has_eulerian_path now accepts directed graphs (delegating to nx for the directed case), matching nx's full contract. 10 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [reality-check] add nx.cycle.cycle_basis
description:
fnx 6.46ms vs nx 0.59ms (11x slower) on BA200. nx uses a single spanning-tree DFS to extract a fundamental cycle basis. Port nx.algorithms.cycles.cycle_basis.

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
close_reason: Ported public cycle_basis back to native Rust with NetworkX DFS ordering parity and regression coverage.

title: parity: random_uniform_k_out_graph + reverse_cuthill_mckee_ordering + flow_matrix_row missing (3 utilities)
description: Three nx utilities not surfaced by franken_networkx: random_uniform_k_out_graph(n, k, self_loops=True, with_replacement=True, seed=None) from generators.directed (k-out random digraph), reverse_cuthill_mckee_ordering(G, heuristic=None) from utils (bandwidth reduction node ordering), and flow_matrix_row(G, weight=None, dtype=float, solver=lu) from algorithms.centrality.flow_matrix (current-flow centrality kernel). Add Python wrappers via _call_networkx_submodule_for_parity, export at top-level, add to __all__.
close_reason: Shipped in e3f2e78: random_uniform_k_out_graph + reverse_cuthill_mckee_ordering + flow_matrix_row wrappers. Verified n=8 k=2 seed=42 nodes/edges match, P8 RCM ordering match, K4 flow_matrix_row count match nx.

title: draw_bipartite should accept top_nodes as positional arg matching test expectations
description:
fnx.draw_bipartite has signature (G, **kwargs) where top_nodes must be passed as kwarg. But three tests in test_drawing_extras.py call it positionally as draw_bipartite(graph, top_nodes, ...).

Pre-existing test failures:
- test_draw_bipartite_uses_bipartite_layout_when_pos_missing
- test_draw_bipartite_transformed_graph_uses_local_layout
- test_draw_bipartite_transformed_graph_respects_explicit_pos

The tests reflect the project's intent: fnx's draw_bipartite should accept top_nodes as the second positional argument (a friendly extension of nx's API since nx itself doesn't take top_nodes). Without it, the user has to compute bipartite_sets explicitly which the test explicitly avoids.

Fix: extend signature to (G, top_nodes=None, **kwargs).
close_reason: done

title: [parity-gap] barycenter must preserve NetworkX backend keyword signature
description:
Audit source: suspicious commit 23c80209. barycenter's new directed-reachability guard is useful, but the public wrapper still omits NetworkX 3.6's keyword-only backend/backend_kwargs signature. Repro: PYTHONPATH=python python3 - <<'PY'
import networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
ng = nx.path_graph(3); fg = fnx.path_graph(3)
print(nx.barycenter(ng, backend=None))
print(fnx.barycenter(fg, backend=None))  # TypeError: unexpected keyword argument 'backend'
PY
Expected: match NetworkX 3.6 signature (G, weight=None, attr=None, sp=None, *, backend=None, **backend_kwargs), validate backend kwargs consistently, and keep the non-strongly-connected DiGraph NetworkXNoPath behavior. Add a differential signature/call test.
close_reason: Fixed barycenter backend/backend_kwargs signature parity and covered in review-mode conformance harness.

title: [reality-check] add nx.paths.astar_path
description:
fnx 0.75ms vs nx 0.18ms (4.2x slower) on BA200. A* with default heuristic = 0 (== Dijkstra). Port nx.algorithms.shortest_paths.astar.astar_path to Rust; reuse the dijkstra_path infrastructure with a Python heuristic callback (only invoke the callback at expansion time).

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
close_reason: Shipped in 5b4b0294. Replaced overly-defensive _graph_has_nonunit_weight Python scan with the native negative-weight check (reusing helper from br-r37-c1-644fx). BA200: 0.24x → 0.64x. BA1000: 0.22x → 0.54x. BA5000: 0.10x → 0.14x (BA5000 still behind nx; remaining gap is HashMap<String,_> + String::clone in Rust astar, out of scope). 56 astar tests pass; negative-weight parity preserved.

title: 7 more path/traversal fns raise wrong exception on unhashable source
description:
Continuation of br-r37-c1-i9whv. Seven more path-finding/traversal functions raise the wrong exception class when given an unhashable source/target node:

  dijkstra_path             fnx: NodeNotFound  nx: TypeError
  bellman_ford_path         fnx: NodeNotFound  nx: TypeError
  node_connected_component  fnx: NodeNotFound  nx: TypeError
  bfs_edges                 fnx: NetworkXError nx: TypeError
  dfs_edges                 fnx: NetworkXError nx: TypeError
  dfs_tree                  fnx: NetworkXError nx: TypeError
  descendants_at_distance   fnx: NodeNotFound  nx: NetworkXError

Drop-in code that does pytest.raises(TypeError) on these wouldn't trigger. The pattern is the same as the previous beads — fnx falls through to a 'node not in graph' check that silently returns False on unhashable, then raises NodeNotFound or NetworkXError instead of the underlying TypeError.

For descendants_at_distance, nx itself raises NetworkXError (not TypeError, oddly — it pre-validates the source node before dict access). fnx raises NodeNotFound. Match nx's exact class.

Fix: hash-validate at entry of each affected wrapper.

title: [code-review] load_centrality should not swallow native exceptions
description:
Audit source: suspicious commit 32ba3622. python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:17186-17195 wraps _raw_load_centrality in try/except Exception and silently falls back to the local Python path. This hides real native panics/logic errors and can make tests pass while the Rust fast path is broken. Repro: PYTHONPATH=python python3 - <<'PY'
import franken_networkx as fnx
orig = fnx._raw_load_centrality
def boom(*args, **kwargs):
    raise RuntimeError('native boom')
fnx._raw_load_centrality = boom
try:
    print(type(fnx.load_centrality(fnx.path_graph(3))).__name__)  # dict today; RuntimeError should surface
finally:
    fnx._raw_load_centrality = orig
PY
Expected: catch only known unsupported-mode exceptions, or remove the blanket fallback so native fast-path failures are visible. Add a no-fallback regression test that patches _raw_load_centrality and asserts unexpected exceptions propagate.
close_reason: Removed blanket _raw_load_centrality exception swallow; unexpected native errors now propagate and are covered in review-mode conformance harness.

title: parity: 34 top-level functions accept positional args that nx made keyword-only
description: fnx's signatures use POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD for params nx declares as KEYWORD_ONLY (after *). Affected: dfs_edges/dfs_tree/dfs_predecessors/dfs_successors/dfs_postorder_nodes/dfs_preorder_nodes (sort_neighbors), random_spanning_tree/random_labeled_tree/random_unlabeled_tree/random_labeled_rooted_tree/random_unlabeled_rooted_tree/random_unlabeled_rooted_forest (seed, multiplicative, number_of_*), connected_watts_strogatz_graph/dense_gnm_random_graph/duplication_divergence_graph/gnm_random_graph/random_lobster_graph/random_powerlaw_tree/generalized_petersen_graph (create_using), directed_edge_swap (nswap, max_tries, seed), from_numpy_array (nodelist), harmonic_diameter (weight), identified_nodes (store_contraction_as), number_of_spanning_trees (weight, root), subgraph_view (filter_node, filter_edge). Forward-compat issue: code using positional form on fnx breaks when ported to nx. Fix: insert * before the documented keyword-only params in each wrapper.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 863a001. 25 functions now match nx's POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD vs KEYWORD_ONLY param kinds. Code using positional form for these params now correctly raises TypeError on both fnx and nx, preventing forward-compat foot-guns. number_of_spanning_trees got a Python wrapper around the Rust binding. 31 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: VF2 GraphMatcher/DiGraphMatcher/MultiGraphMatcher classes missing
description: networkx.algorithms.isomorphism exposes GraphMatcher, DiGraphMatcher, MultiGraphMatcher, MultiDiGraphMatcher classes for the classical VF2 algorithm with rich API: subgraph_is_isomorphic(), subgraph_isomorphisms_iter(), match(), candidate_pairs_iter(), semantic_feasibility(), syntactic_feasibility(), initialize(). fnx exposes only the top-level functions (is_isomorphic, vf2pp_*) but no Matcher classes. Add wrapper classes that delegate to nx.algorithms.isomorphism via _networkx_graph_for_parity, exporting as fnx.GraphMatcher etc.
close_reason: Completed in 742c813

title: [modes-of-reasoning] algorithmic-correctness
description: Mode report: Algorithmic-correctness / edge-case lens. Finding 1: public wrappers can hide native-kernel correctness gaps. Repro verified: fnx.is_planar(fnx.complete_bipartite_graph(3,3)) returns False while fnx._raw_is_planar(...) returns True. Evidence: python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:9487-9506 routes public is_planar through check_planarity because raw Rust classified K3,3/Petersen as planar; crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs:18492-18515 documents the raw kernel as necessary-only. Finding 2: weighted shortest-path correctness still depends on wrapper fallback guards; python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:3427-3447 detects non-unit edge weights so wrappers avoid raw hop-count behavior. Action: build a raw-vs-public algorithm consistency audit for wrapped _raw_* kernels, starting with planarity and weighted shortest paths; each mismatch should be classified as fixed-native, intentionally-wrapper-hidden, or unsafe-to-expose with a regression test.
close_reason: Completed raw-vs-public algorithm consistency audit coverage for planarity and weighted Dijkstra wrappers.

title: [reality-check] add nx.connectivity.edge_connectivity
description: Audited 2026-05-03. fnx.edge_connectivity on barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5) takes 32_374ms vs nx 23.7ms (1370x slower). Pathological slowdown — likely O(|V|x|E|) per-pair ST-cut instead of White-Whitney + Edmonds-Karp. Port nx.algorithms.connectivity.edge_connectivity. See artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.
close_reason: Completed: replaced undirected global edge_connectivity all-pairs sweep with NetworkX-style dominating-set flow schedule; added min-degree no-pair-sweep guard; validated fnx-algorithms and fnx-python via rch.

title: [code-review] no public-API conformance lock for the directed-distance-metric wrapper safety net
description:
br-r37-c1-0xhhq added require_undirected guards to 5 _fnx.* Rust functions (diameter, radius, center, periphery, barycenter). These guards mean direct callers of _fnx.* on directed inputs now raise NetworkXNotImplemented; the public Python wrappers fnx.* continue to work on directed inputs via native paths (fnx.eccentricity / fnx.shortest_path_length).

But there's no Python-level conformance test that LOCKS the wrapper-level safety net. A future refactor that 'simplifies' fnx.diameter back to return _fnx.diameter(G) (forgetting the directed routing) would silently break every directed-graph diameter call: with the new Rust guard it'd raise NetworkXNotImplemented — visibly breaking — but a future contributor could 'fix' the breakage by removing the require_undirected guard, re-introducing the silent-wrong-answer behaviour.

The test test_public_fnx_wrappers_handle_directed_via_native_paths parametrized over diameter / radius / center / periphery / barycenter on a directed cycle3 (vertex-transitive, so simple expected values: diameter=2, radius=2, center=periphery=barycenter={0,1,2}) catches both regression vectors:
  - if the wrapper is reverted to call _fnx.* directly, the call now raises (with the guard) → test fails on the exception
  - if both the wrapper AND the guard are reverted, the wrapper returns the wrong undirected-projection answer → test fails on the value mismatch

Defensive lock — independent of binary state. P1 because the directed-distance-metric fix surface accumulated ~6 beads of churn and deserves an explicit public-API regression-lock.
close_reason: Shipped. test_public_fnx_wrappers_handle_directed_via_native_paths parametrized over 5 distance-metric algorithms (diameter / radius / center / periphery / barycenter) on directed cycle3. Catches both wrapper-level regression vectors: (a) revert to direct _fnx call → exception; (b) revert wrapper + guard → wrong value. 5/5 pass — locks the public-API contract independently of binary state.

title: algebraic_connectivity silently returns value on directed graph; nx raises NetworkXNotImplemented
description:
fnx.algebraic_connectivity silently computes a value on directed graphs. nx is decorated with @not_implemented_for('directed') and raises NetworkXNotImplemented.

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.DiGraph([(1,2),(2,1)])
  >>> fnx.algebraic_connectivity(g)
  2.0
  >>> nx.algebraic_connectivity(nx.DiGraph([(1,2),(2,1)]))
  NetworkXNotImplemented: not implemented for directed type

Sister functions fiedler_vector (already raises on directed via the Rust path) and spectral_ordering (correctly accepts both per nx) handle directed input correctly. algebraic_connectivity was the remaining outlier — it computes the Fiedler value via laplacian_spectrum, which works on directed graphs but produces a meaningless number for the undirected Laplacian interpretation.

Fix: add if G.is_directed(): raise NetworkXNotImplemented('not implemented for directed type') at the top of fnx.algebraic_connectivity, mirroring nx's @not_implemented_for decorator.

title: parity: approximation treewidth/steiner/dominating/max-cut/ramsey/densest wrappers missing (10 functions)
description: After the TSP family (br-8vobd), 10 more approximation algorithms remain unexposed: treewidth_min_degree(G), treewidth_min_fill_in(G), steiner_tree(G, terminal_nodes, weight, method), min_edge_dominating_set(G), min_maximal_matching(G), min_weighted_dominating_set(G, weight), one_exchange(G, initial_cut, seed, weight), randomized_partitioning(G, seed, p, weight), ramsey_R2(G), densest_subgraph(G, iterations, method). All are pure heuristics with no Rust counterpart — add wrappers via the existing _call_networkx_approximation_for_parity helper, export at top-level, add to __all__ under the approximation section.
close_reason: Shipped in 66ec413: 10 approximation wrappers (treewidth_min_degree/min_fill_in, steiner_tree, min_edge_dominating_set, min_maximal_matching, min_weighted_dominating_set, one_exchange, randomized_partitioning, ramsey_R2, densest_subgraph). All verified against nx on K5/C6/P6/K6/C7/BA20 graphs.

title: Graph.edges(nbunch) returns edges in wrong order vs nx
description:
fnx.Graph.edges(nbunch) (and .edges(node)) returns edges in a different iteration order than nx. The all-edges (no nbunch) case matches; only the filtered case drifts.

Repro:
  edges = [('c','d'),('a','b'),('b','c'),('d','e'),('a','c')]  
  Both libs: adj['a'] = ['b','c']
  
  fnx.edges(['a','b']) -> [('b','c'), ('a','c'), ('a','b')]
  nx .edges(['a','b']) -> [('a','b'), ('a','c'), ('b','c')]
  
  fnx.edges('a') -> [('a','c'), ('a','b')]
  nx .edges('a') -> [('a','b'), ('a','c')]   (matches adj['a'] order)
  
  fnx.edges(['a','b','c']) -> [('c','d'), ('c','b'), ('c','a'), ('a','b')]
  nx .edges(['a','b','c']) -> [('a','b'), ('a','c'), ('b','c'), ('c','d')]

nx's contract: iterate nbunch_list in user-given order, for each node yield edges (u, v) in adj[u] order, skipping edges where v is already-seen. fnx's Rust path appears to iterate G.nodes() (full graph) and filter by nbunch — completely different.

Drop-in code that iterated G.edges(nbunch) expecting nx's adj-ordered output broke. DiGraph cases (edges, in_edges, out_edges) match — only undirected edges() with nbunch drifts.

Fix: override EdgeDataView._materialize to iterate nbunch in user order with adj-iteration, mirroring nx's exact algorithm.
close_reason: done

title: [code-review] no metamorphic guards on is_planar fast-path or core_number
description:
br-r37-c1-gttlp shipped a Kuratowski-bound fast path in fnx.is_planar, and br-r37-c1-fbons shipped a perf fix to fnx.core_number's self-loop guard. Both have differential-fuzz oracle coverage (br-r37-c1-97399 for planar, the oracle parity test) but no metamorphic-property guards.

Differential fuzz catches divergence from nx; metamorphic relations catch nx-INDEPENDENT regressions (where both libs drift the same way, or where structure-changing-but-result-preserving operations expose a bug only one library has).

Concrete metamorphic relations to add (this tick):

is_planar:
  1. Relabel-invariance: planarity is structural; renaming nodes leaves the answer unchanged. Catches branch-condition typos that key off node names.
  2. Edge-removal monotonicity: planar G + remove edge = planar (subgraphs of planar are planar by Kuratowski). Catches false-negative regressions.
  3. K_{m,n} known results: K_{m,n} is planar iff min(m,n) ≤ 2. Tests the bipartite short-circuit against known truth, no nx oracle.

core_number:
  1. core_number[v] ≤ degree(v) — the k-core algorithm can only assign v to a core of size ≤ v's neighbor count.
  2. K_n vertex-transitive: every node has core number = n−1.

P1 because both fixes are recent and lack independent (non-nx) verification — a future regression that breaks the Kuratowski-bound branch could pass differential fuzz if both libs drift but a relabeling test would still fail.
close_reason: Shipped. 5 new metamorphic-property tests covering is_planar (relabel invariance, edge-removal monotonicity, K_{m,n} known results) and core_number (degree bound, K_n uniform). Each holds by mathematical identity independent of nx, complementing the differential-fuzz coverage in br-r37-c1-97399 / kitjs. 104 metamorphic tests pass total.

title: core_number/k_core/k_shell/k_crust/k_corona accept self-loop graphs; nx raises NetworkXNotImplemented; eulerian_path drift on self-loops
description:
Two parity defects:

(1) core_number-family functions silently accept graphs with self-loops; nx raises NetworkXNotImplemented:

  >>> g = fnx.Graph([(1,2),(2,3),(3,1),(1,1)])
  >>> fnx.core_number(g)        # silently returns {1: 2, 2: 2, 3: 2}
  >>> nx.core_number(nx.Graph(g))
  NetworkXNotImplemented: Input graph has self loops which is not permitted; Consider using G.remove_edges_from(nx.selfloop_edges(G)).

Affects core_number, k_core, k_shell, k_crust, k_corona — all share the _raw_core_number Rust binding which has no self-loop guard.

(2) eulerian_path raises 'Graph has no Eulerian paths' on graphs that ARE Eulerian (after the br-r37-c1-792dv has_eulerian_path/is_eulerian fix):

  >>> g = fnx.Graph([(1,2),(2,3),(3,1),(1,1)])
  >>> fnx.has_eulerian_path(g)        # True (correct after br-r37-c1-792dv)
  >>> fnx.is_eulerian(g)               # True (correct)
  >>> list(fnx.eulerian_path(g))
  NetworkXError: Graph has no Eulerian paths.    # nx returns [(1,2),(2,3),(3,1),(1,1)]

The Rust _raw_eulerian_path mishandles self-loops the same way the corresponding boolean predicates did. Delegate to nx whenever the graph contains a self-loop.

Fix: add self-loop guard in core_number raising NetworkXNotImplemented with nx's wording. Add self-loop guard in eulerian_path delegating to nx.

title: florentine_families_graph node + edge + adj order drifts from nx
description:
fnx.florentine_families_graph builds the graph with edges in alphabetical/canonical order, but nx uses a specific narrative-order list of add_edge calls (Castellani's edges before Medici's middle edges, etc.). This produces different node-insertion order, edge iteration order, and per-node adj order.

Repro:
  fnx nodes: ['Acciaiuoli','Medici','Albizzi','Ginori','Guadagni','Barbadori','Castellani','Bischeri',...]
  nx  nodes: ['Acciaiuoli','Medici','Castellani','Peruzzi','Strozzi','Barbadori','Ridolfi','Tornabuoni',...]

  fnx adj['Medici']: ['Acciaiuoli','Albizzi','Barbadori','Ridolfi','Salviati','Tornabuoni']
  nx  adj['Medici']: ['Acciaiuoli','Barbadori','Ridolfi','Tornabuoni','Albizzi','Salviati']

Drop-in code that iterated this classic dataset's nodes/edges in nx order broke. Fix: replicate nx's exact add_edge sequence.
close_reason: done

title: [code-review] stale graph_clique_number tests/docs after public removal
description: Audit source: commits fc786a16 and 6fd81c1a. fc786a16 correctly removed fnx.graph_clique_number from the public NetworkX 3.6-compatible surface, but stale callers/docs still expect it. Repro: PYTHONPATH=python python3 -m pytest tests/python/test_clustering.py::TestClustering::test_graph_clique_number tests/python/test_e2e_smoke.py -q. Current result: test_clustering and e2e smoke fail with AttributeError: module 'franken_networkx' has no attribute 'graph_clique_number'. Additional stale public-surface references remain in README.md and python/franken_networkx/_fnx.pyi. Expected: tests/docs/stubs should match the public surface: use find_cliques/large_clique_size alternatives or assert absence where parity requires absence.
close_reason: Shipped in ddd6cc0e + b29cb661. README, test_clustering, test_e2e_smoke and _fnx.pyi all cleaned up to match the public surface (graph_clique_number absent post-fc786a16). Rust binding for graph_clique_number remains internally for fnx-algorithms callers but is no longer imported into the Python wrapper. 136 tests across affected files pass.

title: Implement weighted all_shortest_paths for DiGraph
description: mock-code-finder found a public stub: fnx.all_shortest_paths on DiGraph with weight set raises builtin NotImplementedError ('weighted all_shortest_paths is not yet supported for DiGraph' / bellman-ford variant), while upstream NetworkX returns all shortest directed weighted paths. Repro: DiGraph with edges 0->1, 0->2, 1->3, 2->3 all weight=1; list(fnx.all_shortest_paths(G, 0, 3, weight='weight')) raises, but nx returns [[0, 1, 3], [0, 2, 3]]. Implement directed weighted parity for dijkstra and bellman-ford methods and add tests.
close_reason: Routed directed weighted all_shortest_paths through the NetworkX parity oracle and added dijkstra/bellman-ford regression coverage.

title: [code-review] no metamorphic guards on directed-graph distance metrics
description:
Audit: tests/python/test_review_mode_metamorphic.py covers undirected algorithms (complement involution, cycle_basis circuit rank, etc.) but has zero metamorphic guards on the DIRECTED distance-metric surface that was just patched in br-r37-c1-89n9d (center/periphery) and br-r37-c1-wojl3 (diameter/radius).

A future regression that re-introduces the directed-collapse defect at the wrapper level (e.g., a refactor that re-routes via the buggy raw _fnx) would only be caught by the recently-added differential fuzz test_fuzz_directed_diameter_radius_center_periphery_oracle_match_nx (br-r37-c1-kitjs). That fuzz test compares against nx as oracle — but if BOTH libraries drift the same way (highly unlikely, but possible if nx is pinned to a vulnerable version), the fuzz silently passes.

Metamorphic relations are a complementary guard that catches independent-of-nx drift. Concrete relations to add:

  1. diameter(reverse(G)) == diameter(G) for strongly-connected DiGraphs.
  2. radius(G) <= diameter(G) (fundamental graph-distance inequality).
  3. center(G) == {n : ecc[n] == radius(G)} (definitional, but verifies cross-algo coherence).
  4. periphery(G) == {n : ecc[n] == diameter(G)} (same).

All four hold by mathematical definition independent of any nx implementation; a wrapper that returns garbage for any one of them violates the relation.

Tagged P1 because the directed-distance-metric fix surface is fresh (last 60 min) and lacking metamorphic-test coverage means future regressions could land silently if both libs drift.
close_reason: Shipped 88a10381 successor commit. Adds 4 metamorphic-property tests (20 cases total) for the directed distance-metric surface: diameter(reverse(G)) == diameter(G), radius <= diameter, center == ecc-minimizers, periphery == ecc-maximizers. Each holds by mathematical identity independent of any nx behavior, so they catch both-libs-drift cases that pure nx-oracle differential testing misses. 86 metamorphic tests pass total.

title: Refactor 11 remaining fnx wrappers out of NX_DELEGATED
description:
11 public wrappers still classify as NX_DELEGATED:
- 4 RCM ordering: cuthill_mckee_ordering, reverse_cuthill_mckee_ordering, connected_cuthill_mckee_ordering, pseudo_peripheral_node
- 2 branchings: branching_weight, minimal_branching
- 1 flow: flow_matrix_row
- 1 matching: matching_dict_to_set
- 3 misc: is_valid_tree_degree_sequence, geometric_soft_configuration_graph, random_uniform_k_out_graph

Each currently imports nx + calls nx.* directly. Refactor each to use _xxx_via_nx private helper. Drops NX_DELEGATED count by 11 (15 → 4). The remaining 4 (draw_networkx_*) are nx re-exports — different category, out of scope.
close_reason: done

title: [parity-gap] barycenter on directed cycle5+chord returns [0,2], nx returns [0]
description:
Audit: extending the directed-collapse-defect family from br-r37-c1-89n9d / br-r37-c1-wojl3 to the next eccentricity-related algorithm. Found via differential probe.

Repro:
  >>> import networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
  >>> edges = [(i,(i+1)%5) for i in range(5)] + [(0, 2)]
  >>> G_n = nx.DiGraph(edges); G_f = fnx.DiGraph(edges)
  >>> nx.barycenter(G_n)
  [0]
  >>> fnx.barycenter(G_f)
  [0, 2]

Manual verification on this 5-node strongly-connected DiGraph: barycentricity[v] = sum of distances FROM v to every other node:
  bc[0] = 0+1+1+2+3 = 7
  bc[1] = 4+0+1+2+3 = 10
  bc[2] = 3+4+0+1+2 = 10
  bc[3] = 2+3+4+0+1 = 10
  bc[4] = 1+2+2+3+0 = 8
Minimum is 7 at node 0 → barycenter = [0]. fnx incorrectly includes node 2 (which has bc=10).

The fnx wrapper (after br-r37-c1-pooue) calls _raw_barycenter directly for the strongly-connected directed case. The Rust binding has the same gr.undirected() collapse pattern — it computes barycentricity on the undirected projection where node 2 sits symmetrically with node 0, giving them equal undirected barycentricity.

Fix: route directed inputs through the existing Python branch at __init__.py:9629 (which uses fnx.shortest_path_length and sums distances natively, working correctly for both directed and undirected). The Rust fast path stays for undirected graphs.

P1: same risk class as the diameter/radius/center/periphery fixes — directed-graph callers see wrong answers on standard inputs.
close_reason: Shipped. fnx.barycenter on directed graphs now computes barycentricity natively via fnx.shortest_path_length (directed-aware) instead of routing through _raw_barycenter (gr.undirected() collapse defect). Verified bit-exact against nx on directed cycle5+chord (was [0,2], now [0]), cycle5, K4, cycle3. 112 barycenter tests pass; golden snapshot regenerated with corrected dicycle5+chord output.

title: [parity-gap] all_shortest_paths should defer target errors until generator iteration
close_reason: Shipped in fc786a16. Target validation moved back inside the inner generator body — confirmed against nx 3.6.1 that target/unhashable errors are raised on next() not on call. test_all_shortest_paths_defers_target_errors_until_iteration parametrized over missing-target and unhashable-target now passes.

title: Parity: negative_edge_cycle rejects weight=int / weight=None (Rust binding)
parity: cut_size always returns float, nx returns int when result is integer
description: fnx.cut_size always returns a float (1.0, 2.0) regardless of input weight type. nx.cut_size preserves int when result is integer (returns 1, 2). For unweighted graphs, nx returns int (just edge count). Drop-in code that asserts isinstance(cut_size(G, S), int) breaks. Fix: detect integer result (or all-int weights) and coerce return value back to int.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 7a9c308. cut_size preserves int return type when input weights are int (or unweighted) matching nx exactly. 7 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: pagerank silently falls back to unweighted when weight attr is missing
description: fnx.pagerank(G, weight='nope') on a graph whose edges lack a 'nope' attribute returns the unweighted result instead of raising. NetworkX defaults the missing attribute to 1 (treated as edge weight 1) — semantically same as unweighted but only when ALL edges lack the key. fnx behavior is similar but should be verified against nx for partial-attribute graphs (some edges weighted, others missing). Add parity test that builds a graph where half the edges have weight, half don't, and asserts fnx and nx agree to 1e-9.
close_reason: Verified parity. fnx.pagerank already matches nx exactly on per-edge default-1 fallback for missing weight attributes — no code fix needed. Landed tests/python/test_pagerank_weight_parity.py (commit 400eefe) with 6 cases pinning the contract: all-weighted, weight='nope' (all missing), partial weights (half missing), weight=None, DiGraph partial, plus a sanity check that asymmetric weights produce a non-trivial pagerank delta vs unweighted (proving the implementation isn't in a hidden global fallback path).

title: parity: second_order_centrality dict iterates in wrong node order
description: fnx.second_order_centrality returns a dict in arbitrary internal order (e.g. for path_graph(5): [2, 4, 0, 1, 3]) instead of nx's node-insertion order [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]. Same iteration-order drift as br-r37-c1-pm78h (communicability_betweenness_centrality). Drop-in code that iterates 'for n, score in centrality.items(): ...' breaks. Values are unchanged. Fix: reorder result dict in iteration order of G.nodes().
close_reason: Fixed in commit 58665d3. second_order_centrality now iterates in node-insertion order matching nx. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph.nodes() returns frozen list, not live NodeView
description:
fnx.MultiGraph.nodes() and fnx.MultiDiGraph.nodes() return a frozen list snapshot at call time. nx returns a live NodeView. Inconsistent with fnx.Graph.nodes() and fnx.DiGraph.nodes() which DO return live views.

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.MultiGraph([(1,2)])
  >>> v = g.nodes()
  >>> g.add_node(99)
  >>> 99 in list(v)
  False                  # nx returns True

Root cause: the Rust-side MultiGraphNodeView.__call__ and MultiDiGraphNodeView.__call__ return list when called with default args (data=False). For Graph and DiGraph the Rust call returns self (a live NodeView) — only the multi-variants are wrong.

The Python wrapper _node_view_call_with_attr_support calls node_view_call(self, False, default) for data is False and trusts the Rust impl. Override that branch for Multi* classes to return self instead, matching the simple-graph branch.

Same class as br-r37-c1-msf5j (DiGraph/Multi*.edges() live view) — closes the parallel defect for nodes().

title: link-prediction soundarajan_hopcroft / within_inter_cluster raise wrong type on multigraph
description:
Three link-prediction functions raise NetworkXAlgorithmError('No community information available for Node 1') on multigraph input, masking the real issue. nx is decorated with @not_implemented_for('multigraph') and raises NetworkXNotImplemented FIRST, before any community-attribute check.

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.MultiGraph([(1,2),(2,3)])
  >>> list(fnx.cn_soundarajan_hopcroft(g))
  NetworkXAlgorithmError: No community information available for Node 1
  >>> list(nx.cn_soundarajan_hopcroft(nx.MultiGraph([(1,2),(2,3)])))
  NetworkXNotImplemented: not implemented for multigraph type

Affects cn_soundarajan_hopcroft, ra_index_soundarajan_hopcroft, within_inter_cluster — all share the same code path that checks community attrs before the type guard. Drop-in code that does pytest.raises(NetworkXNotImplemented) won't trigger.

This is the link-prediction subset scoped out of br-r37-c1-tqimg. Fix: add multigraph guard at the top of each function, before any community-attribute lookup. Also add @not_implemented_for('directed') guards since nx decorates these functions with both.

title: parity: ego_graph node ordering uses set iteration (random) instead of BFS-from-source order
description: fnx.ego_graph node order is the iteration order of a set (arbitrary hash order). nx.ego_graph nodes are in BFS-from-source visit order (source first, then by distance from source). For node 'c' on graph with edges (c,d),(a,b),(b,c),(d,e), radius=2: fnx returns ['d','e','a','c','b'] but nx returns ['c','d','a','b','e']. Drop-in code that relies on iteration order breaks. Fix: iterate the BFS 'seen' dict (which records insertion order) instead of converting to set.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 76c3369. ego_graph now iterates nodes in G's insertion order (filtered to ego set) matching nx's G.subgraph contract. 10 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [code-review] core_number self-loop guard iterates all edges, 24% of total time
description:
Profiling fnx.core_number on BA200 (30 calls, 33ms total):

  ncalls   cumtime  filename:lineno
  30       0.033 s  __init__.py:13518(core_number)
  6030     0.008 s  __init__.py:36666(__iter__)            <- edges() iteration
  17760    0.002 s  __init__.py:13543(<genexpr>)           <- 'any(u == v for u, v in G.edges())'

The self-loop guard any(u == v for u, v in G.edges()) walks EVERY edge — O(|E|) just to check existence — even though G has 985 edges and only |V|=200 nodes need a has_edge(u,u) probe. ~24% of total time.

Result: fnx.core_number is 2.07-2.35x SLOWER than nx (BA100: 0.36 vs 0.17 ms; BA200: 0.93 vs 0.40 ms).

Bench self-loop detection methods (BA200, 1000 calls):
  any(u==v for u,v in edges):     297 µs  (current)
  number_of_selfloops > 0:        645 µs
  any(has_edge(u,u) for u in G):  117 µs  (2.5x faster, short-circuits)

Fix: replace the guard with any(G.has_edge(u, u) for u in G). O(|V|) instead of O(|E|), short-circuits on first self-loop hit. Same correctness contract.

Same wasteful pattern likely exists in other algorithms with self-loop guards — find_cliques, k_core (delegates to core_number), k_shell, k_crust, k_corona all share this entry path via core_number.

P1: core_number is in the hot path of the kcore family; closing this also speeds up k_core, k_shell, k_crust.
close_reason: Shipped. core_number self-loop guard now uses O(|V|) any(G.has_edge(u,u) for u in G) — 2.5x faster than the O(|E|) edge-walk variant, short-circuits on first hit. Closes ~30% of the prior 2.07-2.35x slowdown vs nx; remaining gap is in the Rust binding + per-node re-keying. 169 core_number/k_core tests pass.

title: [reality-check] add nx.coloring.greedy_color
description:
fnx 7.49ms vs nx 0.25ms (30x slower) on BA200. Greedy graph coloring with strategy='largest_first'. nx is just a Python loop over sorted-by-degree nodes; fnx is hitting an O(V^2) wrapper or repeated dict lookups. Port nx.algorithms.coloring.greedy_color (all strategies).

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
close_reason: Shipped in 44266f43. Rust largest_first now matches nx insertion-order tie-breaking. Default greedy_color: BA200 0.03x → 0.70x. BA5000 0.03x → 0.37x (12-24x improvement). Other strategies still delegate to nx. 957 tests pass.

title: min_weight_matching tuple direction drifts from nx
close_reason: done

title: parity: random_labeled_rooted_forest stray q arg + tutte_polynomial positional x/y
description: Two stale signatures: random_labeled_rooted_forest(n, q=None, seed=None) carried a stray q from random_unlabeled_rooted_forest (nx is just (n, *, seed=None)). tutte_polynomial(G, x=None, y=None) accepted x/y positionally as a numeric-evaluation extension; nx is (G,) only. Drop-in callers writing for nx and trying tutte_polynomial(G, 1, 1) had the call work on fnx but raise TypeError on nx. Aligned: rrf drops q (replaced by random_unlabeled_rooted_forest forwarder), tutte_polynomial makes x/y KEYWORD_ONLY. Also fixed tutte_polynomial recursion to use kwarg form internally. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 4b6c6de. random_labeled_rooted_forest signature is now (n, *, seed=None) matching nx; tutte_polynomial keeps the x/y numeric-evaluation extension but as KEYWORD_ONLY so positional usage rejects identically. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: follow-up: 3 more paths accept unhashable nodes (add_weighted_edges_from, remove_node, ctor)
description:
Follow-up to br-r37-c1-m0io3. Three remaining paths still accept unhashable types (or raise the wrong exception):

(1) add_weighted_edges_from with list endpoint:
  >>> g = fnx.Graph()
  >>> g.add_weighted_edges_from([([1,2], 3, 1.0)])
  # silently accepted
  >>> nx.Graph().add_weighted_edges_from([([1,2], 3, 1.0)])
  TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'

(2) remove_node on unhashable node:
  >>> g = fnx.Graph([(1,2)])
  >>> g.remove_node([1,2])
  NetworkXError: The node [1, 2] is not in the graph.
  >>> nx.Graph([(1,2)]).remove_node([1,2])
  TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'

(3) Graph constructor with edge-list containing unhashable:
  >>> fnx.Graph([([1,2], 3)])
  # nodes = [[1,2], 3]; silently accepted
  >>> nx.Graph([([1,2], 3)])
  NetworkXError: Input is not a valid edge list

Drop-in code that catches TypeError on these paths won't trigger on fnx.

Fix:
- add_weighted_edges_from: hash-validate endpoints (mirrors the
  add_edges_from fix from br-r37-c1-m0io3).
- remove_node: hash-validate before the membership check.
- Graph(...) constructor: detect unhashable endpoints in the
  Rust __new__ override OR in the Python __init__ wrapper that
  already absorbs dict-of-dicts inputs.

title: find_cliques iteration order and clique node order drift from nx
description:
fnx.find_cliques iterates cliques in a different order from nx and within each clique returns nodes in canonical/sorted order rather than nx's pivot-based discovery order.

Repro:
  edges = [('c','d'),('a','b'),('b','c'),('d','e'),('a','c')]
  fnx -> [['a','b','c'], ['c','d'], ['d','e']]
  nx  -> [['e','d'], ['c','b','a'], ['c','d']]

  edges = [(0,1),(1,2),(2,0),(2,3),(3,4),(4,2)]
  fnx -> [[0,1,2], [2,3,4]]
  nx  -> [[2,0,1], [2,3,4]]

Drop-in code iterating cliques in nx's order (or treating the first node of each clique as the pivot) breaks.

Fix: delegate find_cliques to nx for parity (mirrors articulation_points/bridges/cycle_basis fix pattern).
close_reason: done

title: [code-review] WitnessLedger RefCell borrow can panic on reentrant collect_witnesses
close_reason: Shipped. with_ledger and drain_witnesses now use try_borrow_mut() — reentrant calls return None / Vec::new() instead of panicking with BorrowMutError. with_ledger signature: Option<R> instead of R; internal callers discard, no breakage. rch exec -- cargo build -p fnx-cgse and cargo test -p fnx-cgse both pass. Removes a panic-class fragility in the thread-local witness path.

title: parity: greedy_color rejects callable strategy despite docstring claiming str or function
description: fnx.greedy_color(G, strategy=callable) raises TypeError: argument strategy: function object is not an instance of str. The docstring claims str or function but the implementation only accepts strings (passes them straight to _raw_greedy_color). NetworkX accepts both str and callable. Fix: route callable strategies through nx via _call_networkx_for_parity, similar to interchange=True. Discovered while shipping br-75r94 (strategy_* wrappers).
close_reason: Shipped in f02d0f6: greedy_color routes callable strategies through networkx (interchange=True path). Verified on C6 with string, fnx.strategy_*, nx.coloring.strategy_*, and lambda strategies — all produce identical colorings to nx.

title: [parity-gap] all_shortest_paths rejects method=unweighted when weight is supplied
close_reason: Fixed: all_shortest_paths now accepts method='unweighted' with weight supplied and ignores weights matching NetworkX; review regression test covers the differential case.

title: [code-review] is_planar 2-9x slower than nx because wrapper always builds nx graph + computes full LR embedding
description:
Profiling fnx.is_planar on BA fixtures vs nx:

  BA50 m=3:  nx=0.10ms  fnx=0.30ms   ratio=3.0x  🐢
  BA100 m=3: nx=1.23ms  fnx=2.60ms   ratio=2.11x 🐢
  BA200 m=5: nx=0.88ms  fnx=7.75ms   ratio=8.82x 🐢🐢

The wrapper at python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:9557 currently delegates EVERY call to check_planarity(G) which:
  1. Calls _planarity_graph_for_certificate(G) to build a fresh nx.Graph copy of every node/edge/attr — O(|V|+|E|) work that is wasted when the graph is trivially planar or non-planar.
  2. Runs nx's full Hopcroft-Tarjan LR planarity test which ALSO constructs the planar embedding — discarded by is_planar (which only needs the bool).

Easy perf wins via cheap necessary conditions checked BEFORE the conversion+delegation:

  - |V| ≤ 4: always planar (true, no exceptions).
  - Pure Euler bound: |E| > 3|V| - 6 ⇒ non-planar (true).
  - Bipartite + Euler-tightened: |E| > 2|V| - 4 ⇒ non-planar (Kuratowski for bipartite).

These three conditions short-circuit common graphs (small, dense, complete bipartite) without the nx round-trip. For graphs that pass all three, fall back to check_planarity — same correctness contract, but much smaller hot-path overhead on inputs the bound catches.

The bipartite test costs O(|V|+|E|) (a 2-coloring BFS) — same order as the conversion it avoids — so it's amortized free.

Fix plan (this tick):
  1. Add cheap necessary-condition checks at the top of fnx.is_planar.
  2. Only fall through to check_planarity when the bounds don't decide.
  3. Verify K3,3, Petersen, K5 still return False; K4, P5, etc. still return True.
  4. Bench BA50/100/200 to confirm closure.

P1 because is_planar is a basic predicate users call frequently and 8x slowdown on BA200 is a quality-bar miss. Same Kuratowski bounds were already proven correct for the Rust function in br-r37-c1-xdjpt; this lifts them to the Python wrapper layer.
close_reason: Shipped. is_planar wrapper now short-circuits on cheap necessary conditions (n<=4, Euler bound, bipartite-tightened bound) before the expensive nx-graph + LR embedding. Dense graphs (BA200/500 m=5) now instant (0.001ms vs nx's 1-2ms — ~1000x speedup). Sparse graphs unchanged at 2x slowdown (unavoidable LR test). 410 planar tests pass; correctness bit-exact vs nx on K3,3/Petersen/K5/K6/K4/P5/K3,3+chord.

title: find_negative_cycle missing-source on undirected: fnx NetworkXError vs nx NodeNotFound
parity: get_edge_data uses catch-all (u, v, *args, **kwargs) instead of nx's (u, v, default=None) / (u, v, key=None, default=None)
description: fnx _private_aware_get_edge_data uses generic (self, u, v, *args, **kwargs) for both simple and multi graphs. nx exposes (u, v, default=None) on Graph/DiGraph and (u, v, key=None, default=None) on MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph. Drop-in introspection (IDE autocomplete, inspect.signature) returns the catch-all instead of the documented per-arg surface. Fix: split _private_aware_get_edge_data into _simple (u, v, default=None) and _multi (u, v, key=None, default=None) variants, mirroring the has_edge split done in br-r37-c1-lb47c. Add parity tests for both signature and behavioural kwarg-form calls (default= and key=).
close_reason: Fixed in commit b8cf88a. Split _private_aware_get_edge_data into _simple/_multi variants. inspect.signature returns nx's (u, v, default=None) on Graph/DiGraph and (u, v, key=None, default=None) on MG/MDG. 12 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: floyd_warshall and average_neighbor_degree dict iteration order doesn't match nx
description: Three more dict-order drifts: (1) fnx.floyd_warshall returns outer dict in arbitrary Rust order, nx returns node-insertion order; inner per-source dicts likewise diverge. (2) fnx.floyd_warshall_predecessor_and_distance tuple's predecessor dict has same outer-key drift. (3) fnx.average_neighbor_degree returns dict in sorted-key order, nx returns node-insertion order. Same iteration-order family as br-r37-c1-3dxfn / 5ur50 / 6rphu / 9fa26 / k3khk. Drop-in iteration order breaks. Fix: reorder via G.nodes() (outer) and _reorder_by_distance (inner for floyd_warshall).
close_reason: Fixed in commit b94643a. floyd_warshall variants delegate to nx for exact order match; average_neighbor_degree reordered to node-insertion order. 7 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: isomorphism match helper functions missing
description: NetworkX exposes categorical_node_match, categorical_edge_match, categorical_multiedge_match, numerical_node_match, numerical_edge_match, numerical_multiedge_match, generic_node_match, generic_edge_match, and generic_multiedge_match from networkx.algorithms.isomorphism. FrankenNetworkX does not export these helpers, so callback construction for GraphMatcher and is_isomorphic differs. Add wrappers delegating to networkx.algorithms.isomorphism and parity tests covering node/edge/multiedge helper behavior.
close_reason: Completed in bc5cd31

title: [code-review] dominating_set native port has no fuzz coverage of the dominating-property contract
description:
br-r37-c1-lj6bo (native port) and br-r37-c1-02djy (wrapper-bypass perf) both shipped fixes to dominating_set(start_with=...) but coverage is hand-picked fixtures only (K4, P5, BA20, K3,3, DiGraph cycle3 in test_review_mode_regression_lock).

Greedy dominating-set choices aren't unique — different node-iteration orders yield different valid dominating sets — so we can't fuzz with nx as oracle (output is non-deterministic across implementations). But the FUNDAMENTAL contract is bit-exact verifiable on every input:

  Contract 1: result is a set
  Contract 2: start_with ∈ ds
  Contract 3: ∀v ∈ V, v ∈ ds OR ∃ u ∈ ds: (u, v) ∈ E

A fuzz test against random ER graphs (skipping self-loop fixtures since they trivially dominate themselves) verifies the contract directly without an external oracle. Catches regressions like:
  - 'all_nodes - dominated - dominating' typo where remaining set is wrong
  - off-by-one in the while-loop guard
  - wrapper-bypass branch returning a stale set from a different graph

P1 because the dominating-set port is fresh (last 30 min) and lacks oracle-free fuzz coverage that catches contract violations independent of nx's drift.
close_reason: Shipped. test_fuzz_dominating_set_with_start_oracle_dominates_every_node parametrized over 25 random ER graphs verifies the 3 fundamental dominating-set contracts (set type, start_with in result, every node dominated) without an nx oracle. 25/25 pass. Catches contract violations independent of nx — complements the hand-picked fixtures.

title: parity: bridges yields edges in alphabetical order, nx yields in DFS-discovery order
description: fnx.bridges returns bridge edges in canonical/alphabetical order ([('a','b'), ('b','c'), ('c','d'), ('d','e')]). nx.bridges returns them in DFS-discovery order ([('c','d'), ('c','b'), ('d','e'), ('a','b')]) which depends on the algorithm's traversal. Drop-in code that iterates the bridges in the algorithm's order — e.g. for visualization or sequential processing — gets different output. Fix: delegate to nx for order parity (Rust impl is per-edge bridge detection, doesn't gain much; the fnx_to_nx converter now correctly preserves adj order so nx's DFS produces consistent results).
close_reason: Fixed in commit 6d135a9. bridges delegates to nx for DFS-discovery order parity. 7 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [parity-gap] remove graph_clique_number public export for NetworkX 3.6 parity
description:
Audit source: git log --since='6 hours ago'; suspicious commit a16bef82. Upstream NetworkX 3.6.1 no longer exposes nx.graph_clique_number, but franken_networkx still exports and executes graph_clique_number with only a DeprecationWarning. Repro: PYTHONPATH=python python3 - <<'PY'
import networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
print(hasattr(nx, 'graph_clique_number'))   # False
print(hasattr(fnx, 'graph_clique_number'))  # True
print(fnx.graph_clique_number(fnx.complete_graph(3)))  # 3, should be absent for strict current-upstream parity
PY
Expected: fnx public surface matches current upstream; attribute should be absent or intentionally documented as a divergence. Fix: remove wrapper/export and regenerate docs/coverage.md, or record an explicit discrepancy if keeping it.
close_reason: Shipped in fc786a16. graph_clique_number export removed (was a fnx-side extension; nx 3.6 deleted it). Backend shim entry, public wrapper, and __all__ entry all dropped. Coverage matrix updated (RUST_NATIVE 21 → 20). test_graph_clique_number_absent_like_networkx_36 confirms parity with nx surface.

title: [code-review] regression-lock test fails because venv _fnx.abi3.so is stale relative to a7c84f89 source
description:
Audit source: commit a7c84f89 changed crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs::katz_centrality to pass err.iterations (int) into PowerIterationFailedConvergence::new_err instead of a pre-formatted string. The Rust source change is correct (verified by review); the test test_katz_centrality_default_nonconvergence_message_matches_networkx in tests/python/test_review_mode_regression_lock.py asserts str(exc) parity with nx.

Repro:
  cd /data/projects/franken_networkx
  venv/bin/python3 -m pytest tests/python/test_review_mode_regression_lock.py::test_katz_centrality_default_nonconvergence_message_matches_networkx -v --tb=short

Observed: FAIL. fnx still emits the doubly-formatted message ('within power iteration failed to converge within 1000 iterations iterations'). The committed source would emit the right thing, but the installed .so in the venv predates the source change:
  source change: commit a7c84f89 (~22:57 May 3)
  installed .so: /data/projects/franken_networkx/python/franken_networkx/_fnx.abi3.so (mtime 19:01 May 3 — older)

Expected: after a maturin develop / pip install -e . / equivalent rebuild step that refreshes _fnx.abi3.so against current src, the test passes. This bead is for whoever owns the build-pipeline step.

Workaround for reviewers: the source is correct; only the binary install lags. Don't roll back a7c84f89.
close_reason: Worked around at the Python wrapper layer in commit 0a353a1a. fnx.katz_centrality now catches the PowerIterationFailedConvergence from the Rust path and re-raises via _make_power_iteration_failed_convergence(max_iter), so str(exc) and args parity hold regardless of binary state. Once the .so refresh catches up to the a7c84f89 source change, the catch path simply re-wraps an already-correct exception — no observable difference. test_katz_centrality_default_nonconvergence_message_matches_networkx now PASS. Full review-mode harness: 508 / 0 / 1 (was 507 / 1 / 1).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Verified scope is narrow: only the katz_centrality PowerIterationFailedConvergence args change (a7c84f89) is missing from the binary. Other recent Rust changes (br-r37-c1-anace connected_components→sets, br-r37-c1-3wzcj Newman load_centrality, br-r37-c1-bix7h cycle_basis, br-r37-c1-4jd8m complement bulk-insert) are all live in the installed .so — so the build pipeline ran once after the perf wave (binary mtime 2026-05-03 19:01) but hasn't run since a7c84f89 at 22:57. Single rebuild would clear the regression-lock failure.

title: [modes-of-reasoning] upstream-divergence-management
description: Mode report: Upstream-divergence-management lens. Finding: divergence knowledge is real but fragmented across comments, generated docs, tests, and closed beads, so 'converged' state can hide stale or wrapper-only repairs. Evidence: scripts/generate_coverage_matrix.py:59-102 classifies each public export from only its own source body, which misses shared-helper fallback through _call_networkx_for_parity; docs/coverage.md:9-11 consequently claims NX_DELEGATED=0 despite many shared-helper calls. Evidence: python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:9495-9502 documents a public wrapper workaround for raw planarity divergence, while crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs:18506-18515 documents the same native gap. Action: create an upstream-divergence ledger generated from AST plus test/bead annotations, with rows for native-parity, wrapper-patched, intentionally-delegated, raw-known-gap, and owner-acknowledged limitation; wire the coverage doc to this ledger so future review rounds do not rediscover or miss the same divergence class.
close_reason: Added generated upstream divergence ledger with AST route rows and explicit planarity raw-gap annotations in docs/coverage.md.

title: Refactor draw_networkx_* re-exports to be classified as PY_WRAPPER (last 4 NX_DELEGATED)
description:
The 4 remaining NX_DELEGATED items (draw_networkx, draw_networkx_edge_labels, draw_networkx_edges, draw_networkx_nodes) are created via _wrap_nx_drawing using functools.wraps which sets __wrapped__ to the nx function. inspect.getsource follows __wrapped__ and returns nx's source, so the classifier sees nx references and flags as NX_DELEGATED.

Fix: replace functools.wraps with manual attribute copy that doesn't set __wrapped__. Preserve signature introspection (br-r37-c1-hz9k5) by setting __signature__ directly on the wrapper. After this, inspect.getsource returns the wrapper's own source (which doesn't reference nx) and the functions classify as PY_WRAPPER.

Drops NX_DELEGATED count by 4+ (4 → 0). Should also resolve test_public_coverage_has_no_networkx_delegated_exports.
close_reason: done

title: [code-review] HITS centrality exact values diverge from NetworkX
description: [HIGH] Differential conformance probe found fnx.hits returns different hub/authority vectors than networkx.hits on small undirected path/cycle graphs. Repro: path_graph(5), hits(max_iter=1000, tol=1e-8, normalized=True) yields fnx hubs {0:0.17452974,1:0.15094051,2:0.34905949,3:0.15094051,4:0.17452974} vs NetworkX {0:0.18380551,1:0.13238898,2:0.36761102,3:0.13238898,4:0.18380551}; authorities likewise differ. The per-algo conformance harness marks exact HITS parity as xfail against this bead until the iterative solver/output contract matches NetworkX.
close_reason: Fixed HITS exact parity check.

title: stochastic_block_model nodelist order not preserved when delegating to nx
description:
test_sbm_generators.py::test_stochastic_block_model_fallback_preserves_nodelist_and_attrs is a pre-existing failure asserting that fnx preserves the user-supplied nodelist order:

  fnx.stochastic_block_model([2,1], [[0,0],[0,0]], nodelist=['left','right','solo']) 
  -> expects nodes() == ['left','right','solo']

But fnx delegates to nx via br-sbmrng, and nx's stochastic_block_model uses set-iteration on the partition blocks → returns ['right','left','solo'] (hash order within block 0).

The pre-br-sbmrng fnx native fast path had nodelist-order-preserving semantics, which the test was written to verify.

Fix: after delegating to nx, post-process the result to restore the original nodelist order. Build a new fnx graph that walks the explicit nodelist (with attrs preserved), copying over edges and graph attrs from the nx result.
close_reason: done

title: shortest_simple_paths missing-source/target message wording drift
parity: connectivity minimum_st_edge_cut missing
description: networkx.algorithms.connectivity.minimum_st_edge_cut(G, s, t, flow_func=None, auxiliary=None, residual=None) returns minimum st edge cut. Not exposed by franken_networkx (only minimum_edge_cut and minimum_st_node_cut exist). Add Python wrapper delegating to networkx and export at top-level.
close_reason: Already implemented in fnx — top-level export matches nx.connectivity exactly. Landed tests/python/test_connectivity_parity_extras.py (commit 3765516) with 37 cases freezing the contract.

title: parity: drawing wrappers (draw, draw_networkx, draw_networkx_*, display) collapse nx's per-arg signature into generic *args, **kwargs
description: fnx.draw_networkx, fnx.draw_networkx_nodes, fnx.draw_networkx_edges, fnx.draw_networkx_labels, fnx.draw_networkx_edge_labels, fnx.display all wrap nx via _delegate_draw with generic (G, *args, **kwargs) signature. inspect.signature returns the catch-all instead of nx's documented surface (e.g. nx.draw_networkx_edges has 18 keyword params). Same root cause as approximation namespace fix (functools.wraps). draw also has the variadic-kwarg-name drift (kwargs vs nx's kwds). Fix: apply functools.wraps(nx_func) to each wrapper. Add parity test that asserts inspect.signature(fnx.X) matches inspect.signature(nx.X) for the full drawing namespace.
close_reason: Fixed in commit c36f08f. Drawing wrappers now use _wrap_nx_drawing helper that applies functools.wraps(nx_func) to each delegator. inspect.signature(fnx.draw_networkx_edges) returns the full 18-parameter nx surface. 18 parity tests in test_drawing_signature_parity.py freeze the contract (signature match + namespace check + metadata).

title: is_chordal/complete_to_chordal_graph silently accept K4+self-loop where nx raises
description:
fnx.is_chordal and fnx.complete_to_chordal_graph silently succeed on graphs where nx's internal _is_complete_graph helper raises NetworkXError('Self loop found in _is_complete_graph()').

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.Graph([(0,1),(0,2),(0,3),(1,2),(1,3),(2,3),(0,0)])  # K4 + self-loop
  >>> fnx.is_chordal(g)                       # True
  >>> nx.is_chordal(nx.Graph(g))
  NetworkXError: Self loop found in _is_complete_graph()

  >>> fnx.complete_to_chordal_graph(g)
  (Graph(nodes=4, edges=7), {0: 0, 1: 0, 2: 0, 3: 0})
  >>> nx.complete_to_chordal_graph(nx.Graph(g))
  NetworkXError: Self loop found in _is_complete_graph()

The drift is selective — nx's _is_complete_graph helper is only called when the algorithm reaches a candidate complete subgraph that happens to contain a self-loop. For triangle + self-loop or chain + self-loop, neither algorithm reaches that branch and both nx and fnx return True / a valid result.

Drop-in code that does pytest.raises(NetworkXError) on the K4+self-loop case won't trigger on fnx. fnx's behavior is more permissive but breaks exact-error parity with nx.

Fix: when the input has any self-loop, delegate is_chordal and complete_to_chordal_graph to nx so the exact raise pattern is preserved.

title: 5+ node-lookup paths raise wrong exception on unhashable input
description:
Follow-up to br-r37-c1-m0io3 / -g438p. Several node-lookup paths still raise the wrong exception class on unhashable inputs — drop-in code that does pytest.raises(TypeError) won't trigger:

  g.neighbors([1,2])    fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  g.adj[[1,2]]          fnx: KeyError         nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  g.nodes[[1,2]]        fnx: KeyError         nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  g[[1,2]]              fnx: KeyError         nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  g.remove_edge([1,2], 2)  fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  g.remove_nodes_from([[1,2]])  fnx: silently skips  nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  descendants(g, [1,2])  fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError(unhashable)
  ancestors(g, [1,2])    fnx: NetworkXError    nx: TypeError(unhashable)

For the dict-getitem cases (adj/nodes/g[n]), nx's underlying dict access raises TypeError because Python's dict tries hash() first. fnx's wrappers catch KeyError-like cases and translate to KeyError, masking the real type issue.

For neighbors/remove_edge/descendants, fnx falls through to a 'node not in graph' check which silently returns False on unhashable, then raises NetworkXError instead of TypeError.

Skip is_isolate (nx itself has a leaky abstraction — accepts unhashable list as nbunch and returns False; matching this is undesirable).

Fix: call hash(node) at the entry of each affected wrapper before any membership check or dict access.

title: Refactor 12 filter/utility funcs out of NX_DELEGATED (hide_*, show_*, no_filter, frozen)
description:
10 filter functions (hide_nodes, hide_edges, hide_diedges, hide_multiedges, hide_multidiedges, show_nodes, show_edges, show_diedges, show_multiedges, show_multidiedges) plus no_filter and frozen all directly import nx and call into nx.classes.filters or nx.classes.function. They classify as NX_DELEGATED.

Per project convention, refactor to use private _xxx_via_nx helpers so the public functions are PY_WRAPPER. Drops NX_DELEGATED count by 12 (40 → 28).
close_reason: done

title: bellman_ford_path_length error message says 'Negative cost cycle' instead of nx's 'Negative cycle'
description:
fnx.bellman_ford_path_length raises NetworkXUnbounded('Negative cost cycle detected.') for negative-cycle inputs. nx uses 'Negative cycle detected.' (no 'cost'). Drop-in code that does pytest.raises(NetworkXUnbounded, match='Negative cycle') matches nx but NOT fnx.

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.DiGraph()
  >>> for u, v, w in [(1,2,1),(2,3,-3),(3,1,-1)]: g.add_edge(u, v, weight=w)
  >>> fnx.bellman_ford_path_length(g, 1, 3, weight='weight')
  NetworkXUnbounded: Negative cost cycle detected.    # nx: 'Negative cycle detected.'

Sister bead br-r37-c1-wtjho fixed bellman_ford_path. The same defect remains in bellman_ford_path_length: the Rust _raw_bellman_ford_path_length raises with 'Negative cost cycle' wording but the Python wrapper at __init__.py:9879 doesn't catch & re-raise with nx's wording.

Fix: catch NetworkXUnbounded in bellman_ford_path_length and re-raise with 'Negative cycle detected.', mirroring the pattern in bellman_ford_path.

title: 12 classic graph generators have adj-iteration order drift from nx (Rust fast paths)
description:
Same pattern as br-r37-c1-o97vk (cycle_graph/wheel_graph): the _rust_* fast paths for many classic graph generators produce adj orderings that don't match nx. The pure-Python construction path for each (used when create_using is set) already matches nx — fix by routing through the Python path always.

Generators that drift (verified via probe):
  * Named (no args): dodecahedral_graph, heawood_graph, frucht_graph,
    sedgewick_maze_graph, truncated_cube_graph,
    truncated_tetrahedron_graph, house_x_graph
  * Parameterised: ladder_graph, circular_ladder_graph,
    circulant_graph(n, offsets), lollipop_graph(m, n),
    barbell_graph(m1, m2)

Repro examples:
  lollipop_graph(3, 4) adj[3]: fnx=[2,4], nx=[4,2]
  barbell_graph(3, 4) adj[3]: fnx=[2,4], nx=[4,2]
  circulant_graph(7,[1,2]) adj[0]: fnx=[1,2,5,6], nx=[6,5,1,2]
  ladder_graph(4) adj[4]: fnx=[0,5], nx=[5,0]
  dodecahedral_graph adj[0]: fnx=[1,10,19], nx=[1,19,10]

Drop-in code that iterated adj[node] of any of these generator-built graphs silently broke (BFS/DFS-strategy tie-breaking, edge-traversal ordering, layouts, visualisations). Fix is the same as the cycle/wheel_graph fix: drop the Rust fast path branch and always use the Python construction.
close_reason: done

title: parity: G.nodes(data=...) returns NodeView (or list), nx returns NodeDataView
description: fnx Graph.nodes(data=True) returns the rust NodeView (class 'NodeView') whereas nx returns a 'NodeDataView'. fnx Graph.nodes(data='attr') returns a Python list whereas nx returns a 'NodeDataView'. Both deviate from nx's class-name contract — drop-in code doing isinstance(v, nx.classes.reportviews.NodeDataView) fails. The list case also breaks live-view semantics (mutations after the view is captured are not reflected). Same root cause and same fix as br-r37-c1-sf1ku (EdgeDataView): introduce a NodeDataView wrapper that re-invokes node_view_call on each access (iter/len/contains/repr) with class name aligned to nx.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 298400a. G.nodes(data=True) and G.nodes(data='attr') now return a live NodeDataView wrapper that re-fetches on each access, matching nx's contract. 13 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: cycle_basis emission/cycle-node order drifts from nx
description:
fnx.cycle_basis emits cycle node lists in canonical (sorted) order while nx yields cycles in DFS-discovery order through the chord-completion path.

Repro:
  g.add_edge('a','b'); g.add_edge('b','c'); g.add_edge('c','a')  # via the converter to nx the back-edge is (a,c)
  fnx.cycle_basis(g) -> [['a','b','c']]
  nx.cycle_basis(gx) -> [['b','a','c']]

Drop-in code that iterates cycles in nx's algorithmic order breaks. Fix: delegate cycle_basis to nx (mirrors articulation_points/bridges/biconnected_component_edges).
close_reason: done

title: parity: draw_bipartite signature divergence — fnx requires top_nodes positionally, nx accepts only **kwargs
description: fnx.draw_bipartite has signature (G, top_nodes, pos=None, **kwds) where top_nodes is a required positional arg, but networkx.drawing.nx_pylab.draw_bipartite has (G, **kwargs). Drop-in calls like nx.draw_bipartite(G) work in nx but fnx raises TypeError missing positional argument top_nodes. Fix: align signature to (G, **kwargs) and pull top_nodes out of kwargs (or compute it via bipartite_sets if absent), so fnx accepts the same call shape as nx.
close_reason: Shipped in 097c007: draw_bipartite now (G, **kwargs) matching nx; top_nodes pulled from kwargs and inferred via bipartite_sets if absent. 170 smoke tests pass.

title: trophic_levels raises on empty graph but test_empty_graph expects {}
description:
fnx.trophic_levels delegates to nx for all inputs after the directed check. nx raises NetworkXError('This graph has no basal nodes') on empty input. But the existing test_trophic_parity.py::TestTrophicLevels::test_empty_graph asserts fnx.trophic_levels(empty_DG) == {} — the project's intent is to be more permissive than nx on this edge case (empty graph trivially has no trophic levels to compute, returning empty dict is user-friendly).

Repro:
  fnx.trophic_levels(fnx.DiGraph()) -> NetworkXError (delegates to nx)
  Test expects: == {}

Pre-existing test failure. Fix: short-circuit empty-graph case to return {} before delegation. Companion functions trophic_differences / trophic_incoherence_parameter should be similarly examined.
close_reason: done

title: [reality-check] add nx.io.write_edgelist
description:
fnx 6.49ms vs nx 0.66ms (10x slower) on BA200. Same pattern as write_adjlist — Python str serialization. Port nx.readwrite.edgelist.write_edgelist.

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
close_reason: Default simple Graph/DiGraph write_edgelist now routes through the Rust PyO3 writer, emitting NetworkX-compatible dict-repr edge data while multigraph and non-default kwargs still delegate to NetworkX. Added fast-path/delegate-path tests. Verified RCH cargo test -p fnx-python (24 passed), RCH cargo build -p fnx-python, RCH cargo build -p fnx-python --release, RCH cargo clippy -p fnx-python --all-targets -- -D warnings, pytest tests/python/test_io.py via freshly built cdylib (31 passed), UBS critical=0, and BA200 BytesIO timing fnx 0.126ms vs nx 0.587ms (4.66x faster).

title: Missing-source/target parity: ssbf_path/_length wording, all_shortest_paths target class, bfs_layers missing-src silent
parity: NodeView/DegreeView reprs stringify node IDs; AtlasView/AdjacencyView reprs uninformative
description: Several view-class __repr__ implementations diverge from nx: (1) NodeView repr converts node IDs to strings — repr(G.nodes) on path_graph(3) returns NodeView(('0', '1', '2')) instead of nx's NodeView((0, 1, 2)). The actual stored values ARE integers; only the repr lies. (2) DegreeView has the same stringification bug — DegreeView([('0', 1), ('1', 2), ...]) vs nx's DegreeView({0: 1, 1: 2, ...}). (3) EdgeView repr shows count format 'EdgeView(2 edges)' vs nx's 'EdgeView([(0, 1), (1, 2)])'. (4) AtlasView/AdjacencyView reprs fall through to default '<franken_networkx.AtlasView object at 0x...>' instead of nx's AtlasView({1: {}}). Drop-in debugging output, repr-snapshot tests, and any tooling that compares __repr__ output break. Fix: override __repr__ at class level from Python on NodeView, DegreeView, EdgeView, AtlasView, AdjacencyView.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 1431c3b. NodeView/DegreeView reprs now show original-typed node IDs; EdgeView reprs show actual edge tuples with per-graph-type prefixes (EdgeView/OutEdgeView/MultiEdgeView/OutMultiEdgeView); AtlasView/AdjacencyView reprs show the underlying mapping. 13 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: triangles dict iterates in sorted-key order, nx iterates in node-insertion order
description: fnx.triangles returns a dict with keys sorted (e.g. for edges added in (2,3),(0,1),(1,2),(3,4) order: [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]) regardless of node-insertion order. nx returns the dict in node-insertion order ([2, 3, 0, 1, 4]). Same iteration-order family fixed in br-r37-c1-pm78h (communicability_betweenness_centrality), br-r37-c1-f6epo (second_order_centrality), and br-r37-c1-9fa26 (core_number). Drop-in code that relies on iteration order matching nx breaks. Fix: reorder result dict in iteration order of G.nodes().
close_reason: Fixed in commit df1fca1. triangles now iterates in node-insertion order matching nx. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: local_node_connectivity broken (TypeError on all calls) + single_source_dijkstra/bellman_ford/multi_source missing-node message wording drift
[parity-gap] graph_clique_number exists in fnx but was removed from nx (3.6+)
description:
fnx.graph_clique_number returns int (the size of the maximum clique). nx 3.6.1 removed this function entirely — accessing nx.graph_clique_number raises AttributeError because the deprecation period ended. The function was relocated/deprecated in favour of computing it inline as max(len(c) for c in nx.find_cliques(G)).

Repro:
  >>> import networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
  >>> nx.__version__
  '3.6.1'
  >>> hasattr(nx, 'graph_clique_number')
  False
  >>> fnx.graph_clique_number(fnx.complete_graph(4))
  4

Drop-in code that does try: nx.graph_clique_number except AttributeError: ... (defensive deprecation handling) won't trigger the except branch on fnx.

Three resolution options:
  1. Keep with a DeprecationWarning, mirroring nx's deprecation policy
  2. Remove entirely, matching strict-parity contract
  3. Leave as fnx-extension (current state)

Recommendation: option 1 — emit DeprecationWarning on call so users get migration nudge, then remove in a future release.

Audit-found 2026-05-03 review-mode pass.
close_reason: Shipped. fnx.graph_clique_number now emits DeprecationWarning matching nx's deprecation policy (nx 3.6 removed it). Backwards-compat preserved; migration path documented. 220 clique tests pass.

title: [code-review] pagerank fast path ignores max_iter non-convergence
description: [HIGH] fnx.pagerank uses the Rust fast path for simple non-negative graphs but pagerank_with_weight always returns a PageRankResult after the iteration loop, even when no convergence criterion was met. Repro: fnx.pagerank(fnx.path_graph(3), max_iter=0) returns uniform scores, while networkx.pagerank(nx.path_graph(3), max_iter=0) raises PowerIterationFailedConvergence. Similarly a directed graph with max_iter=1 and tol=1e-20 returns partial scores in fnx but raises in NetworkX. Fix should propagate convergence state from the Rust PageRank implementation to the PyO3 wrapper and raise PowerIterationFailedConvergence(max_iter) on exhaustion.
close_reason: Fixed convergence check

title: test_stochastic_block_model_default_uses_native_fast_path asserts wrong contract since br-sbmrng
description:
Pre-existing failure in test_sbm_generators.py::test_stochastic_block_model_default_uses_native_fast_path: asserts that fnx.stochastic_block_model calls _rust_stochastic_block_model (the native Rust fast path).
