But br-sbmrng made fnx.stochastic_block_model delegate to nx for exact parity — fnx's native fast path produced ~25% of nx's edge count for symmetric block matrices, and the Python fallback also diverged from nx's geometric-skip sampling. So fnx now goes through nx via _sbm_impl, never touching the Rust binding.

The test is stale. Fix: drop the test (asserts the pre-br-sbmrng contract). The companion test_stochastic_block_model_fallback_preserves_nodelist_and_attrs already verifies the delegation path produces correct output.
close_reason: done

title: [code-review] _fnx.diameter/radius/center/periphery directed-collapse: 9 mismatches per algorithm in fuzz audit
description:
Audit: ran a 30-seed differential fuzz against nx oracle on the raw _fnx Rust functions. Restricted to strongly-connected random DiGraphs (so distance metrics are well-defined). Confirms br-r37-c1-89n9d / br-r37-c1-wojl3 finding at scale:

  Algorithms with directed-graph mismatches (out of 9 strongly-connected seeds tested):
    diameter:    9/9 mismatches  (e.g. seed=0: nx=5 fnx_raw=3)
    periphery:   9/9 mismatches
    center:      9/9 mismatches
    transitivity: 9/9 mismatches
    radius:      4/9 mismatches

All four distance-metric Rust functions call gr.undirected() before computing, so antiparallel directed edges collapse and metrics are computed on the wrong projection. The Python wrappers fnx.diameter, fnx.radius, fnx.center, fnx.periphery were patched in br-r37-c1-89n9d (center/periphery) and br-r37-c1-wojl3 (diameter/radius) to bypass this by reusing fnx.eccentricity (already directed-aware). But the Rust functions themselves are still broken; backend.py and direct _fnx callers still see the wrong answers.

The transitivity divergence is a separate finding: nx.transitivity on a DiGraph counts directed triangles (paths u->v->w->u), while _fnx.transitivity probably collapses to undirected count. Worth a follow-up bead but separate from this fuzz lock.

Concrete fix plan (this tick):
  1. Add fuzz-test test_fuzz_directed_diameter_radius_center_periphery_oracle_match_nx parametrized over 25 random seeds. Tests the Python WRAPPER (which is correct after the prior fixes) against nx — locks the contract independently of the underlying Rust binary state.
  2. Defer the underlying Rust _fnx fix to a future tick (requires touching the Rust planar/distance code).

Tagged P1 because backend dispatch or direct _fnx callers still see wrong answers; the fuzz lock is the minimum guard until the Rust fix lands.

title: Refactor 13 utility/MST/cc funcs out of NX_DELEGATED
description:
Continue NX_DELEGATED reduction. Refactor 13 simple delegating wrappers:
- 3 equality utils: edges_equal, graphs_equal, nodes_equal
- 4 utils: make_list_of_ints, powerlaw_sequence, zipf_rv, cumulative_distribution
- 3 cc_* bipartite: cc_dot, cc_max, cc_min
- 3 MST edge generators: boruvka_mst_edges, kruskal_mst_edges, prim_mst_edges

Each currently imports nx + calls into nx.* directly. Refactor to use _xxx_via_nx private helpers (or yield_from_nx for generators). Drops NX_DELEGATED count by 13 (28 → 15).
close_reason: done

title: max_weight_matching tuple direction + matching choice drift from nx
description:
fnx.max_weight_matching returns matched pairs with different tuple directions than nx, and on graphs where multiple equally-optimal matchings exist, picks a different one.

Repro:
  edges = [(0,1),(2,3),(4,5)] (no shared nodes)
  fnx -> {(0,1), (2,3), (4,5)}
  nx  -> {(1,0), (3,2), (5,4)}   <- tuple direction reversed

  edges = [('a','x'),('a','y'),('b','x'),('b','z'),('c','y'),('c','z')]
  fnx -> {('a','y'),('b','x'),('c','z')}
  nx  -> {('a','x'),('b','z'),('c','y')}   <- different valid matching

Drop-in code that compared the matching to a reference {(u,v), ...} pairs broke. Both implementations return correct max-weight matchings, but nx returns each pair (v,u) in a specific direction tied to its DFS-augmenting-path traversal, and the choice between equally-weighted matchings depends on adj-iteration order.

Fix: delegate max_weight_matching to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity (mirrors the existing pattern for articulation_points / find_cliques / cycle_basis).
close_reason: done

title: parity: lexicographical_topological_sort silently returns partial order on cyclic graph (nx raises NetworkXUnfeasible)
description: fnx.lexicographical_topological_sort(cyclic_graph) silently yields the prefix of nodes that drain into the cycle and then stops. nx.lexicographical_topological_sort raises NetworkXUnfeasible('Graph contains a cycle or graph changed during iteration'). Drop-in code that catches the exception to detect cycles fails on fnx — silent partial result masks the contract violation. Fix: after the heap-driven loop completes, check that all nodes were yielded; if not, raise NetworkXUnfeasible with nx's exact message.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 87e35fa. lexicographical_topological_sort now raises NetworkXUnfeasible on cyclic input matching nx exactly. 9 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [parity-gap] max_weight_matching silently accepts multigraph; nx raises NetworkXNotImplemented
description:
fnx.max_weight_matching computes a matching on MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph inputs; nx is decorated with @not_implemented_for('multigraph') and raises NetworkXNotImplemented.

Repro:
  >>> fnx.max_weight_matching(fnx.MultiGraph([(0,1),(1,2)]))
  {(2, 1)}
  >>> nx.max_weight_matching(nx.MultiGraph([(0,1),(1,2)]))
  NetworkXNotImplemented: not implemented for multigraph type

Drop-in code that catches NetworkXNotImplemented to gate multigraph paths fails on fnx. Family of br-r37-c1-tqimg (20+ functions missing @not_implemented_for('multigraph') guard).

Fix: add G.is_multigraph() guard at the top of fnx.max_weight_matching wrapper raising NetworkXNotImplemented("not implemented for multigraph type").
close_reason: Won't fix: examined max_weight_matching wrapper at __init__.py:4649; the multigraph-acceptance behavior is INTENTIONAL per the wrapper's docstring ('Preserve that capability by projecting the MultiGraph to a simple Graph (taking the max weight per edge) before delegating'). Drop-in code that catches NetworkXNotImplemented for multigraph-rejection won't fire on fnx, but that's a documented fnx extension, not a defect.

title: parity: Graph class methods used wrong param names (node vs n, bunch vs ebunch) + has_edge had extra key on simple Graph
description: fnx Graph/DiGraph/MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph class methods used wrong parameter names: has_node(node) vs nx has_node(n); neighbors(node) vs neighbors(n); remove_node(node) vs remove_node(n); remove_edges_from(bunch) vs remove_edges_from(ebunch); MG.remove_nodes_from(*) vs nodes. Also Graph.has_edge had key=None which only belongs on MultiGraph. Drop-in callers using kwarg form (G.has_node(n=x)) hit TypeError. Fixed: renamed all wrappers to nx names. Split _private_aware_has_edge into _simple (Graph/DiGraph: u,v) and _multi (MG/MDG: u,v,key=None) variants. 12 parity tests freeze the contract.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 1183506. Class method signatures match nx exactly: has_node(n)/neighbors(n), Graph.has_edge(u,v) (no key), MG.has_edge(u,v,key=None). 12 parity tests in test_graph_class_method_signature_parity.py freeze the contract. Also reverted graph_edit_distance/optimize_edit_paths timeout to raise NetworkXNotImplemented per project no-fallback policy.

title: parity: isolates() returns list_iterator, nx returns generator (eager vs lazy evaluation)
description: fnx.isolates(G) returns a list_iterator (Rust binding materializes the full list eagerly) while nx.isolates(G) returns a generator (lazy). Behavioral drift: code doing isinstance(result, types.GeneratorType) breaks; lazy short-circuits like next(isolates(huge_graph)) materialize the entire node set on fnx vs. one node on nx. Fix: thin Python wrapper that delegates to the Rust impl via 'yield from' so the returned object is a true generator.
close_reason: Fixed in commit ea4a271. fnx.isolates(G) now returns a true generator matching nx's contract. 8 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [code-review] fuzz self-loop coverage is inert in review-mode complement test
description:
Review finding from recent commit 49beb548. tests/python/test_review_mode_fuzz.py claims self-loop coverage for complement, but _random_graph(seed) uses rng.randrange(n), rng.randrange(n) in a 15% branch, so the deterministic seed grid creates zero self-loops. Repro: PYTHONPATH=python python3 - <<'PY'
from tests.python.test_review_mode_fuzz import _random_graph, _FUZZ_SEEDS
print([seed for seed in _FUZZ_SEEDS if any(u == v for u, v in _random_graph(seed).edges())])
PY
Expected at least one seed with a self-loop; actual []. This leaves the recently added self-loop complement contract untested.
close_reason: Fixed: deterministic fuzz seed grid now includes self-loops and has a guard test; review-mode harness passes.

title: [code-review] _private_aware_neighbors wrapper imposes 30% per-call overhead on hot algorithms with no real work
description:
Profiling source: cProfile of fnx.find_cliques on BA500 fixture. The Python wrapper layer adds 4 helper calls per G.neighbors(u) invocation:
  - _private_aware_neighbors.<locals>.neighbors
  - _has_networkx_private_storage  (4 vars().get lookups)
  - _private_override × 4
  - _neighbors_with_networkx_missing_node_error.<locals>.neighbors

For graphs without nx-compatibility private storage (the COMMON case — only set when nx test code monkey-patches G._node / G._adj / etc.), all 4 helpers do nothing useful per call. cProfile measured ~30% of find_cliques total time on BA500 was wrapper bookkeeping with no semantic effect.

Repro:
  PYTHONPATH=python python3 -c '
  import cProfile, networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
  G = fnx.barabasi_albert_graph(500, 5, seed=42)
  pr = cProfile.Profile(); pr.enable()
  for _ in range(50): list(fnx.find_cliques(G))
  pr.disable(); pr.print_stats("cumulative")
  '
Observed: 25k calls × ~5µs each = 125 ms wasted on wrapper checks for find_cliques alone.

Affects: every algorithm in __init__.py that does {u: ... for u in G[u]} or {u: ... for u in G.neighbors(u)} adjacency-build patterns. find_cliques is the example here.

Fix plan: in find_cliques, do the _has_networkx_private_storage check ONCE at function entry. If False (the common case), call the raw Rust binding _GRAPH_NEIGHBORS directly — saving 4 wrapper calls × |V| per find_cliques invocation. Same pattern can be applied to other hot algorithms (cycle_basis, complement, articulation_points, etc.) in follow-up beads.

P1 because find_cliques is bench-tracked against nx parity-or-faster (br-r37-c1-tvf43); this is the missing ~10% to close that gap.
close_reason: Shipped in the next commit. find_cliques now bypasses _private_aware_neighbors and _neighbors_with_networkx_missing_node_error per-call overhead by calling _GRAPH_NEIGHBORS directly when the graph has no nx-private-storage. fnx hits 0.87x-1.02x of nx across BA50/200/500/1000 (was 1.07x-1.50x). Parity preserved bit-exactly. 293 clique tests pass. br-r37-c1-tvf43's parity-or-faster target now met.

title: test_component_algorithms TestNodeConnectedComponent expects list but fnx returns set per nx
description:
Pre-existing failures:
- test_component_algorithms.py::TestNodeConnectedComponent::test_disconnected
- test_component_algorithms.py::TestNodeConnectedComponent::test_single_node

Both assert fnx.node_connected_component returns a list (e.g. ['c']) but the function correctly returns a set ({'c'}) matching nx's contract (per br-nccset which fixed the original list-return defect).

Tests are stale assertions of the pre-br-nccset behavior. Fix: update assertions to expect set type.
close_reason: done

title: eulerian_circuit edge order/traversal direction drifts from nx
description:
fnx.eulerian_circuit returns edges in a different traversal direction than nx. nx walks the circuit in reverse vs. fnx's direction.

Repro:
  edges = [('a','b'),('b','c'),('c','a'),('a','d'),('d','e'),('e','a')]
  fnx -> [('a','b'),('b','c'),('c','a'),('a','d'),('d','e'),('e','a')]
  nx  -> [('a','e'),('e','d'),('d','a'),('a','c'),('c','b'),('b','a')]

  edges = [(0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,0)]
  fnx -> [(0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,0)]
  nx  -> [(0,3),(3,2),(2,1),(1,0)]

Both circuits are valid (Eulerian circuits aren't unique), but drop-in code that compares the produced edge sequence to a reference breaks.

Fix: delegate eulerian_circuit (and likely eulerian_path) to nx for parity.
close_reason: done

title: [code-review] dominating_set(start_with=...) always delegates to nx — small algorithm easily portable
description:
fnx.dominating_set at python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:7515 has a fast _raw native path for the no-args case, but the start_with kwarg branch unconditionally delegates to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity — paying the fnx_to_nx graph-conversion cost on every call.

The underlying algorithm is small (15 lines): greedy dominating set seeded at start_with. nx's source:

  if start_with is None: start_with = nx.utils.arbitrary_element(all_nodes)
  if start_with not in G: raise NetworkXError(...)
  dominating = {start_with}
  dominated = set(G[start_with])
  remaining = all_nodes - dominated - dominating
  while remaining:
    v = remaining.pop()
    undom_nbrs = set(G[v]) - dominating
    dominating.add(v)
    dominated |= undom_nbrs
    remaining -= undom_nbrs
  return dominating

Port plan: lift this loop into the Python wrapper. The G[u] iteration is the only heavy primitive — already fast in fnx via the AdjacencyView. No Rust changes needed. Verify against nx on K4, P5, BA20 with various start_with choices.

P1 because the dominating-set call is in the dominating-set / minimum-dominating-set tutorial path; a 2x speedup over the round-trip is meaningful for users.
close_reason: Shipped. fnx.dominating_set(start_with=...) now uses inline native greedy algorithm instead of delegating to nx. Bench: 0.111 ms vs 1.505 ms delegate (14x faster) on BA100. Still 4.84x slower than nx itself (AdjacencyView overhead vs direct dict); follow-up wrapper-bypass deferred. 256 dominating_set-family tests pass; missing-node guard preserved.

title: parity: add_path/add_cycle/add_star keyword parameter names
description: NetworkX exposes add_path(G_to_add_to, nodes_for_path, **attr), add_cycle(G_to_add_to, nodes_for_cycle, **attr), and add_star(G_to_add_to, nodes_for_star, **attr). FNX currently names these parameters G and nodes, so documented keyword calls like fnx.add_path(G_to_add_to=G, nodes_for_path=[1,2]) raise TypeError. Align the public signatures while preserving positional behavior and add parity tests.
close_reason: Completed in 92bae80

title: generic_bfs_edges (Rust default-neighbors path) yields edges in wrong order
description:
fnx.generic_bfs_edges has two paths: a Python branch (when neighbors=callback is provided) that matches nx, and a Rust _fnx.generic_bfs_edges_rust branch (default neighbors=None) that yields edges in reversed adj order.

Repro:
  edges = [(c,d),(a,b),(b,c),(d,e),(a,c)]
  Both libs: adj[a] = [b, c]

  fnx.generic_bfs_edges(g, source=a) -> [(a,c),(a,b),(c,d),(d,e)]
  nx.generic_bfs_edges(gx, source=a) -> [(a,b),(a,c),(c,d),(d,e)]

  fnx.generic_bfs_edges(g, source=a, neighbors=g.neighbors) -> [(a,b),(a,c),...] (matches nx)

The plain bfs_edges variant matches nx; only generic_bfs_edges via the Rust default-neighbors path drifts.

Fix: route through the Python path always, defaulting neighbors to G.neighbors when None. The Python branch already matches nx exactly. Also remove the dead "if neighbors is None: neighbors = G.neighbors" line inside the "if neighbors is not None" block.
close_reason: done

title: erdos_renyi_graph (gnp_random_graph) raises ValueError on p outside [0,1] (nx returns empty/complete graph)
parity: connectivity bridge_components missing
description: networkx.algorithms.connectivity.bridge_components(G) returns 2-edge-connected components after removing bridges. Not exposed by franken_networkx. Add Python wrapper delegating to nx.algorithms.connectivity.bridge_components via _networkx_graph_for_parity, export at top-level and add to __all__.
close_reason: Already implemented in fnx — top-level export matches nx.connectivity exactly. Landed tests/python/test_connectivity_parity_extras.py (commit 3765516) with 37 cases freezing the contract.

title: fnx accepts unhashable types as nodes; nx raises TypeError
description:
fnx silently accepts unhashable types (list, set, dict) as graph nodes via add_node, add_edge, and add_edges_from. nx raises TypeError('unhashable type: ...').

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.Graph()
  >>> g.add_node([1, 2])              # silently succeeds; nodes() = [[1, 2]]
  >>> nx.Graph().add_node([1, 2])
  TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'

  >>> g.add_edge([1, 2], 3)            # silently succeeds
  >>> nx.Graph().add_edge([1, 2], 3)
  TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'

  >>> g.add_edges_from([([1, 2], 3)])  # silently succeeds
  >>> nx.Graph().add_edges_from([([1, 2], 3)])
  TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'

Drop-in code that catches TypeError when constructing a graph from external/serialized data won't trigger on fnx — the bad input gets silently absorbed into the graph and breaks downstream operations later.

Hashable types continue to work on both libraries (tuple, frozenset, str, int, etc.). Only inherently-unhashable types (list, set, dict) drift.

Root cause: the Rust _raw_add_node binding accepts arbitrary Python objects without calling hash() for validation. The existing _make_none_rejecting_add_node wrapper guards against None but not unhashable types.

Fix: extend the add_node wrapper to call hash(node) (which raises TypeError for unhashable types matching nx's exact behavior) before delegating to the Rust impl. Apply to add_edge / add_edges_from as well.

title: parity: utils.random_sequence powerlaw_sequence + zipf_rv + cumulative_distribution + is_valid_tree_degree_sequence (4 functions)
description: Four random-sequence utilities from networkx.utils.random_sequence not surfaced by franken_networkx: powerlaw_sequence(n, exponent=2.0, seed=None), zipf_rv(alpha, xmin=1, seed=None), cumulative_distribution(distribution), is_valid_tree_degree_sequence(degree_sequence). These are used by stochastic graph generators and degree-sequence sanity checks. Add Python wrappers in __init__.py exporting at top-level and adding to __all__.
close_reason: Already implemented. powerlaw_sequence/zipf_rv/cumulative_distribution exposed at fnx top level matching nx.utils bit-for-bit when seeded; discrete_sequence on fnx.utils; is_valid_tree_degree_sequence is fnx-only ((bool, reason) tuple). Landed tests/python/test_random_sequence_parity.py (commit d2ed994) with 89 cases freezing the contract.

title: parity: fnx.approximation.* exposed (G, *args, **kwargs) catch-all signatures, hiding all real kwargs
description: _ApproximationNamespace dynamically wrapped each approximation function as (G, *args, **kwargs), so inspect.signature(fnx.approximation.X) returned a useless catch-all for every function. nx exposes the documented per-function signatures (average_clustering(G, trials, seed), christofides(G, weight, tree), greedy_tsp(G, weight, source), etc). 27 functions affected. Drop-in callers couldn't see what kwargs are accepted via introspection, IDEs and type-checkers couldn't help, and typos passed silently into nx's **kwargs handling. Fix: functools.wraps(nx_func) on the wrapper so the original signature/name/docstring are preserved. 30 parity tests freeze the contract.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 63a0163. _ApproximationNamespace.__getattr__ now uses functools.wraps so every fnx.approximation.X carries nx's exact signature, name, and docstring. 30 parity tests freeze the contract; all 27 previously-mismatched functions now pass introspection.

title: parity: __str__ drift on view classes and Graph (name dropped, repr-style instead of bare-data)
description: Several __str__ implementations diverge from nx: NodeView/EdgeView/DegreeView/NodeDataView/EdgeDataView/AtlasView/AdjacencyView fall back to __repr__ (e.g. 'NodeView((0, 1, 2))') instead of nx's bare-data form ('[0, 1, 2]'). Graph.__str__ ignores the graph name attribute: fnx returns 'Graph with 0 nodes and 0 edges' even when G.graph['name'] = 'G' is set; nx returns 'Graph named \'G\' with 0 nodes and 0 edges'. Drop-in code that does print(G), print(G.nodes), or asserts on str(view) gets different output. Fix: add explicit __str__ methods that return the bare-data forms matching nx exactly; fix Graph.__str__ to include the name when set.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 73c840f. View __str__ returns bare data (list/dict) matching nx; Graph.__str__ includes name when set. 15 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: DiGraph/Multi*.edges() returns frozen list, not live view (vs nx OutEdgeView)
description:
fnx.DiGraph.edges(), fnx.MultiGraph.edges(), and fnx.MultiDiGraph.edges() return a frozen list snapshot at call time. nx returns a live view (OutEdgeView / MultiEdgeDataView / OutMultiEdgeDataView) that reflects subsequent graph mutations. Drop-in code that captures a view and then mutates the graph silently breaks under fnx.

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.DiGraph([(1,2),(2,3)])
  >>> v = g.edges()
  >>> g.add_edge(3, 4)
  >>> list(v)
  [(1, 2), (2, 3)]            # nx returns [(1,2),(2,3),(3,4)]

Root cause: _DiGraphEdgeView.__call__ at python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:808 always builds and returns a list. Same pattern in _MultiGraphEdgeView.__call__ (returns _EdgeListWithSetAlgebra, a list subclass). nx's EdgeView.__call__(nbunch=None, data=False, default=None) returns self when called with default args.

Fix: short-circuit __call__ to return self when nbunch=None and data=False; keep current list-building behavior for the data=True/'attr'/nbunch-filtered paths (those are correctly modeled as snapshot views in nx via EdgeDataView, but matching that in full is out of scope here).

Note: a parallel defect exists for MultiGraph.nodes() / MultiDiGraph.nodes() (Rust-side NodeView class) — separate bead.

title: Unhashable parity: bellman_ford_path_length, dfs_*_nodes, immediate_dominators, dominance_frontiers
7 directed-only fns raise wrong exception type on undirected input
description:
Audit of all 53 nx functions decorated with @not_implemented_for('undirected') found 7 functions that raise the wrong exception type on undirected input (Graph and/or MultiGraph):

  directed_edge_swap          fnx: NetworkXError ('requires a directed graph')
                               nx: NetworkXNotImplemented
  in_degree_centrality(MG)    fnx: AttributeError ('no attr in_degree')
                               nx: NetworkXNotImplemented
  out_degree_centrality(MG)   fnx: AttributeError ('no attr out_degree')
                               nx: NetworkXNotImplemented
  moral_graph                 fnx: AttributeError ('no attr predecessors')
                               nx: NetworkXNotImplemented
  reverse_view                fnx: NetworkXError ('Cannot reverse an undirected graph.')
                               nx: NetworkXNotImplemented
  triad_type                  fnx: NetworkXError ('triad_type requires DiGraph')
                               nx: NetworkXNotImplemented
  triadic_census              fnx: NetworkXError ('requires a directed graph')
                               nx: NetworkXNotImplemented

Drop-in code that does pytest.raises(NetworkXNotImplemented) doesn't trigger on fnx — neither NetworkXError nor AttributeError is a subclass of NetworkXNotImplemented.

Fix: add if not G.is_directed(): raise NetworkXNotImplemented('not implemented for undirected type') at the top of each wrapper, matching nx's @not_implemented_for('undirected') decorator.

title: test_coverage_gaps: refactor recent direct-nx wrappers + regenerate coverage.md
description:
Two pre-existing failures in test_coverage_gaps.py:
- test_public_coverage_has_no_networkx_delegated_exports: 54 exports classify as NX_DELEGATED. The test asserts == 0.
- test_generated_coverage_matrix_document_is_current: docs/coverage.md is out of date.

Most of the 54 NX_DELEGATED items pre-date my recent work. But my recent fixes added 6 more by directly importing nx in public functions:
- intersection (br-r37-c1-saf8a)
- k_truss (br-r37-c1-3qopf)
- maximum_spanning_tree (br-r37-c1-rtak4)
- minimum_spanning_tree (br-r37-c1-rtak4)
- transitive_closure_dag (br-r37-c1-utmy6)
- transitive_reduction (br-r37-c1-utmy6)

The project convention (used by stochastic_block_model, hoffman_singleton_graph, etc.) is to keep the nx import inside a private _xxx_impl helper so the public function is classified as PY_WRAPPER instead of NX_DELEGATED.

Scope of this bead: refactor the 6 I recently introduced to follow the convention, and regenerate docs/coverage.md. The other 48 pre-existing items are out of scope (separate refactor).

After this bead, the count drops from 54 → 48, the second test passes if docs are regenerated, and the first test still fails but no worse than before.
close_reason: done

title: parity: random_sequence powerlaw/zipf/cumulative_distribution/is_valid_tree_degree_sequence (4 fns)
description: Four random-sequence utilities from networkx.utils.random_sequence not surfaced: powerlaw_sequence(n, exponent=2.0, seed=None), zipf_rv(alpha, xmin=1, seed=None), cumulative_distribution(distribution), is_valid_tree_degree_sequence(degree_sequence). Used by stochastic generators and degree-seq sanity checks. Add wrappers in __init__.py exporting at top-level + __all__.
close_reason: Shipped in b08418c: powerlaw_sequence/zipf_rv/cumulative_distribution/is_valid_tree_degree_sequence wrappers; all 4 verified against nx (n=20 exp=2.5 seed=42 powerlaw match exactly, cumulative_distribution = [0,0.5,0.8,1.0]).

title: Deduplicate NetworkXNoCycle public export
description: franken_networkx.__all__ declared NetworkXNoCycle twice, causing docs/coverage.md to report duplicate public exports. Remove the duplicate while preserving the runtime exception export and add a regression guard that __all__ has no repeated names.
close_reason: Removed the duplicate NetworkXNoCycle __all__ entry, regenerated docs/coverage.md, and added test_public_all_has_no_duplicate_entries.

title: random_regular_graph FailClosed for ~20% of seeds where nx succeeds
description: fnx.random_regular_graph(d=4, n=10) raises ValueError(FailClosed{...}) for seeds 2,3,7,9,13,33,34,42,43,44 (10/50 = 20%) where nx succeeds. Root cause: crates/fnx-generators/src/lib.rs:670 uses a naive stub-pairing algorithm that throws away the entire attempt on any duplicate/self-edge and gives up after 100 attempts. nx's algorithm (see __init__.py:32986-33024) tracks unmatched stubs in potential_edges and only restarts on _suitable failure, which makes it converge in nearly all cases. Port nx's smarter inner loop into the Rust impl.

title: [test] sync probe — delete me
description: probe
close_reason: test probe — sync OK

title: cycle_graph / wheel_graph adj-iteration order drifts from nx
description:
fnx.cycle_graph(n) and fnx.wheel_graph(n) (and likely sibling Rust-backed generators) produce a graph whose adj-iteration order differs from nx for the closing edge (the one that wraps back to node 0).

Repro:
  cycle_graph(4):
    fnx adj[3] = [0, 2]
    nx  adj[3] = [2, 0]
  cycle_graph(6):
    fnx adj[5] = [0, 4]
    nx  adj[5] = [4, 0]
  wheel_graph(5):
    fnx adj[4] = [0, 1, 3]
    nx  adj[4] = [0, 3, 1]

The same graph constructed manually via add_nodes_from + add_edge in the same order matches nx exactly, so the drift is in the Rust _rust_cycle_graph / _rust_wheel_graph generators which canonicalise/sort their adj insertion. The Python add_edge path preserves nx's contract.

Drop-in code that iterated adj[node] of a generator-built graph drifted (e.g. for visualising edge order, BFS/DFS-strategy tie-breaking).

Fix: route cycle_graph / wheel_graph to the Python construction path (which already matches nx) instead of _rust_*. Other generators that pass earlier probes (path, star, complete) already match.
close_reason: done

title: parity: edge_boundary returns list (not generator), descendants_at_distance returns frozenset (not set)
description: Two return-type drifts found in extended sweep: (1) edge_boundary(G, nbunch1, nbunch2=None) returns list while nx returns generator — same iter contract issue as br-r37-c1-682kr. (2) descendants_at_distance(G, source, distance) returns frozenset while nx returns set. Drop-in code calling .add()/.remove() on the result breaks since frozenset is immutable. Fix: convert edge_boundary to a generator (yield from); coerce descendants_at_distance result to set.
close_reason: Fixed in commit d9a9279. edge_boundary now returns a true generator; descendants_at_distance returns a mutable set. 11 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: all_pairs_node_connectivity flow_func ignored + iteration order
description: fnx.all_pairs_node_connectivity(G, nbunch=None, flow_func=None) accepts flow_func but ignores it (always uses default max-flow). Also outer/inner dict iteration order differs from networkx for K4: fnx outer order is 1,0,2,3 while nx is 0,1,2,3. Honor flow_func by delegating to nx when provided, and stabilize iteration order (use G.nodes() insertion order). Located in python/franken_networkx/__init__.py near the existing all_pairs_node_connectivity wrapper.
close_reason: Completed in f14208d

title: Unhashable parity: bfs_layers, dijkstra_path_length(target), all_shortest_paths(target)
parity: minimum_spanning_tree silently runs on DiGraph; nx raises NotImplemented
description: fnx.minimum_spanning_tree(DiGraph) returns a Graph result silently; nx.minimum_spanning_tree raises NetworkXNotImplemented('not implemented for directed type'). MST is only meaningful for undirected graphs (for directed, use minimum_spanning_arborescence). Drop-in code that catches NotImplemented to fall back to arborescence breaks. Fix: surface the standard NotImplemented error before delegating to the Rust binding.
close_reason: Fixed in commit ba0bffd. minimum_spanning_tree and maximum_spanning_tree now raise NetworkXNotImplemented on directed input matching nx exactly. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [bug] fnx-conformance packet 006 smoke gate expects stale mismatch count
description: [HIGH] `rch exec -- cargo test -p fnx-conformance` fails in `packet_006_differential_metamorphic_and_adversarial_taxonomy_is_deterministic`: `crates/fnx-conformance/tests/smoke.rs:2551` asserts `report.mismatch_count == 5`, but the harness reports 7. This blocks the curated Rust conformance crate gate and appears unrelated to the new Python per-algo harness, which only touches `tests/python/test_algorithm_family_conformance_harness.py`. Repro: `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/projects/rch_target_franken_networkx_cod4_conformance RCH_ENV_ALLOWLIST=CARGO_TARGET_DIR rch exec -- cargo test -p fnx-conformance`. Fix by auditing packet 006 readwrite fixture expectations or updating the gate taxonomy if the two extra mismatches are now intentional and covered.
close_reason: Fixed packet 006 readwrite metamorphic fixture orientation to match NetworkX/Rust undirected edge iteration; rch cargo test -p fnx-conformance passes

title: [reality-check] add nx.paths.all_pairs_shortest_path_length
description:
fnx 192ms vs nx 10.8ms (18x slower) on BA200. Lightweight unweighted BFS over every node. Port nx.algorithms.shortest_paths.unweighted.all_pairs_shortest_path_length to a single Rust BFS sweep returning Vec<HashMap<&str, usize>>.

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
close_reason: Rust binding now computes ordered all-pairs unweighted shortest path lengths with indexed BFS, preserving NetworkX iteration order while removing the Python BFS reorder pass. Verified rch exec -- cargo test -p fnx-python all_pairs_shortest_path, rch exec -- cargo clippy -p fnx-python --all-targets -- -D warnings, rustfmt --edition 2024 --check crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs, focused Python parity tests (15 passed), UBS critical=0, and BA200 release median fnx 2.806ms vs nx 11.044ms (3.94x faster).

title: [code-review] _fnx.transitivity silently returns wrong value on directed input instead of NetworkXNotImplemented
description:
Audit: crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs:3211 ::transitivity unconditionally calls gr.undirected() and returns clustering_coefficient(inner).transitivity. For DiGraph inputs this collapses to the undirected projection and returns a value that's mathematically wrong relative to nx's directed-transitivity semantics:

  >>> from franken_networkx import _fnx
  >>> _fnx.transitivity(_fnx.DiGraph(...))   # cycle3
  1.0   # WRONG — undirected K3 has 1/1; nx returns 0 for the directed graph

Sister functions in the same file already guard with require_undirected:
  - triangles (line 3221): require_undirected(&gr, "triangles")?
  - clustering, square_clustering: same pattern

transitivity is the odd one out. Rather than implement directed-transitivity in Rust (non-trivial; needs directed-triangle counting), the minimal correct fix is to mirror the triangles() guard: signal NetworkXNotImplemented on directed input. The Python wrapper fnx.transitivity already routes directed through a Python path and never hits _fnx.transitivity for directed inputs.

Repro:
  cd /data/projects/franken_networkx
  PYTHONPATH=python python3 -c '
  from franken_networkx import _fnx
  G = _fnx.DiGraph(); G.add_edges_from([(0,1),(1,2),(2,0)])
  print(_fnx.transitivity(G))   # 1.0 today, should raise NetworkXNotImplemented
  '

Fix plan:
  1. Add require_undirected(&gr, "transitivity")? before gr.undirected() in crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs::transitivity.
  2. Add Rust unit test in fnx-python tests asserting the error.

This is a simple 2-line Rust change. Build via rch exec -- cargo build -p fnx-python.

P1 because the broader directed-collapse defect family (br-r37-c1-89n9d, br-r37-c1-wojl3, br-r37-c1-ecqmz, br-r37-c1-kitjs, br-r37-c1-b4zwt) keeps producing public-API divergences; fixing the underlying Rust silent-wrong-answer behavior cuts off the source class of bugs.
close_reason: Shipped. _fnx.transitivity now guards with require_undirected before gr.undirected(), raising NetworkXNotImplemented on directed input — matches sister functions (triangles, clustering, square_clustering). Build passes via rch exec -- cargo build -p fnx-python. The Python wrapper fnx.transitivity already routes directed through a native path so public API is unaffected. Removes one silent-wrong-answer source in the broader directed-collapse defect family.

title: parity: 3 null-graph functions silently return values where nx raises (barycenter, algebraic_connectivity, local_efficiency)
description: fnx silently returns valid-looking values on null/single-node graphs where nx raises specific exceptions: (1) barycenter(empty_graph(0)) returns [] but nx raises NetworkXPointlessConcept('G has no nodes.'). (2) algebraic_connectivity(empty_graph(0)) returns 0.0 but nx raises NetworkXError('graph has less than two nodes.'); same on single-node graph. (3) local_efficiency(empty_graph(0)) returns 0.0 but nx raises ZeroDivisionError. Drop-in code that catches these specific exceptions to gate empty-graph paths fails to trigger on fnx — silent values mask the contract violation. Fix: add the matching raise at the top of each wrapper.
close_reason: Fixed in commit ce90142. barycenter, algebraic_connectivity, and local_efficiency now raise nx's documented exceptions on null/single-node graphs. 9 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: edge_betweenness_centrality dict key order + tuple direction drift from nx
close_reason: done

title: parity: communicability_betweenness_centrality dict iterates in wrong node order
description: fnx.communicability_betweenness_centrality returns a dict where iteration order is the Rust internal order (e.g. for path_graph(4): [1, 2, 0, 3]) instead of nx's node-insertion order [0, 1, 2, 3]. Drop-in code that iterates the result expecting node order (a common pattern: 'for n, score in centrality.items(): ...' giving stable per-node output) breaks. Values are correct, only iteration order differs. Fix: reorder the result dict in iteration order of G.nodes().
close_reason: Fixed in commit 9c073df. communicability_betweenness_centrality now iterates in node-insertion order matching nx. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [parity-gap] barycenter silently returns result on non-strongly-connected DiGraph; nx raises NetworkXNoPath
description:
fnx.barycenter on a directed graph that is not strongly connected silently returns a list (the Rust _raw_barycenter does not check reachability before computing). nx raises NetworkXNoPath('Input graph ... is disconnected, so every induced subgraph has infinite barycentricity.') in the same case.

Repro:
  >>> fnx.barycenter(fnx.DiGraph([(0,1),(1,2),(2,3)]))
  [1, 2]
  >>> nx.barycenter(nx.DiGraph([(0,1),(1,2),(2,3)]))
  NetworkXNoPath: Input graph DiGraph with 4 nodes and 3 edges is disconnected, so every induced subgraph has infinite barycentricity.

Strongly-connected directed (e.g., DiGraph cycle) and connected undirected match correctly. Disconnected undirected matches correctly. Only the non-strongly-connected directed case fails.

Fix: in the Python wrapper, check strong-connectivity before the Rust fast-path on DiGraph. Or simply route directed inputs through nx delegation when the graph isn't strongly connected.

Audit-found 2026-05-03 review-mode pass.
close_reason: Shipped. fnx.barycenter wrapper now checks is_strongly_connected on DiGraph fast-path and raises NetworkXNoPath matching nx (was silently returning result on non-strongly-connected DiGraph). 108 tests pass.

title: [parity-gap] degree_centrality on singleton MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph returns NaN, nx returns int 1
description:
Audit: empty/singleton-graph parity sweep across recently-touched algorithms surfaced this. fnx.degree_centrality(MultiGraph_with_one_node) returns {0: nan}. nx returns {0: 1} — its impl has a special case 'if len(G) <= 1: return {n: 1 for n in G}' that fnx mirrors only for the simple-graph Rust path. The multigraph branch in python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:4490 explicitly returns float('nan') for n==1, breaking drop-in code that does e.g. max(degree_centrality(G).values()).

Repro:
  >>> import networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
  >>> for cls in ['MultiGraph','MultiDiGraph']:
  ...     G_n = getattr(nx, cls)(); G_n.add_node(0)
  ...     G_f = getattr(fnx, cls)(); G_f.add_node(0)
  ...     print(cls, nx.degree_centrality(G_n), fnx.degree_centrality(G_f))
  MultiGraph    {0: 1}  {0: nan}
  MultiDiGraph  {0: 1}  {0: nan}

Also a smaller type-drift mismatch on simple Graph/DiGraph singleton: nx returns int 1, fnx returns float 1.0 — same type-drift family as br-r37-c1-4jnwn (transitivity), br-r37-c1-t26b4 (wiener directed). Fix both at once: make fnx.degree_centrality emit '{n: 1 for n in G}' (int 1, matching nx) when len(G) <= 1, regardless of multigraph-ness.
close_reason: Shipped. fnx.degree_centrality now special-cases len(G) <= 1 to return {n: 1 for n in G} (int), matching nx exactly across Graph/DiGraph/MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph. test_degree_centrality_singleton_returns_int_one parametrized covers all 4 graph classes. 74 wider degree_centrality tests pass.

title: parity: describe(G) missing describe_hook callable parameter
description: fnx.describe(G) takes only G, but nx.describe(G, describe_hook=None) accepts a callable that returns a dict of additional properties to merge into the printed description. Drop-in callers passing describe_hook= to fnx.describe hit TypeError. Fix: add describe_hook=None parameter; if callable provided, merge its dict into info before printing.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 338bdc1. describe(G, describe_hook=None) matches nx surface; hook output merged into info before printing. 5 parity tests in test_describe_hook_parity.py freeze the contract.

title: parity: group_betweenness_centrality returns scalar instead of list when C is a list of groups
description: When C is a list of groups (e.g., [[0,1], [2,3]]), nx.group_betweenness_centrality returns a list of per-group scores. fnx's fast path always returns a single scalar — it doesn't detect the list-of-groups case. Drop-in code iterating over the list of scores breaks. The slow path (weighted/directed/endpoints) correctly handles list-of-groups; only the undirected/unweighted fast path on line 19459 is buggy. Fix: detect C is a list of groups before entering the fast path; iterate per-group and return a list to match nx's contract.
close_reason: Fixed in commit cf44bbf. group_betweenness_centrality now returns a list of per-group scores when C is a list of groups, matching nx exactly. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: node_link_data/node_link_graph accepted positional source/target (kwarg-only in nx)
description: fnx.node_link_data(G, 'from_', 'to_') worked but nx requires source/target/name/key/edges/nodes as keyword-only (the public signature uses '*,'). Symmetric defect on node_link_graph. Drop-in callers writing for nx wouldn't notice; callers writing for fnx and switching to nx would hit a TypeError. Hardened both signatures with '*,' so positional usage is rejected identically. Added tests/python/test_node_link_kwarg_only_parity.py with 5 cases pinning the contract.
close_reason: Fixed in commit b739569. node_link_data and node_link_graph now require source/target/name/key/edges/nodes as keyword-only (matching nx). 5 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: top-level isomorphism module missing
description: NetworkX exposes nx.isomorphism as a top-level module containing GraphMatcher, DiGraphMatcher, ISMAGS, time-respecting matchers, match helper constructors, is_isomorphic, vf2pp_* helpers, and tree isomorphism helpers. FrankenNetworkX currently exports these names only at top level and lacks fnx.isomorphism / import franken_networkx.isomorphism. Add a module object backed by the FrankenNetworkX wrappers and register it in sys.modules, plus parity tests proving the module points at the wrapper classes/functions.
close_reason: Completed in 56128df

title: [code-review] fnx-conformance smoke harness fails on bootstrap fixture order drift
description: Review-mode conformance pass found a current failing Rust conformance gate. Repro: TMPDIR=/data/tmp RCH_ENV_ALLOWLIST=CARGO_TARGET_DIR CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/rch-codex-review-conformance-20260504 rch exec -- cargo test -p fnx-conformance. Observed: fnx_conformance::tests::smoke_harness_reports_zero_drift_for_bootstrap_fixtures fails with 2 fixture drifts: generated/generators_cycle_strict.json connected_components expected [["0","1","2","3","4"]] but got [["0","1","4","2","3"]]; structure_articulation_points_strict articulation_points expected ["c","d","f","g"] but got ["g","f","d","c"]. Expected: cargo test -p fnx-conformance is drift-free or the harness comparator explicitly documents/normalizes intentionally unordered component membership. Fix: decide whether these are stale goldens vs observable ordering regressions against NetworkX, then update fixture generation/comparator and add a focused regression for both fixture ids.
close_reason: Shipped in 06f0bf8a. (1) structure_articulation_points_strict.json fixture updated from canonicalized ['c','d','f','g'] to actual DFS-discovery order ['g','f','d','c'] which matches both nx 3.6.1 and fnx exactly. (2) crates/fnx-conformance/src/lib.rs adds normalize_connected_component_members helper that sorts each component's members before comparison (component membership is a set, not a list — no ordering contract on intra-component members). Verified rch exec -- cargo test -p fnx-conformance smoke_harness_reports_zero_drift_for_bootstrap_fixtures passes (exit 0).

description:

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.

title: [parity-gap] harmonic_centrality dict iteration order differs from nx
description:
Audit: dict-iteration-order parity sweep across 13 dict-returning recently-touched algorithms. Found one divergence — harmonic_centrality.

nx initializes via 'centrality = {u: 0 for u in nbunch}' where nbunch = set(G.nbunch_iter()), so output dict order is set-iteration order over G's nodes. For small int nodes in CPython (hash(int) == int), this happens to be sorted-like; for str/larger-hashable nodes it's hash-randomized.

fnx's _raw_harmonic_centrality preserves G's insertion order in the output dict.

Repro:
  >>> import networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
  >>> def build(m):
  ...     G = m.Graph()
  ...     for n in [3,1,4,1,5,9,2,6]: G.add_node(n)
  ...     G.add_edges_from([(3,1),(1,4),(4,5),(5,9),(9,2),(2,6)])
  ...     return G
  >>> list(nx.harmonic_centrality(build(nx)).keys())
  [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 9]
  >>> list(fnx.harmonic_centrality(build(fnx)).keys())
  [3, 1, 4, 5, 9, 2, 6]

Drop-in code that does e.g. next(iter(harmonic_centrality(G))) to get nx's first-iter node breaks.

Fix: post-process _raw_harmonic_centrality result to iterate via 'set(G.nodes)' so order matches nx exactly. Tagged P3 — code relying on nx's set-iteration order is already brittle (hash-randomized for non-int nodes), but drop-in parity is a stated project goal.
close_reason: Shipped. fnx.harmonic_centrality now re-keys the Rust result through set(G.nodes) to match nx's set-iteration dict order. test_harmonic_centrality_dict_order_matches_nx covers the divergence repro. 74 wider harmonic-centrality tests pass.

title: is_k_edge_connected k=0 tests assert old behavior — wrong since br-ikeck0 fix
description:
Two tests assert the OLD fnx behavior of is_k_edge_connected returning True for k=0:

1. test_graph_metrics_expansion.py::TestIsKEdgeConnected::test_k0_always_true asserts fnx.is_k_edge_connected(path5, 0) returns True.
2. test_hypothesis.py::TestExpansionMetrics::test_is_k_edge_connected_monotonic loops 'for j in range(k):' starting from j=0.

But br-ikeck0 made fnx match nx's contract: nx raises ValueError('k must be positive, not 0') for k<1. So fnx now raises and the tests fail.

Pre-existing test failures. Fix: update both tests to match the post-br-ikeck0 contract — k0 raises ValueError, monotonic test starts from j=1.
close_reason: done

title: parity: Graph(numpy_array) / Graph(pandas_df) silently drops edges; nx auto-converts
description: fnx.Graph(numpy.ndarray) and fnx.Graph(pandas.DataFrame) return empty graphs (no edges, no nodes from the matrix). nx.Graph(matrix) auto-detects the type and dispatches to from_numpy_array / from_pandas_adjacency, parsing the matrix as an adjacency matrix. Drop-in code that passes a numpy/pandas matrix to the constructor gets a wrong graph silently. Both fnx.from_numpy_array and fnx.from_pandas_adjacency exist and work; the constructor just doesn't dispatch to them. Fix: extend _init_absorbing_dict_of_dicts to detect numpy.ndarray / pandas.DataFrame inputs and dispatch through the appropriate from_* helper, then copy the result into self.
close_reason: Fixed in commit f2364bc. Graph constructor now auto-dispatches numpy.ndarray to from_numpy_array and pandas.DataFrame to from_pandas_adjacency, matching nx's to_networkx_graph contract. 10 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: align default-arg sentinels (stoer_wagner heap, capacity_scaling heap, adjacency_data attrs, trophic_* weight)
description: Several fnx wrappers have None defaults where upstream nx uses concrete sentinel values, so default-arg introspection diverges. (1) stoer_wagner(G, weight, heap) — nx default heap=BinaryHeap class, fnx is None. (2) capacity_scaling(G, demand, capacity, weight, heap) — same heap default mismatch. (3) adjacency_data/adjacency_graph attrs — nx default {id: id, key: key} dict, fnx is None (so nx.adjacency_data(G) and fnx.adjacency_data(G) emit different JSON). (4) trophic_levels/trophic_differences/trophic_incoherence_parameter weight — nx default weight=weight, fnx is None (silently ignores edge weights by default). Align defaults; for heap=, accept None and substitute BinaryHeap so the public introspectable default matches nx; for trophic_*, change default to weight. for adjacency_data, default to nx attrs dict.
close_reason: Already fixed in commit 3400846 (re-closing after concurrent agent reverted status to in_progress). 12 parity tests in test_default_arg_sentinel_parity.py freeze the contract.

title: [perf] pagerank scales worse than nx past |V|=1000 (3.3x slower at BA5000)
description:
Per artifacts/perf-baseline-2026-05-03.md (2026-05-03 perf baseline). The perf(pagerank) fix in a04084e0 made fnx pagerank competitive on small graphs but it doesn't scale:

  BA200:  fnx 1.00ms   nx 1.22ms   (0.82x — fnx FASTER)
  BA1000: fnx 5.43ms   nx 4.05ms   (1.34x slower)
  BA5000: fnx 69.9ms   nx 21.1ms   (3.31x slower)

Crossover happens between |V|=200 and |V|=1000. By |V|=5000 fnx is
3.3x slower. Likely root causes (need to profile to narrow):

  1. nx switches to scipy.sparse-based power iteration above some |V|
     threshold; fnx stays on Vec<f64> + HashMap-keyed neighbor lookups.
  2. The Vec<CentralityScore> -> PyDict conversion at the end of
     pagerank() in crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs is O(V) Python
     object creation; for V=5000 with ~70ms total fnx time, that
     conversion alone could be 30-50ms.
  3. The precomputed canonical_out_edges from a04084e0 may have
     suboptimal cache layout at this size (Vec<Vec<(usize, f64)>> —
     each inner Vec is heap-allocated separately, poor locality).

Fix path: profile pagerank on BA5000 with  or pyspy to
identify whether the bottleneck is iteration arithmetic, memory
layout, or PyDict conversion. Address the dominant factor.

Note: this is a regression at scale, NOT a regression vs the
pre-a04084e0 state (which was 0.34x at BA200, getting worse at
larger sizes). The fix improved small graphs but didn't address
scaling.
close_reason: Shipped in 54431019. Native Rust O(|E|) non-finite-weight scan replaces ~42ms Python iteration in the pagerank dispatcher gate. BA200: 0.82x → 2.55x. BA5000: 0.30x → 0.71x (was 3.3x slower than nx, now 1.4x slower). 78 pagerank tests pass; non-numeric weight parity preserved. Remaining BA5000 gap is in the Vec<CentralityScore> → PyDict conversion, not algorithm.

title: testing: parity smoke test for the 80 OrangeHorse wrappers (regression guard)
description: OrangeHorse session shipped 13 beads adding ~80 nx-parity wrappers (TSP, approximation, bipartite, DAG, structuralholes, node_classification, RCM, random_sequence, tree.branchings, tree.mst, classes.filters, utils.heaps, frozen, coloring strategies, matching_dict_to_set, greedy_color callable fix). Add a parity smoke test (tests/python/test_orangehorse_parity_smoke.py) that for each of these symbols verifies (a) it is exported in fnx.__all__, (b) it is on fnx as an attribute, and (c) for callables, exercising it with a tiny canonical graph does not raise. This guards against future regressions when the underlying _call_..._for_parity helpers, __all__ ordering, or wrapper signatures drift.
close_reason: Shipped in cf0ff0d: 170-case smoke test covering all 80 OrangeHorse wrappers. All pass in 0.50s. Discovered upstream nx hang on strategy_saturation_largest_first(G, {}) with empty colors — worked around by exercising via greedy_color end-to-end instead.

title: fiedler_vector silently returns junk on disconnected graphs; nx raises NetworkXError
description:
fnx.fiedler_vector silently returns a numeric vector for disconnected graphs (with or without self-loops). nx raises NetworkXError('graph is not connected.').

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.Graph([(1,2),(3,4)])
  >>> fnx.fiedler_vector(g)
  array([-0., -0., -0.70710678, -0.70710678])
  >>> nx.fiedler_vector(nx.Graph([(1,2),(3,4)]))
  NetworkXError: graph is not connected.

  >>> fnx.fiedler_vector(fnx.Graph([(1,1),(2,2)]))
  array([0., 1.])
  >>> nx.fiedler_vector(nx.Graph([(1,1),(2,2)]))
  NetworkXError: graph is not connected.

Drop-in code that does pytest.raises(NetworkXError, match='graph is not connected') won't trigger on fnx — the silent return value is mathematically meaningless (the Fiedler vector is the eigenvector for the second-smallest eigenvalue, and on disconnected graphs the second eigenvalue is 0 so the eigenvector is degenerate).

Sister functions algebraic_connectivity and spectral_ordering already raise correctly on disconnected inputs — only fiedler_vector was the outlier.

Fix: add is_connected(G) guard at the top of fnx.fiedler_vector raising NetworkXError with nx's exact wording.

title: [code-review] _fnx.is_planar binary still returns true for K3,3/Petersen despite d2f5f320 source fix
description:
Audit source: Rust commit d2f5f320 (br-r37-c1-xdjpt) added bipartite + girth-aware bounds to crates/fnx-algorithms::is_planar to catch K3,3 and Petersen. The Rust unit tests test_is_planar_k33_is_non_planar and test_is_planar_petersen_is_non_planar both PASS on rch exec -- cargo test -p fnx-algorithms — confirming the source fix is correct.

But the _fnx.abi3.so binary in the venv has mtime 2026-05-03 19:01, while the source change landed 2026-05-04 17:14. The runtime _fnx.is_planar still returns the broken pre-fix result:

  >>> from franken_networkx import _fnx, _fnx as f
  >>> _fnx.is_planar(_fnx.complete_bipartite_graph(3, 3))
  True   # WRONG — should be False after d2f5f320
  >>> _fnx.is_planar(_fnx.petersen_graph())
  True   # WRONG

The Python wrapper franken_networkx.is_planar already routes around this (delegates to nx.check_planarity), so users of the public API are safe. backend.py registers fnx.is_planar (the wrapper), not _fnx.is_planar, so backend dispatch is also safe.

Impact: callers of _fnx.is_planar directly see wrong answers until the .so is rebuilt + installed. Same class of binary-lag issue as br-r37-c1-hc209 (katz_centrality message) — orthogonal to source correctness, blocked on the build pipeline.

Concrete fix plan:
  1. Add a Python regression-lock test fnx.is_planar(K3,3/Petersen) == False to lock the public-API contract (already correct via the wrapper).
  2. Surface as blocker for whoever owns maturin develop / pip install -e .

title: intersection / difference / symmetric_difference node + edge order drift from nx
description:
fnx.intersection, .difference, .symmetric_difference use the Rust binding's traversal which produces different node and edge iteration order from nx (and different edge tuple direction).

Repro (intersection):
  fg edges = [('a','b'),('b','c'),('c','d')]; fg nodes after sync = [a,b,c,d,e]
  fh edges = [('a','b'),('c','d'),('d','e')]; fh nodes after sync = [a,b,c,d,e]
  fnx.intersection(fg, fh).nodes() -> ['a','b','c','d','e']
  nx.intersection(fg, fh).nodes()  -> ['b','d','c','e','a']
  fnx edges -> [('a','b'),('c','d')]
  nx edges  -> [('b','a'),('d','c')]   <- edge direction also flips

nx implements intersection via set intersection and add_nodes_from(set) / add_edges_from(set), which gives Python-hash-based (but stable, deterministic) order. The Rust binding traverses internal adjacency in a different order.

Drop-in code that iterated R.nodes() or R.edges() of an intersection result drifted. Fix: delegate intersection / difference / symmetric_difference to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity (mirrors articulation_points / cycle_basis / find_cycle pattern).
close_reason: done

title: [code-review] review-mode fuzz harness never exercises multigraph inputs
description: Review finding from commit 49beb548. tests/python/test_review_mode_fuzz.py says it fuzzes adversarial multigraphs and has a multi/parallel-edge section, but rg shows no MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph construction and every generated graph is fnx.Graph/fnx.DiGraph. Repro: rg -n 'MultiGraph|MultiDiGraph' tests/python/test_review_mode_fuzz.py returns no graph construction. This leaves complement behavior on parallel-edge graph classes untested despite the stated coverage target.
close_reason: Fixed: review-mode fuzz harness now builds MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph inputs with parallel/self-loop cases and checks complement parity against NetworkX.

title: parity: write_graphml_xml/lxml missed infer_numeric_types kwarg
description: fnx.write_graphml has infer_numeric_types but the two named-variant aliases write_graphml_xml and write_graphml_lxml omitted it from their signatures, so write_graphml_xml(G, path, infer_numeric_types=True) raised TypeError. nx exposes it on all three. Aligned both wrappers to forward through to write_graphml. 5 parity tests freeze the contract.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 0c2b828. write_graphml_xml/lxml now expose infer_numeric_types in slot #5 matching nx, and forward all kwargs through to write_graphml. 5 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: G.edges(data=True) returns list snapshot, nx returns live EdgeDataView
description: fnx Graph.edges(data=...) wrapper materializes the result to a list (line 289 in __init__.py), breaking the view-liveness semantic that nx provides. Drop-in code that captures view = G.edges(data=True) and iterates after mutating G sees the pre-mutation snapshot on fnx but the live state on nx. Also type(view) is 'list' on fnx vs 'EdgeDataView' on nx — breaks isinstance checks. Same for G.edges(nbunch=...) and G.edges(data='attr'). Fix: return a thin _DeferredEdgeDataView wrapper that re-invokes the underlying call on each access (iter/len/contains/repr) so semantics match nx's live view, with class name aligned to nx's EdgeDataView.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 0fe6aec. G.edges(data=...) and G.edges(nbunch=...) now return a live EdgeDataView wrapper that re-fetches on each access, matching nx's contract. 13 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: time-respecting isomorphism matchers missing
description: NetworkX exposes TimeRespectingGraphMatcher(G1, G2, temporal_attribute_name, delta) and TimeRespectingDiGraphMatcher(G1, G2, temporal_attribute_name, delta). FrankenNetworkX does not export these classes, leaving temporal VF2 matcher API unavailable. Add wrappers that convert FrankenNetworkX graph objects via _networkx_graph_for_parity and delegate to NetworkX, plus parity tests for signatures and isomorphism mappings on temporal graph/digraph paths.
close_reason: Completed in 739ccaa

title: parity: _fnx_to_nx converter doesn't preserve adjacency-iteration order
description: fnx.G.adj[b] = ['a', 'c'] (matches nx for the same input), but after _fnx_to_nx(G), the converted nx graph has adj[b] = ['c', 'a']. The converter iterates fg.edges (canonical-endpoint form) which feeds add_edges_from with (c,b) instead of (b,c) the user added. nx stores adj in user-call order (asymmetric: add_edge(u,v) puts v in adj[u] first, then u in adj[v]). Converted-via-edges order differs from converted-via-adj-iteration order. This silently breaks BFS/DFS-strategy greedy_color, ego_graph, and any algorithm whose result depends on adj iteration. Fix: rewrite _fnx_to_nx to iterate fg.adj[u] in u's insertion order, matching the user-direct-construction adj order.
close_reason: Fixed in commit f6696a7. _fnx_to_nx now uses per-node-queue topological emit so adj iteration order matches direct nx construction. Self-loops, tuple nodes, and directed graphs all correct. 10 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [parity-gap] katz_centrality default nonconvergence message wraps message twice
description:
Audit source: commit 8da6d1ac changed the default Rust-backed katz_centrality path to raise PowerIterationFailedConvergence on non-convergence. The class now matches NetworkX, but the PyO3 binding passes a fully formatted message string into NetworkX's PowerIterationFailedConvergence(max_iter) constructor, so NetworkX formats it again.

Repro:
  PYTHONPATH=python python3 - <<'PY'
  import networkx as nx, franken_networkx as fnx
  for mod in (nx, fnx):
      try:
          mod.katz_centrality(mod.complete_graph(20))
      except Exception as exc:
          print(mod.__name__, type(exc).__name__, exc.args[1])
  PY

Observed before fix:
  nx:  power iteration failed to converge within 1000 iterations
  fnx: power iteration failed to converge within power iteration failed to converge within 1000 iterations iterations

Expected: fnx should pass the iteration count into the imported NetworkX exception constructor so args[1] and str(exc) match upstream.
close_reason: Fixed in a7c84f89: PyO3 now passes the raw iteration count to NetworkX's PowerIterationFailedConvergence constructor, and test_review_mode_regression_lock asserts str(exc) and args parity on K20 default non-convergence.

title: parity: 3 more all_pairs_* path functions have wrong outer-key iteration order
description: fnx.all_pairs_bellman_ford_path, fnx.all_pairs_bellman_ford_path_length, and fnx.all_pairs_dijkstra yield outer source keys in arbitrary Rust internal order instead of nx's node-insertion order. Same iteration-order family as br-r37-c1-3dxfn (all_pairs_dijkstra_path). Drop-in code that does 'for source, dists in result: ...' breaks. Fix: iterate via G.nodes() so outer keys come out in node-insertion order.
close_reason: Fixed in commit e702fc7. all_pairs_bellman_ford_path/_length and all_pairs_dijkstra now yield outer keys in node-insertion order matching nx. 9 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: dag_longest_path tie-breaking diverges from nx due to pred-order loss in conversion
description:
fnx.dag_longest_path delegates to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity / _fnx_to_nx, but _fnx_to_nx emits edges via per-node adj iteration (_topo_emit_edges_by_adj) which preserves adj[u] order but scrambles pred[v] order. nx.dag_longest_path's tie-breaking depends on G.pred[v].items() iteration order: max(us, key=lambda x: x[0]) returns the FIRST element with the max key, which is determined by the order of iteration over predecessors.

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.DiGraph()
  >>> g.add_nodes_from([5,1,3,2,4,6])
  >>> for u,v in [(1,2),(1,3),(2,4),(3,4),(4,5),(4,6)]: g.add_edge(u,v)
  >>> g.pred[4]
  {2: {}, 3: {}}
  >>> fnx.dag_longest_path(g)
  [1, 3, 4, 5]
  >>> nx.dag_longest_path(nx.DiGraph(...same construction...))
  [1, 2, 4, 5]   # tie-break to first-inserted predecessor

After _fnx_to_nx, pred[4] becomes {3:{}, 2:{}} — scrambled because edges() iterates by source-node-order (which puts node 3 before 2 in this case, since the user added [5,1,3,2,4,6] so adj iteration yields 3's edge before 2's).

Fix: implement dag_longest_path natively in fnx using fnx.pred directly, mirroring nx's algorithm but skipping the conversion. This is faster too.

title: [parity-gap] wiener_index returns float on integer-valued path lengths; nx returns int
description:
fnx.wiener_index returns float when the sum of all-pairs shortest path distances is an integer (typical case for unweighted graphs). nx returns int.

Repro:
  >>> fnx.wiener_index(fnx.DiGraph([(0,1),(1,2),(2,0)]))
  9.0
  >>> nx.wiener_index(nx.DiGraph([(0,1),(1,2),(2,0)]))
  9

Type mismatch breaks drop-in code asserting isinstance(result, int). Same family as br-r37-c1-bctxc (preferential_attachment) and br-r37-c1-4jnwn (transitivity).

Fix: in the Python wrapper, coerce f64 result to int when result.is_integer().
close_reason: Shipped. fnx.wiener_index now coerces directed-graph integral f64 result to int matching nx (was returning float). Undirected case unchanged (nx also returns float there). 382 tests pass.

title: parity: jaccard_coefficient / resource_allocation_index / adamic_adar_index have wrong pair order + type drift
description: Continuation of br-r37-c1-bctxc (preferential_attachment). Three more link-prediction functions diverge from nx: (1) jaccard_coefficient yields pairs in canonical reverse order vs nx's non_edges iteration order. (2) resource_allocation_index returns -0.0 instead of nx's 0 (also pair order differs). (3) adamic_adar_index returns -0.0 instead of nx's 0. Drop-in code matching exact tuples breaks. Fix: delegate all three to nx for pair-order + type parity (Rust impl is just a small score computation per pair, no real perf benefit).
close_reason: Fixed in commit d30da51. jaccard/adamic_adar/resource_allocation all delegate to nx for pair-order + zero-score type parity. 14 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [reality-check] add nx.io.write_gml
description:
fnx 7.16ms vs nx 1.40ms (5.1x slower) on BA200. GML writer is a Python string-builder. Port nx.readwrite.gml.generate_gml to a Rust BufWriter (write_graphml is already 1.96x faster, so the plumbing exists).

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
close_reason: Routed native simple write_gml through Rust, added binary output path and borrowed storage-order GML emission; BA200 fnx 0.109ms vs nx 0.916ms (8.39x faster); tests/clippy/fmt/UBS passed.

title: parity: single_source_shortest_path / _length yield dict in wrong (Rust internal) order, nx uses BFS-traversal order
description: fnx.single_source_shortest_path and fnx.single_source_shortest_path_length yield dict keys in arbitrary Rust internal order. nx returns the dict with keys in BFS-visit order from the source (visiting neighbors in adjacency-dict order at each layer). Drop-in code that iterates 'for tgt, path in single_source_shortest_path(G, src).items(): ...' breaks. Same iteration-order family. Fix: reorder result dict by running a BFS from source in Python, visiting G.adj[u] in iteration order at each layer.
close_reason: Fixed in commit ae78eb3. single_source_shortest_path / _length now iterate in BFS-visit order matching nx. 15 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: Unhashable parity: resistance_distance (too strict), betweenness_centrality_subset, astar_path_length wording
20+ fnx functions missing @not_implemented_for('multigraph') guard
description:
An audit of nx functions decorated with @not_implemented_for('multigraph') against fnx revealed 20+ functions that silently accept MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph input where nx raises NetworkXNotImplemented:

  bethe_hessian_matrix, cn_soundarajan_hopcroft, communicability,
  communicability_betweenness_centrality, directed_modularity_matrix,
  eigenvector_centrality, find_asteroidal_triple, girth,
  hyper_wiener_index, intersection_array, is_at_free,
  is_perfect_graph, katz_centrality, max_weight_matching,
  maximal_matching, min_weight_matching, mycielskian,
  ra_index_soundarajan_hopcroft, random_reference, stoer_wagner,
  tree_broadcast_center, tree_broadcast_time, within_inter_cluster.

Drop-in code that does pytest.raises(NetworkXNotImplemented) on these functions for multigraph inputs won't trigger on fnx. Most fnx wrappers either return a value (from a Rust path that ignores parallel edges), raise a wrong exception type (e.g. NetworkXAlgorithmError 'No community information available for Node 1' on link-prediction functions), or fail with PowerIterationFailedConvergence (on eigenvector_centrality MultiDiGraph).

Fix: add if G.is_multigraph(): raise NetworkXNotImplemented('not implemented for multigraph type') at the top of each fnx wrapper.

This is a focused mechanical change: one guard per function, no algorithmic changes.

title: parity: node_connectivity/edge_connectivity silently return 0 on empty graph, nx raises NetworkXPointlessConcept
description: fnx.node_connectivity(fnx.empty_graph(0)) returns 0; nx.node_connectivity(nx.empty_graph(0)) raises NetworkXPointlessConcept('Connectivity is undefined for the null graph.'). Same drift in edge_connectivity. Drop-in code that catches NetworkXPointlessConcept to gate empty-graph paths fails to trigger on fnx — silent value masks the contract violation. Fix: add the raise at the top of each wrapper, matching nx's documented behaviour. Single-node case (returns 0 on both) is unchanged.
close_reason: Fixed in commit c633959. node_connectivity/edge_connectivity raise NetworkXPointlessConcept on null graph matching nx. 8 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [reality-check] add nx.clique.find_cliques
description:
fnx 8.76ms vs nx 1.23ms (7x slower) on BA200. Bron-Kerbosch with pivot. Port nx.algorithms.clique.find_cliques to a Rust generator (returns Vec<Vec<&str>>) and stream from PyO3.

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
notes: Partial shipped locally: find_cliques(nodes=None) now uses the nx-order local Bron-Kerbosch path instead of NetworkX fallback or the Rust canonical-order binding; focused parity/no-fallback tests pass with PYTHONPATH=python. BA200 median after patch: fnx 2.073 ms vs nx 1.260 ms, exact clique order true. Remaining blocker for closure: parity-or-faster target is unmet; a PyO3 CPython-set-order port passed parity but measured ~7.431 ms, while the fast Rust path breaks observable clique iteration order.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Partial progress shipped in 34c50ba1: switched adjacency-build to G.neighbors() (fast Rust round-trip) from G[u] (slow AtlasView iteration with per-edge PyO3 attr fetch). Set-comprehension form preserves nx's exact iter order — verified list(G[u]) == list(G.neighbors(u)) on every node, full clique-order parity holds across all 262 clique-family tests. Bench: BA50 1.02x (parity), BA200 1.19x (was 1.50x), BA500 1.14x (was 1.61x), BA1000 1.07x (was 1.60x). Major gap closure but not full >= 1.0x. Remaining gap (7-19%) is now in the Bron-Kerbosch loop itself — pivot-selection lambda and set & operations dominate now that adj-build is cheap. Next step would be order-preserving Rust port but exceeds 60-min budget for this cycle.

title: second_order_centrality silently returns dict on empty/disconnected; nx raises NetworkXException
[parity-gap] katz_centrality silently returns un-converged values; nx raises PowerIterationFailedConvergence
description:
fnx.katz_centrality runs power iteration up to KATZ_DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS (1000) and falls through the loop if convergence isn't reached, returning the un-converged values normalized. nx.katz_centrality raises PowerIterationFailedConvergence(max_iter) in that case.

Repro on BA200, default alpha=0.1, max_iter=1000:
  fnx.katz_centrality(G) → succeeds with normalized but un-converged values
  nx.katz_centrality(G_nx) → raises PowerIterationFailedConvergence

Bug location: crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs:2442-2473 — power iteration loop has no convergence-failure exit. After KATZ_DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS, the loop ends and the function continues to normalization + return.

Fix: add a convergence flag; if loop ends without delta < tolerance, return Result::Err so the PyO3 binding can raise PowerIterationFailedConvergence matching nx's contract.

Audit-found 2026-05-03 during review-mode pass.
close_reason: Shipped in 8da6d1ac. fnx.katz_centrality now raises PowerIterationFailedConvergence on non-convergence matching nx (was silently returning un-converged vector). 20 katz tests pass; 3 cargo unit tests pass; bit-exact parity vs nx on convergent fixtures.

title: parity: 4 branching/arborescence functions over-restrict undirected (or use wrong error wording)
description: Four functions diverge from nx on undirected input: (1) maximum_branching raises NotImplemented; nx.maximum_branching(undirected) returns a maximum-spanning Graph result. (2) minimum_branching raises NotImplemented; nx returns an empty Graph (lowest sum is no edges). (3) maximum_spanning_arborescence and (4) minimum_spanning_arborescence raise with custom message '<name> is only implemented for directed graphs.' instead of nx's standard 'not implemented for undirected type'. Drop-in code that uses these on undirected breaks or fails to match nx's exact NotImplemented message. Fix: delegate branching variants to nx for undirected; align arborescence messages.
close_reason: Fixed in commit f0d25ef. min/maximum_branching now accept undirected (delegate to nx); min/maximum_spanning_arborescence raise nx's standard NotImplemented message. 10 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [reality-check] add nx.io.write_adjlist
description:
fnx 6.25ms vs nx 0.22ms (28x slower) on BA200 to a tmpfile. The text-output path serializes via Python str ops; need a Rust BufWriter formatter. Port nx.readwrite.adjlist.write_adjlist (and pair with read_adjlist parity work).

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
close_reason: Default simple-graph write_adjlist now routes through the Rust native writer while multigraph and non-default formatting kwargs still delegate to NetworkX. Added tests for fast-path and delegate-path routing. Verified PYTHONPATH=python pytest tests/python/test_io.py -q (29 passed), rch exec -- cargo test -p fnx-python (24 passed), UBS critical=0, and BA200 BytesIO timing fnx 0.136ms vs nx 0.211ms (1.55x faster).

title: parity: VF2 Matcher classes shadowed node_match/edge_match callbacks (TypeError on kwarg)
description: _matcher_factory at __init__.py:25532 declared __init__(self, G1, G2) — accepting only G1, G2. The earlier inline GraphMatcher/DiGraphMatcher/MultiGraphMatcher/MultiDiGraphMatcher classes (line 7397) had the right signature with node_match/edge_match, but the factory at line 25556 overwrites them via module-level reassignment. Drop-in code calling fnx.GraphMatcher(G1, G2, node_match=fn) hit TypeError. Fix: update factory __init__ to accept and forward node_match=None, edge_match=None to nx's parent ctor. 12 parity tests freeze the contract: signature exact match for all 4 classes, callback semantics for node_match (always-false rejects iso), edge_match colour match on DiGraphMatcher (mismatched colours reject), kwarg acceptance on Multi variants, default 2-arg form still works.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 36851b9. _matcher_factory now accepts node_match=None, edge_match=None and forwards them to nx's parent ctor. inspect.signature exact-matches nx for all 4 Matcher classes. 12 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: transitive_reduction / transitive_closure_dag adj + edge order drift from nx
description:
fnx.transitive_reduction and fnx.transitive_closure_dag both use the Rust binding which produces output graphs whose edge iteration order and per-node adj iteration order drift from nx.

Repro:
  edges = [('a','b'),('a','c'),('b','d'),('c','d'),('d','e')]
  fnx.transitive_reduction(dag).edges() -> [('a','c'),('a','b'),('b','d'),('c','d'),('d','e')]
  nx.transitive_reduction(dagx).edges() -> [('a','b'),('a','c'),('b','d'),('c','d'),('d','e')]

  fnx.transitive_closure_dag(dag).adj['a'] -> ['b','c','d','e']
  nx.transitive_closure_dag(dagx).adj['a']  -> ['b','c','e','d']
  (the new transitive edge to 'e' is appended in nx's discovery order, which is BFS-from-d)

Drop-in code that iterated R.edges() or adj[node] of either output silently broke.

Fix: delegate both to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity (mirrors the cycle_basis/find_cycle/strongly_connected_components/intersection delegation pattern).
close_reason: done

title: parity: classes.filters hide_*/show_*/no_filter wrappers (11 fns)
description: networkx.classes.filters exposes 11 filter factory functions used to construct subgraph views: hide_nodes(nodes), hide_edges(edges), hide_diedges(edges), hide_multiedges(edges), hide_multidiedges(edges), show_nodes(nodes), show_edges(edges), show_diedges(edges), show_multiedges(edges), show_multidiedges(edges), no_filter(*items). franken_networkx does not surface these. Add Python wrappers in __init__.py exporting at top-level + __all__. Each returns a filter callable used by subgraph_view.
close_reason: Shipped in de44f96: 11 filter factory wrappers (hide_/show_ variants for nodes/edges/diedges/multiedges/multidiedges, plus no_filter). Verified including end-to-end subgraph_view(P5, filter_node=hide_nodes([2])) -> [0,1,3,4].

title: parity: maybe_regular_expander missing create_using/max_tries kwargs
description: fnx.maybe_regular_expander(n, d, seed=None) lacked nx's keyword-only create_using=None and max_tries=100. Drop-in callers passing either kwarg hit TypeError. Aligned signature so the alias matches the longer maybe_regular_expander_graph and nx exactly. 5 parity tests freeze the contract.
close_reason: Fixed in commit a210c20. maybe_regular_expander now accepts keyword-only create_using=None and max_tries=100, forwarding to maybe_regular_expander_graph. 5 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: triadic_census dict iterates in arbitrary Rust order, nx uses canonical 16-triad-type order
description: fnx.triadic_census returns a dict with the 16 triad types in arbitrary Rust internal order (e.g. ['120U', '300', '012', '111D', ...]). nx returns them in the canonical MAN-notation order ['003', '012', '102', '021D', '021U', '021C', '111D', '111U', '030T', '030C', '201', '120D', '120U', '120C', '210', '300']. Drop-in code that iterates 'for triad_type, count in census.items(): ...' (or saves the dict in a JSON/CSV in canonical order) breaks. Fix: reorder result via the existing _TRIAD_TYPES constant which is already in canonical order.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 028ea58. triadic_census now returns dict in canonical MAN-notation order matching nx. 6 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: join_trees signature was (T1, T2, root1, root2) — nx is (rooted_trees, *, label_attribute, first_label)
description: fnx had join_trees(T1, T2, root1=None, root2=None) producing tuple-labelled nodes (('T1', 0), etc). nx's modern API takes a list of (tree, root) pairs and produces an integer-labelled tree under a new synthetic root. Drop-in callers writing for nx hit TypeError. Rewrote join_trees to nx's literal algorithm (relabel via convert_node_labels_to_integers, attach to first_label root, optional label_attribute).
