Verified isomorphism with nx on balanced_tree(2,4)+balanced_tree(2,4) -> balanced_tree(2,5), exact edge equality, label_attribute preservation, first_label offsetting, empty input. 7 parity tests freeze the contract.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 99c70f5. join_trees rewritten to match nx's modern API (rooted_trees, *, label_attribute, first_label). 7 parity tests freeze the contract incl. exact edge equality with nx and the balanced_tree(2,h) docstring example.

title: parity: greedy_color dict iteration order is Rust-internal, nx uses processing-order from strategy
description: fnx.greedy_color returns dict keys in arbitrary Rust internal order. nx returns them in the order the strategy processed them (e.g. for 'largest_first': sorted by degree descending then in some tiebreak). Drop-in code that iterates 'for node, color in greedy_color(G).items(): ...' breaks. Same iteration-order family. Fix: delegate to nx so the ordering is preserved exactly (Rust impl is for performance only; values match either way).
close_reason: Fixed in commit b9b495e. greedy_color now delegates to nx for processing-order match. 7 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: laplacian_matrix returns float64 instead of int64 for unweighted graphs
description:
fnx.laplacian_matrix returns a float64 sparse array even when the graph is unweighted (no edge weights). nx returns int64 in that case (matches the int dtype of nx.to_scipy_sparse_array on a weight-less graph).

Repro:
  >>> g = fnx.Graph([(1,2),(2,3)])
  >>> fnx.laplacian_matrix(g).toarray().dtype
  dtype('float64')
  >>> nx.laplacian_matrix(nx.Graph([(1,2),(2,3)])).toarray().dtype
  dtype('int64')

The values agree element-wise, but downstream code that sizes buffers based on dtype, exports to formats with int constraints (e.g., GraphBLAS bool/int variants), or just uses np.array_equal across libraries gets a divergence.

Root cause: python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:12674 forces dtype=float on the diags_array call:
    D = scipy.sparse.diags_array(
        np.asarray(A.sum(axis=1)).flatten(), dtype=float
    )

nx (lib/python/networkx/linalg/laplacianmatrix.py) uses dia_array without forcing dtype, so D inherits A's dtype.

Fix: drop the dtype= kwarg so D's dtype is inferred from the input vector, which itself derives from the sparse-array sum (same dtype as A).

title: parity: connectivity is_locally_k_edge_connected missing
description: networkx.algorithms.connectivity.is_locally_k_edge_connected(G, s, t, k) tests local k-edge-connectivity between s and t. Not exposed by franken_networkx. Add Python wrapper delegating to networkx and export at top-level.
close_reason: Already implemented in fnx — top-level export matches nx.connectivity exactly. Landed tests/python/test_connectivity_parity_extras.py (commit 3765516) with 37 cases freezing the contract.

title: edges nbunch with unhashable element raises TypeError vs nx NetworkXError
parity: Graph add_*/remove_* methods reject nx-canonical kwarg names (u_of_edge, node_for_adding, ebunch_to_add, n, nodes, ebunch)
description: All four Graph classes use fnx-internal positional arg names that differ from nx's documented kwarg names. Drop-in callers using the documented kwarg form hit TypeError: G.add_edge(u_of_edge=0, v_of_edge=1), G.add_node(node_for_adding=0), G.add_weighted_edges_from(ebunch_to_add=...), G.remove_node(n=0), G.remove_nodes_from(nodes=...), G.remove_edges_from(ebunch=...). MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph add_edge takes u_for_edge/v_for_edge per nx. Fix: thin Python wrappers that accept the nx-canonical names and forward to the rust impl. Add parity tests asserting both kwarg-form calls work and inspect.signature names match nx.
close_reason: Fixed in commit f748b59. All Graph class wrappers now use nx's documented kwarg names (u_of_edge/v_of_edge for simple add_edge, u_for_edge/v_for_edge for multi add_edge, node_for_adding, ebunch_to_add, n, nodes, ebunch). Drop-in callers using the documented kwarg form now work on fnx exactly like nx. Also added _wraps_without_signature_poisoning helper so to_directed/to_undirected error messages match nx's wording (Graph.to_undirected vs <locals>.to_undirected). 32 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [reality-check] add nx.clustering.clustering
description:
fnx 16.3ms vs nx 2.77ms (5.9x slower) on BA200. Bundles 4 algorithms: clustering, transitivity, average_clustering, generalized_degree (all share the same triangle-iteration core). Port a single fnx_algorithms::clustering_coefficient_all that returns per-node clustering + global transitivity + global average + generalized degree counts in one BFS-style scan.

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
close_reason: Shipped in 96b05f5c. Wrapper now uses Rust _raw_clustering/_raw_average_clustering/_raw_transitivity for undirected, no-weight, all-nodes case. BA1000: clustering 0.17x → 5.9x. BA5000: 0.14x → 4.3x. Bit-exact parity. 315 tests pass.

title: parity: tree.branchings + tree.mst edge iterators (5 fns)
description: Five tree-related utilities from networkx.algorithms.tree not surfaced. Branchings: branching_weight(G, attr=weight, default=1), minimal_branching(G, *, attr=weight, default=1, preserve_attrs=False, partition=None). MST edge iterators: boruvka_mst_edges, kruskal_mst_edges, prim_mst_edges (all signature G, minimum, weight=weight, keys=True, data=True, ignore_nan=False, partition optional). These give users access to the underlying MST/branching algorithm primitives. Add Python wrappers in __init__.py exporting at top-level + __all__.
close_reason: Shipped in 7cca063: branching_weight + minimal_branching + boruvka/kruskal/prim mst_edges iterators. All 5 verified on weighted K5 (MSTs match nx exactly) and 3-node DiGraph (branching weight + edge count match).

title: [parity-gap] diameter/radius delegate directed graphs to nx, _raw_* mishandles antiparallel directions
description:
Audit source: python/franken_networkx/__init__.py::diameter (line 5233) and ::radius (line 5266). Same defensive 'or G.is_directed(): _call_networkx_for_parity' guard as br-r37-c1-89n9d (center/periphery), but with an additional twist — the underlying Rust _raw_diameter / _raw_radius produce WRONG values on directed graphs because they call gr.undirected() before computing.

Repro:
  >>> from franken_networkx._fnx import diameter as raw_diam
  >>> import franken_networkx as fnx
  >>> G = fnx.DiGraph([(i,(i+1)%5) for i in range(5)])
  >>> raw_diam(G)
  2   # WRONG — should be 4 (length of the directed path around the cycle)
  >>> import networkx as nx
  >>> nx.diameter(nx.DiGraph([(i,(i+1)%5) for i in range(5)]))
  4

Same shape as harmonic_diameter's documented 'directed-collapse defect' (same family as eccentricity, is_eulerian, is_tree, core_number — all fixed previously). The Python wrapper currently masks this by routing directed graphs to nx, paying the fnx_to_nx graph-conversion cost on every directed-graph diameter/radius call.

Fix plan:
  1. For undirected (the original fast Rust path stays): _raw_diameter / _raw_radius.
  2. For directed: reuse fnx.eccentricity (already directed-aware, fast Rust path), then take max for diameter / min for radius.
  3. Other-args delegation (e=, usebounds, weight=) stays unchanged.

This avoids the fnx_to_nx graph-conversion overhead on every directed-graph call AND keeps the undirected fast path. Verified bit-exact against nx 3.6.1 on directed cycle5, directed K5, directed cycle3+chord — diameter and radius all match.

P1 because directed-graph callers of fnx.diameter / fnx.radius pay the round-trip cost on every call; affects every directed-graph user.
close_reason: Shipped. fnx.diameter and fnx.radius now natively handle directed graphs via fnx.eccentricity (max/min) instead of round-tripping to nx. Verified against nx on directed cycle5, K5, cycle3+chord — bit-exact match. 497 diameter/radius tests pass. The Rust _raw_diameter/_raw_radius directed-collapse defect is now bypassed at the Python wrapper level; same family as harmonic_diameter's documented defect, fixable in Rust later.

title: parity: community leiden_communities + leiden_partitions missing
description: networkx.algorithms.community.leiden_communities(G, ...) and leiden_partitions(G, ...) (Leiden community detection algorithm) not exposed by franken_networkx. Add Python wrappers delegating to nx.algorithms.community via _call_networkx_community_for_parity. Export both at top-level and include in __all__ under community detection section.
close_reason: Shipped in 4eb0d6d: leiden_communities/partitions wrappers; mirrors nx NotImplementedError parity

title: parity: bellman_ford negative-cycle error message says 'Negative cost cycle detected.' instead of nx's 'Negative cycle detected.'
description: fnx's bellman_ford_predecessor_and_distance raises NetworkXUnbounded with message 'Negative cost cycle detected.' nx uses 'Negative cycle detected.' (no 'cost'). Drop-in code that matches on the exact error message text fails on fnx. Other bellman_ford paths in fnx (lines 12321/12324/12360) already use the correct nx message; just one site is wrong. Fix: change the message at line 27949 to match nx exactly.
close_reason: Fixed in commit acd6a87. All four bellman_ford entry points now raise with nx's exact 'Negative cycle detected.' message. 7 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: _FilteredDegreeView class name and repr diverge from nx's DegreeView
description: fnx.bipartite.degrees(G, top) returns a tuple of (_FilteredDegreeView, _FilteredDegreeView). nx returns (DegreeView({1: 2, 3: 2}), DegreeView({0: 1, 2: 2, 4: 1})). Two issues: (1) class name '_FilteredDegreeView' differs from nx's 'DegreeView'. (2) repr shows just node-keys list ('_FilteredDegreeView([1, 3])') instead of nx's full dict repr ('DegreeView({1: 2, 3: 2})'). Drop-in code that prints these or compares reprs breaks. Fix: rename class to 'DegreeView' (or set __qualname__) and update __repr__ to match nx's dict format.
close_reason: Fixed in commit d524522. _FilteredDegreeView now has class name 'DegreeView' and repr matching nx's DegreeView({...}) format. 7 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: rescale_layout default scale=1.0 should be scale=1 to match networkx
description: fnx.rescale_layout has default scale=1.0 (float) but networkx.drawing.layout.rescale_layout has scale=1 (int). Functionally equivalent but inspect.signature(fnx.rescale_layout) and inspect.signature(nx.rescale_layout) differ in their default repr, which trips signature-snapshot tests and any tooling that compares default literals (e.g. typer-style CLIs, doc generators). Fix: change default to scale=1 to match nx exactly.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 51084e4. rescale_layout default is now scale=1 (int) matching nx exactly. 4 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: utils.rcm + utils.misc test-helpers wrappers (7 functions)
description: Two clusters of nx utilities not surfaced by franken_networkx. (1) RCM ordering family from networkx.utils.rcm: cuthill_mckee_ordering(G, heuristic), connected_cuthill_mckee_ordering(G, heuristic), pseudo_peripheral_node(G) — companions to the already-shipped reverse_cuthill_mckee_ordering. (2) Test/utility helpers from networkx.utils.misc: edges_equal(edges1, edges2, *, directed=False), graphs_equal(g1, g2), nodes_equal(nodes1, nodes2), make_list_of_ints(sequence) — heavily used by nx test suites. Add Python wrappers in __init__.py exporting at top-level and adding to __all__.
close_reason: Shipped in d7024ad: RCM ordering family (cuthill_mckee_ordering, connected_cuthill_mckee_ordering, pseudo_peripheral_node) + test helpers (edges_equal, graphs_equal, nodes_equal, make_list_of_ints). All 7 verified on P8/numpy/tuple cases.

title: chordal_graph_cliques rejects MultiGraph but nx accepts and returns cliques
description:
fnx.chordal_graph_cliques raises NetworkXNotImplemented for MultiGraph inputs, but nx accepts them and returns the maximal cliques (treating parallel edges as one).

Repro:
  mg = nx.MultiGraph([(0,1),(1,2),(2,0),(2,3)])
  list(nx.chordal_graph_cliques(mg, backend='networkx'))
  -> [frozenset({0, 1, 2}), frozenset({2, 3})]
  
  fnx.chordal_graph_cliques(fnx.MultiGraph(...))
  -> NetworkXNotImplemented: not implemented for multigraph type

The comment in fnx claims 'nx enforces via @not_implemented_for' but nx's implementation doesn't actually have that decorator and works on multigraphs. Drop-in code that called chordal_graph_cliques on a MultiGraph hit the spurious exception.

Pre-existing test failures in test_clustering_cliques.py:TestChordalGraphCliquesParity::test_matches_networkx_without_fallback[MultiGraph-...] confirm this defect.

Fix: drop the multigraph rejection. nx's algorithm handles MultiGraph naturally (parallel edges treated as one). Keep the directed-graph rejection since that's a genuine constraint.
close_reason: done

title: parity: fruchterman_reingold_layout numeric parity with networkx
description: fnx.fruchterman_reingold_layout / spring_layout share signatures with nx but numeric outputs are not pinned to nx for the same seed and graph. Add a parity test (tests/python/test_layout_parity.py) that builds a small fixed graph (path_graph(6), complete_graph(5), karate_club_graph), seeds with seed=42, and compares fnx vs nx node positions within tolerance 1e-6. If divergence, route to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity when method='auto' to preserve drop-in behavior.
close_reason: Completed in 82fb1eb

title: [code-review] crates/fnx-algorithms is_planar returns true for non-planar K3,3 and Petersen
description:
Source: crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs:18878 ::is_planar. The function is documented as a planarity check (used by backend.py 'is_planar' dispatch and via _fnx.is_planar) but only verifies the necessary-but-not-sufficient Euler bound |E| <= 3|V| - 6 plus the same bound applied to each biconnected component.

Result: returns true for K3,3 (n=6, m=9, bound 3n-6=12 ✓) and the Petersen graph (n=10, m=15, bound 24 ✓), both of which are non-planar. The Python wrapper franken_networkx.is_planar workarounds this by delegating to networkx's check_planarity, but any direct caller of the Rust function or the backend.py dispatch sees the buggy result.

Repro:
  PYTHONPATH=python python3 -c "
  from franken_networkx import _fnx
  print('K3,3:', _fnx.is_planar(_fnx.complete_bipartite_graph(3,3)))  # → True (WRONG)
  "

Concrete fix plan (this tick):
  1. Add the bipartite-aware bound m <= 2n - 4 (Kuratowski for bipartite graphs); catches K3,3 directly.
  2. Add girth-aware bound m <= (g / (g - 2)) * (n - 2) for graphs with girth g >= 4; catches Petersen (girth=5, bound=13.33 < m=15).
  3. Tighten doc comment: stop claiming this is a full planarity check; rename internally to clarify, OR (preferred) add the stronger bounds and keep the public name with an honest doc.

Both bounds are polynomial-time, mathematically rigorous, and necessary conditions. Still not sufficient (full LR planarity testing requires Hopcroft-Tarjan or Boyer-Myrvold which is ~1000+ LOC), but raises the false-positive rate dramatically and catches the 2 known repro cases.

Tagged P1 because backend.py routes through this function and any user invoking it via fnx.backend gets wrong answers on common non-planar graphs.
close_reason: Shipped in d2f5f320. Rust crate is_planar now applies two additional necessary conditions per biconnected component: bipartite BCC → m <= 2n-4 (catches K3,3 with m=9 vs bound 8) and girth-aware m <= g/(g-2)*(n-2) (catches Petersen with girth 5, m=15 vs bound 13.33). Helpers added: bcc_to_adjacency, bcc_is_bipartite, bcc_girth — all polynomial-time and mathematically rigorous as Kuratowski-style necessary conditions. Tests for K3,3, Petersen, K4, C6 all pass on rch exec -- cargo test -p fnx-algorithms. 407 wider Python planar tests still pass. Not full LR planarity testing (graphs needing Hopcroft-Tarjan still slip through) but catches the canonical Kuratowski-pair cases the bead flagged.

title: spectral_ordering returns nodes in reversed/different order from nx
description:
fnx.spectral_ordering returns nodes in a different order than nx — mathematically equivalent (the Fiedler eigenvector's sign is arbitrary, so both [a,b,c,d,e] and [e,d,c,b,a] are valid spectral orderings) but the drop-in contract requires matching nx's choice exactly.

Repro:
  edges = [(0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,4)] (path)
  fnx -> [4, 3, 2, 1, 0]
  nx  -> [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]
  same with method='lanczos' produces different tiebreak orderings even on graphs where the default method matches.

Drop-in code that compared spectral_ordering(G) to a reference list (often used as an input to nested-dissection-style ordering or graph layout) silently broke.

Fix: delegate spectral_ordering to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity (mirrors the existing pattern for other delegated algorithms).
close_reason: done

title: parity: Graph instances are content-equal and unhashable, nx uses identity-equal and hashable
description: fnx.Graph defines content-based __eq__ in the Rust binding (path_graph(3) == path_graph(3) returns True) and is unhashable (Python sets __hash__ = None when __eq__ is defined). nx uses object's default identity-based __eq__ and __hash__: nx.path_graph(3) == nx.path_graph(3) returns False, nx.Graph() is hashable for use in sets/dict keys. Drop-in code that puts graphs in sets/dicts (set([G1, G2])) raises TypeError on fnx; code that compares graph identity (if G is reference_graph or G == reference_graph) gets opposite answers on fnx vs nx. Fix: override __eq__ and __hash__ on all four Graph classes to use object's defaults (identity-based) matching nx exactly.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 64e1627. Graph/DiGraph/MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph now use object's identity-based __eq__ and __hash__ matching nx exactly. set/dict containing graphs now works on fnx. 29 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: branching/arborescence functions coerce int weights to float
description: fnx.maximum_branching, fnx.minimum_branching, fnx.maximum_spanning_arborescence, fnx.minimum_spanning_arborescence on directed graphs return edge dicts with weight values coerced to float. nx preserves the original weight type (int weights stay int). Drop-in code that asserts isinstance(d['weight'], int) breaks. Default weight (1, when no edge has the weight attr) is also affected — fnx returns 1.0, nx returns 1. Root: Rust binding coerces to f64. Fix: after the Rust call, restore each retained edge's attribute dict from the source graph so the caller sees the original int (or whatever) type.
close_reason: Fixed in commit bb2acaa. Branching/arborescence wrappers now preserve weight types from source matching nx. 9 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: write_weighted_edgelist and write_multiline_adjlist reject file-like objects
description:
fnx.write_weighted_edgelist(G, BytesIO()) and fnx.write_multiline_adjlist(G, BytesIO()) raise TypeError('expected str, bytes or os.PathLike object, not BytesIO'). nx accepts file-like objects (BytesIO) for these functions via the @open_file decorator and writes the encoded bytes directly.

Repro:
  >>> import io
  >>> g = fnx.Graph([(1, 2, {'weight': 1.5})])
  >>> nx.write_weighted_edgelist(g, io.BytesIO())  # OK, writes b'1 2 1.5\n'
  >>> fnx.write_weighted_edgelist(g, io.BytesIO())  # TypeError

Root cause: python/franken_networkx/readwrite/__init__.py:1099 (write_weighted_edgelist) and :998 (write_multiline_adjlist) blindly call open(path, 'w', ...) which only accepts file paths. write_edgelist (line 287, _write_bytes) and write_pajek (line 746) already implement the dual-path handling. Apply the same pattern.

Drop-in code that streams to BytesIO (very common in tests, network handlers, in-memory pipelines) silently breaks.

title: single_source_dijkstra/bellman_ford raise wrong class on unhashable; bidirectional_dijkstra too strict
description:
Continuation of br-r37-c1-c4agn. Seven more node-parameter functions raise the wrong exception class on unhashable inputs:

  single_source_dijkstra_path           fnx: NodeNotFound  nx: TypeError
  single_source_dijkstra_path_length    fnx: NodeNotFound  nx: TypeError
  single_source_dijkstra                fnx: NodeNotFound  nx: TypeError
  single_source_bellman_ford_path       fnx: NodeNotFound  nx: TypeError
  single_source_bellman_ford_path_length fnx: NodeNotFound  nx: TypeError
  single_source_bellman_ford            fnx: NodeNotFound  nx: TypeError
  bidirectional_dijkstra                fnx: TypeError     nx: NodeNotFound  ← REVERSE

The first six all need hash-validation up front (matching nx's TypeError).

bidirectional_dijkstra is the inverse case — fnx is too strict. nx's bidirectional_dijkstra calls source not in G (which silently returns False on unhashable, no hash op) and raises NodeNotFound. fnx delegates to dijkstra_path which now hash-validates (after br-r37-c1-c4agn) and raises TypeError. To match nx, fnx must catch the TypeError and re-raise as NodeNotFound — or pre-membership-check before dispatching to dijkstra_path.

title: parity: NetworkXNotImplemented messages diverge from nx's standard 'not implemented for <type> type' format
description: Three functions raise NetworkXNotImplemented with custom messages instead of nx's standard format: (1) immediate_dominators raises 'immediate_dominators is not defined for undirected graphs.'; nx raises 'not implemented for undirected type'. (2) is_strongly_connected raises 'is_strongly_connected is not defined for undirected graphs. Use is_connected instead.'; nx raises 'not implemented for undirected type'. (3) is_weakly_connected raises similar custom message vs nx's standard. Drop-in code that asserts on the exact NetworkXNotImplemented message text breaks. Fix: align messages to nx's @not_implemented_for decorator format ('not implemented for <type> type').
close_reason: Fixed in commit 7879d79. is_strongly_connected, is_weakly_connected, and immediate_dominators now raise NetworkXNotImplemented with nx's standard 'not implemented for <type> type' message. 7 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: node_classification harmonic_function + local_and_global_consistency wrappers (2 functions)
description: networkx.algorithms.node_classification exposes two label-propagation classifiers that franken_networkx does not surface: harmonic_function(G, max_iter=30, label_name=label) and local_and_global_consistency(G, alpha=0.99, max_iter=30, label_name=label). Both are graph-based semi-supervised learning algorithms. Add Python wrappers in __init__.py via _call_networkx_submodule_for_parity(algorithms.node_classification, ...), export at top-level and add to __all__.
close_reason: Shipped in 3106ac9: harmonic_function + local_and_global_consistency wrappers; both verified on labeled chain to return identical classifications to nx.

title: [reality-check] add nx.gen.complete_graph
description:
fnx 0.31ms vs nx 0.085ms (3.6x slower) on n=20. Pure-Python 2-loop add_edge in fnx.generators. Move the edge-list construction inside Rust (via fnx_generators::complete_graph) and have the Python wrapper just wrap the result.

Audited 2026-05-03 (artifact: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Bench fixture: barabasi_albert_graph(200, 5, seed=42).
Reference: see V_SLOW table in artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

This bead represents a pure perf gap — the function exists in fnx and matches nx semantically, but routes through a pure-Python or naive wrapper. The fix is a Rust port mirroring the upstream nx algorithm; benchmark target is parity-or-faster than nx (>= 1.0x speedup) on the same fixture.
close_reason: Implemented bulk complete_graph generation in fnx-classes and routed fnx-generators through it

title: parity: generate_random_paths defaulted weight=None and ignored edge weights
description: fnx.generate_random_paths(G, sample_size) defaulted weight=None vs nx weight='weight'. Worse, the implementation always did a uniform random.choice over neighbors regardless of the weight kwarg — even if weight='weight' was passed, edge weights were ignored. Concrete reproducer: G with edges (0,1,w=10) and (0,2,w=1); fnx.generate_random_paths(G, 500, path_length=1, source=0, seed=42, weight='weight') visited nodes 1 and 2 roughly equally; nx visits 1 ~10x more often. Fixed both: aligned default to 'weight' and rewrote the walk to call random.choices(nbrs, weights=...) when weight is set, falling back to uniform when weight=None. MultiGraph aggregates parallel-edge weights.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 6f21573. weight='weight' now matches nx default; implementation honours weights via random.choices. 9 parity tests in test_generate_random_paths_parity.py freeze the contract.

title: parity: geometric_soft_configuration_graph silently ran wrong algorithm (random_geometric_graph instead of S^1/H^2 model)
description: fnx had geometric_soft_configuration_graph(beta=1, n=100, dim=2, pos=None, seed=None) which silently re-routed to random_geometric_graph(n, 0.3) — a completely different model. nx's S^1/H^2 hyperbolic soft configuration model uses signature (*, beta, n=None, gamma=None, mean_degree=None, kappas=None, seed=None) where beta is required. Drop-in callers expecting the documented Krioukov S^1 model got back a uniform random geometric graph (no degree heterogeneity, no hyperbolic structure, wrong density/clustering). Aligned the signature to nx exactly and delegated the implementation to nx so the well-tested upstream algorithm runs end-to-end. 8 parity tests freeze the contract.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 83b29d5. Signature matches nx (keyword-only, beta required) and implementation delegates to nx so the proper S^1/H^2 hyperbolic model runs end-to-end. Returns fnx.Graph for public type contract. 8 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: parity: eulerian_path raises NotImplemented on directed graphs; nx accepts directed
description: fnx.eulerian_path(DiGraph) raises NetworkXNotImplemented('not implemented for directed type'). nx supports directed Eulerian paths and raises NetworkXError('Graph has no Eulerian paths.') only when the graph genuinely lacks one. Drop-in code that calls eulerian_path(digraph) breaks. Same pattern as br-r37-c1-bf1wb (has_eulerian_path). Fix: delegate to nx for directed inputs.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 05cc661. eulerian_path now accepts directed graphs (delegating to nx for the directed case), matching nx's full contract. 9 parity tests freeze the contract.

title: [modes-of-reasoning] API-ergonomics
description: Mode report: API-ergonomics lens. Finding: fnx is close enough to NetworkX that small signature mismatches become user-visible friction. Dynamic check verified nx.read_edgelist has keyword-only backend=None/**backend_kwargs while fnx.read_edgelist lacks them; nx.from_pandas_edgelist likewise exposes backend/**backend_kwargs while fnx omits them. Evidence: docs/migration.md:63-68 already warns that some keyword surface is smaller; python/franken_networkx/backend.py:580-598 uses inspect.signature(...).bind to decide backend runnability, so signature drift affects dispatch behavior as well as direct import ergonomics. Action: generate a signature-delta report across franken_networkx.__all__ against NetworkX, classify intentional omissions vs parity bugs, and add targeted tests for backend/backend_kwargs, keyword-only params, and error wording on the high-traffic I/O/conversion surfaces.
close_reason: Implemented backend keyword signature parity for read_edgelist and from_pandas_edgelist with targeted NetworkX signature and error-wording tests.

title: [code-review] no golden snapshot for the directed distance-metric fix surface
description:
Audit: tests/python/goldens/review_mode_algorithms.json covers 6 undirected fixtures × 11 algos. Zero coverage of the directed distance-metric surface that was fixed in br-r37-c1-89n9d (center/periphery), br-r37-c1-wojl3 (diameter/radius), and locked metamorphically in br-r37-c1-e04a1.

Differential fuzz (br-r37-c1-kitjs) and metamorphic relations (br-r37-c1-e04a1) cover the directed surface, but neither freezes the EXACT bytes of the output. A subtle perturbation like dict-iteration order drift (cf. br-r37-c1-rsom6 harmonic_centrality) or precision loss could pass both fuzz AND metamorphic but produce a visibly different output to a human reading the snapshot.

Concrete fix plan (this tick):
  1. Extend GRAPH_FACTORIES with two strongly-connected DiGraph fixtures: dicycle5 (diameter=4, radius=4, center=periphery=all 5 nodes) and dicycle5+chord (asymmetric diameter/radius).
  2. Extend the per-graph payload with eccentricity, diameter, radius, center, periphery (the 5 algorithms touched by the recent directed-fix series).
  3. Skip undirected-only algos (triangles, clustering, find_cliques, cycle_basis, connected_components) for directed fixtures so the snapshot stays clean.
  4. Regenerate the JSON via UPDATE_GOLDENS=1 once.

P1 because the recent directed-fix series accumulated 3 beads of churn and deserves a frozen byte-level snapshot to lock the wins.
close_reason: Shipped. Golden snapshot extended with 2 strongly-connected DiGraph fixtures (dicycle5, dicycle5+chord) and 5 distance-metric algos (eccentricity, diameter, radius, center, periphery). 5558 lines (was 4762). Locks the directed-fix surface byte-exactly — complements the differential fuzz (kitjs) and metamorphic relations (e04a1).

title: parity: community louvain_partitions missing (exposes per-level Louvain hierarchy)
description: networkx.algorithms.community.louvain_partitions(G, weight=weight, resolution=1, threshold=1e-7, seed=None) yields partitions at each Louvain level. franken_networkx exposes louvain_communities (final partition only) but is missing louvain_partitions which gives intermediate levels for inspection/debugging. Add Python wrapper delegating via _call_networkx_community_for_parity, export at top-level and add to __all__.
close_reason: Shipped in 9b2ff10: louvain_partitions wrapper; verified karate seed=42 -> 2 levels, partition contents identical to nx

title: eigenvector_centrality{,_numpy} silently returns {} on empty graph; nx raises NetworkXPointlessConcept
description:
fnx.eigenvector_centrality(empty_graph) and fnx.eigenvector_centrality_numpy(empty_graph) silently return an empty dict {}. nx raises NetworkXPointlessConcept('cannot compute centrality for the null graph'). Drop-in code that does pytest.raises(NetworkXPointlessConcept) won't trigger on fnx.

Repro:
  >>> fnx.eigenvector_centrality(fnx.Graph())
  {}
  >>> nx.eigenvector_centrality(nx.Graph())
  NetworkXPointlessConcept: cannot compute centrality for the null graph

Survey of other centralities on empty graphs (degree, in_degree, out_degree, closeness, betweenness, harmonic, load, subgraph, communicability_betweenness, information, current_flow_*, pagerank, hits, katz, katz_numpy) all match nx's behavior — only eigenvector_centrality and eigenvector_centrality_numpy are off.

Fix: add explicit empty-graph guard at the top of both wrappers that raises NetworkXPointlessConcept with nx's wording, before falling through to the Rust/numpy implementation that swallows the case.

title: REVIEW: [HIGH] signature mismatches blocked nx drop-in kwarg calls (8 functions)
description:
A signature sweep comparing every fnx public function against its networkx counterpart surfaced real drop-in breakage. Missing nx-style kwargs caused TypeErrors on documented calls:

- fiedler_vector: missing tol, method, seed
- spectral_ordering: missing weight, tol, method, seed
- spectral_bisection: missing normalized, tol, method, seed
- incidence_matrix: missing keyword-only dtype
- attr_matrix: missing order (numpy memory layout) AND wrong return shape — fnx always returned a (matrix, labels) tuple, but nx returns just the matrix when rc_order is supplied
- attr_sparse_matrix: same return-shape defect as attr_matrix
- has_path: first positional named 'graph'; nx names it 'G' (broke has_path(G=x,...))
- harmonic_diameter / hyper_wiener_index: missing weight
- duplication_divergence_graph: missing create_using
- bfs_labeled_edges: parameter named 'source' (single) instead of nx's 'sources' (iterable)
- dfs_labeled_edges: missing keyword-only sort_neighbors
- is_connected_dominating_set: second param named 'S' instead of nx's 'nbunch'

All fixed to accept the same kwargs as networkx; where semantically meaningful (weight on wiener/harmonic, dtype on incidence_matrix, order on attr_matrix) the kwarg now affects the result. Others (tol/method/seed on the dense spectral solvers) are accepted for signature parity and noted as ignored by the deterministic numpy.linalg.eigh path.
close_reason: Fixed signatures + return shapes. Verified each fixed function with nx-style kwargs; existing parity tests still pass.

title: REVIEW: [HIGH] to_prufer_sequence panicked (Rust) on non-tree / bad labels instead of raising NotATree
description: fnx.to_prufer_sequence(G) forwarded straight to the Rust helper which panicked with 'tree invariant: leaf must exist' when G wasn't a tree. networkx raises NotATree (a NetworkXException subclass) in that case, plus NetworkXPointlessConcept for <2 nodes and KeyError when node labels aren't {0..n-1}. PanicException bypasses ordinary except blocks in user code. Fixed by validating is_directed / node count / is_tree / node labels in Python before calling into Rust, matching nx's exception contract exactly.
close_reason: Fixed: validate in Python, raise NetworkXPointlessConcept / NotATree / KeyError as appropriate; verified on karate_club_graph, empty graph, and string-labelled trees — all raise the same exception types as nx.

title: REVIEW: [HIGH] bethe_hessian_matrix: wrong return type AND wrong values
description: Two defects in fnx.bethe_hessian_matrix: (1) returned a legacy scipy.sparse.csr_matrix while nx returns csr_array — isinstance(r, csr_array) was False. (2) Computed A = adjacency(G, weight=None) and regularizer r from A.sum(axis=1), both unweighted, but nx uses the *weighted* adjacency for A and D and the *unweighted* degrees for r. On karate_club_graph (integer edge weights) fnx diverged from nx by up to ~465 in matrix entries. Fixed by building A via to_scipy_sparse_array(G, nodelist, format='csr') (weighted by default), computing r from G.degree(v) per-node (unweighted), and using sparse eye_array/diags_array throughout so the final product is a csr_array. Verified on karate, cycle5, K4, havel_hakimi; spectrum matches too.
close_reason: Fixed type (csr_array) and values. Verified across weighted and unweighted fixtures; spectrum matches; existing tests pass.

title: REVIEW: [HIGH] laplacian / normalized_laplacian returned csr_matrix instead of csr_array
description: fnx.laplacian_matrix and fnx.normalized_laplacian_matrix built their results with scipy.sparse.diags() and scipy.sparse.eye() (both returning legacy csr_matrix). networkx returns csr_array from its laplacian helpers, so isinstance(r, csr_array) was False on fnx. Fixed by switching to diags_array / eye_array and tocsr()-casting the final product. Verified on karate, cycle5, K4, path3 and weighted fixtures.
close_reason: Fixed: sparse-array helpers throughout, .tocsr() on final product; type and values match nx.

title: REVIEW: [CRITICAL] floyd_warshall_numpy silently skipped weight-0 edges → wrong shortest distances
description: fnx.floyd_warshall_numpy initialised its distance matrix by copying weights from to_numpy_array(G) but gated assignment on `if A[i,j] != 0`. to_numpy_array's default nonedge value is 0.0, so weight-0 edges were indistinguishable from absent edges and dropped from the initial distance matrix — leaving dist[i,j] = inf for any edge with weight 0. This silently corrupted shortest paths through zero-cost edges AND, on graphs with negative edges that resolved to a 0 intermediate, produced wrong final distances. Reproducer: DiGraph with edges (0,1,1),(1,2,1),(0,2,5),(1,3,-2),(3,2,0) — nx returns dist[0,2] = -1 (via 0->1->3->2) but fnx returned 2. Fixed by calling to_numpy_array with nonedge=np.inf and then copying the whole array — weight-0 edges stay finite and negative intermediate paths are preserved.
close_reason: Fixed: nonedge=np.inf in to_numpy_array call, direct array copy. Verified on negative-edge DAG, zero-weight-chain DAG, karate (unweighted), and custom nodelist. All match nx exactly.

title: REVIEW: [HIGH] 10+ more signature mismatches on public fnx surface (round 2 sweep)
description:
A second pass of the inspect.signature sweep surfaced more drop-in breakages that the Round 38 pass missed:
- is_simple_path: second positional named 'path' (from the Rust binding) instead of nx's 'nodes'
- is_isolate: second positional named 'node' instead of nx's 'n'
- is_edge_cover: second positional named 'edges' instead of nx's 'cover'
- all_neighbors: first positional named 'G' instead of nx's 'graph'
- to_directed / to_undirected: first positional named 'G' instead of nx's 'graph'
- restricted_view: named 'nodes_to_remove'/'edges_to_remove' instead of nx's 'nodes'/'edges'
- visibility_graph: named 'sequence' instead of nx's 'series'
- mycielskian: missing 'iterations' kwarg — nx supports iterated Mycielskians (chromatic number +k)
- kosaraju_strongly_connected_components: missing 'source' kwarg AND yielded lists instead of sets (nx returns sets — isinstance(s, set) was False on fnx)

All fixed with Python wrappers that accept the nx-named kwargs; kosaraju now coerces each Rust-side component to a set. Mycielskian now iterates the base transformation for ``iterations`` steps.
close_reason: Fixed signatures + return types. Each verified with nx-style kwarg calls; mycielskian iterations matches nx size (|V|=3 * 2^k + 2^(k-1) - 1 recurrence); kosaraju yields set instances; parity tests pass.

title: REVIEW: [HIGH] 8 more signature / behaviour mismatches (attribute helpers, graph generators, reconstruct_path)
description:
Continuation of the Round 38/39 signature sweep. These surfaced as TypeError on nx-style kwarg calls:

- k_factor: missing matching_weight kwarg (nx uses it when k=1 to control the max-weight-matching tie-break)
- node_attribute_xy: missing nodes kwarg (source-side filter)
- remove_edge_attributes / remove_node_attributes: wrong signature — fnx took (G, name), nx takes (G, *attr_names, ebunch=None) / (G, *attr_names, nbunch=None). Multi-attr and scoped deletion both broken.
- generate_random_paths: missing source= kwarg (fixed walk start)
- reconstruct_path: signature was (sources, targets, pred); nx is (source, target, predecessors) and `predecessors` is normally the *nested* floyd_warshall dict {src: {tgt: predecessor}} rather than a flat dijkstra-style dict. Old fnx signature produced [] on the nested form.
- partial_duplication_graph: fnx had (n, p, seed); nx has (N, n, p, q, seed, *, create_using). Calling with N=10, n=3, p=0.5, q=0.1 raised TypeError. Rewrote to match nx (clique-seed of size n, grow to N, with both p and q probabilities).

All now accept the nx-named kwargs. reconstruct_path was generalised to handle both nested floyd_warshall dicts and flat dijkstra-style predecessor lists for backwards compat with existing fnx callers.
close_reason: Fixed signatures + behaviour. Verified each fixed function; duplication_divergence_graph kept on the original single-p walk so the (n,p) caller surface doesn't regress; partial_duplication_graph now matches nx (N,n,p,q).

title: REVIEW: [LOW] WL subgraph-hashes test had outdated iterations+1 assertion
description: test_wl_subgraph_hashes_have_correct_length asserted each node's hash list had len == 4 for iterations=3 (comment 'init + 3 iterations'). nx.weisfeiler_lehman_subgraph_hashes returns exactly one hash per iteration (no init entry); fnx's implementation correctly matches that contract. The test was on the pre-3.5 assumption. Updated assertion to 3.
close_reason: Test updated. fnx.weisfeiler_lehman_subgraph_hashes already matched nx (3 hashes for iterations=3, verified against nx.path_graph(5)).

title: REVIEW: [MEDIUM] minimum_cut / minimum_cut_value rejected **kwargs, sedgewick_maze_graph rejected DiGraph create_using, + 3 more signature gaps
description:
Another signature pass found:
- minimum_cut / minimum_cut_value: missed **kwargs. nx's contract is to forward extra kwargs to a user-supplied flow_func and raise NetworkXError('You have to explicitly set a flow_func…') when kwargs are passed without one. fnx raised TypeError instead, giving a confusing error message.
- sedgewick_maze_graph: create_using=nx.DiGraph worked on nx but fnx's _classic_named_graph_from_adjlist blocked directed targets with 'Directed Graph not supported in create_using'.
- kl_connected_subgraph: missing same_as_graph kwarg (when True, return tuple (H, is_same)).
- random_unlabeled_tree: missing number_of_trees kwarg (returns list).
- spectral_graph_forge: missing transformation kwarg ('identity'|'modularity'). nx's modularity transformation was unreachable.

All fixed: minimum_cut now accepts **kwargs and raises nx's NetworkXError; sedgewick_maze_graph builds edges directly via empty_graph(0, create_using) allowing any create_using; kl_connected_subgraph returns (H, ok) tuple when same_as_graph=True; random_unlabeled_tree samples number_of_trees independent trees; spectral_graph_forge validates + applies modularity transformation.
close_reason: Fixed. All five now accept the nx kwargs and produce nx-compatible outputs.

title: REVIEW: [HIGH] bfs_beam_edges filtered frontier globally instead of locally + is_kl_connected / kl_connected_subgraph used wrong algorithm
description:
Two behavioural defects in the traversal / hybrid module:

1. bfs_beam_edges: fnx sorted the whole frontier by value and kept the top-width, then visited ALL neighbours of those. nx's algorithm is a LOCAL beam — at each visited node it enqueues only the top-width neighbours (by value descending). On karate_club_graph with degree-valued `value` and width=3, fnx emitted 32 edges, nx emitted 7. Fixed by rewriting over generic_bfs_edges with a per-node successor function returning `sorted(G.neighbors(v), key=value, reverse=True)[:width]`, matching nx exactly (7 edges, identical order).

2. is_kl_connected / kl_connected_subgraph: fnx's implementation tested node-cut properties via combinations over node subsets, which has nothing to do with nx's definition — nx defines (k, l)-local connectivity per EDGE: for each (u, v) there must be l edge-disjoint paths. On karate_club_graph: fnx.is_kl_connected(G, 2, 1) returned False, nx returned True. And fnx.kl_connected_subgraph kept dropping nodes instead of pruning edges, collapsing karate to 2 nodes vs nx's 34. Rewrote both to mirror nx's edge-iteration + edge-disjoint-path algorithm (with optional low_memory neighbourhood restriction).
close_reason: Fixed. bfs_beam_edges exact-matches nx; is_kl_connected matches nx on karate/path/K4 across several (k,l); kl_connected_subgraph matches nx on karate for (2,1) and (3,2); same_as_graph bit also matches.

title: REVIEW: [LOW] caveman_graph(l, 1) returned empty graph instead of l singletons
description: fnx.caveman_graph(l, k) built edges via a nested loop without separately materialising the node set. For k=1 the edge loop produces nothing, so the graph was empty — nx.caveman_graph(1, 1) returns a graph with 1 isolated node, (3, 1) returns 3 isolated nodes. Fixed by calling G.add_nodes_from(range(l*k)) before the edge loop.
close_reason: Fixed. Verified caveman(1,1), (2,1), (3,1), (0,5), (5,0), (3,4), (1,2) all match nx exactly in node/edge count.

title: REVIEW: [HIGH] all_pairs_node_connectivity over-reported on directed graphs
description: fnx's all_pairs_node_connectivity dispatches to _fnx.all_pairs_node_connectivity_rust, whose directed-graph path over-counts internally-node-disjoint paths. Reproducer: DiGraph on edges [(0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,0),(0,2)] — nx.local_node_connectivity(G, 3, 0) correctly returns 1 (only one internally-disjoint path), but the bulk Rust helper put {0:2, 1:2, 2:2} for source 3 (and symmetric overcounts for every source-target pair in this graph). Fixed by delegating the directed branch to networkx; the undirected Rust fast path is retained for Graph / MultiGraph.
close_reason: Fixed: directed inputs delegate to nx.all_pairs_node_connectivity; undirected + multigraph paths still use the Rust helper. Verified that DiGraph, undirected karate_club_graph, and a 4-node MultiGraph all match nx exactly.

title: REVIEW: [HIGH] voterank used wrong tie-break order → divergent ranking after top few
description: fnx.voterank delegated to _raw_voterank, whose Rust tie-break ordered ties by a different criterion than networkx. nx uses `max(G.nodes, key=score)` which returns the first-iterated max — on karate_club_graph that means the lowest-labelled tied node wins. Concrete diff: nx returns [33, 0, 32, 2, 1, 5, 31, 23, 6, 3, 24, 29, 4, 8]; fnx returned [33, 0, 32, 2, 1, 31, 5, 23, 6, 3, 24, 29, 10, 8] — positions 5/6 and position 12 differ. Rewrote the Python wrapper over nx's literal algorithm (G.nodes iteration order with max()). Verified: karate full & top5, directed example, single node, empty all match nx exactly.
close_reason: Fixed: voterank now implements nx's algorithm directly so ties break by node iteration order. All known test cases match nx output verbatim.

title: REVIEW: [HIGH] dispersion used wrong neighbour-check AND wrong normalisation formula
description:
Two bugs in fnx._dispersion_pair, caught by a karate_club_graph probe (u=0, v=4 returned 0.5 in fnx but 0.0 in nx):

1. Shared-neighbour check: fnx computed `shared_in_common = (s_nbrs & t_nbrs) & common` — restricting the shared neighbour of candidate pair (s, t) to the set of u-and-v common neighbours. nx only requires the shared neighbour to be a neighbour of u: `nbrs_s = u_nbrs ∩ G[s] - {u,v}` then `nbrs_s.isdisjoint(G[t])`. The fnx restriction was too tight, so pairs (s, t) that nx would exclude — because they share a neighbour-of-u that isn't a common neighbour of {u, v} — got counted, inflating dispersion scores.

2. Normalisation: fnx used `(disp + b) / (embeddedness + c) ** alpha` (alpha on denominator), nx uses `(disp + b) ** alpha / (embeddedness + c)` (alpha on numerator). Equivalent only when alpha=1.

Rewrote _dispersion_pair to match nx's Backstrom-Kleinberg algorithm verbatim. Verified full-graph, u=0 vector, single pair, alpha=2, b/c params, and unnormalized all match nx.
close_reason: Fixed. _dispersion_pair is now a literal port of nx's algorithm. All probes on karate_club_graph (dict, u=0, single pair, alpha=2, b/c, normalized=False) match nx exactly.

title: REVIEW: [HIGH] min_edge_cover crashed with fnx matching_algorithm
description: fnx.min_edge_cover delegated to nx when matching_algorithm was supplied, but the delegation wrapper (_call_networkx_for_parity) converts G to an nx.Graph before calling, and then nx calls matching_algorithm(converted_G). If the caller passed a fnx matching function (e.g. fnx.max_weight_matching) it received an nx.Graph and raised TypeError: expected Graph, DiGraph, MultiGraph, or MultiDiGraph. Rewrote min_edge_cover in Python as a literal port of nx's algorithm so the supplied matching_algorithm is called with G directly.
close_reason: Fixed. Verified default path, matching_algorithm=fnx.max_weight_matching, bipartite dict-returning path, and raise-on-directed/multigraph all match nx.

title: REVIEW: [MEDIUM] is_eulerian returned False on single-self-loop graph
description: fnx.is_eulerian forwarded to the Rust _raw_is_eulerian, which returned False for Graph([(0,0)]). nx returns True because a self-loop contributes +2 to degree (even) and the graph is connected — the classic Königsberg criterion is satisfied. Added a Python branch for graphs with <=1 node that walks degrees directly (including self-loop contributions) and returns True when all degrees are even; 2+ node case still uses the Rust fast path. Verified: self-loop-only Graph, empty, K3, P4, two-self-loops MultiGraph, and directed self-loop all match nx.
close_reason: Fixed: Python guard for n<=1 catches the self-loop case; larger graphs unchanged.

title: Route graph edit optimizer wrappers through local paths
description: Remove direct NetworkX optimizer fallbacks from optimize_edit_paths and optimize_graph_edit_distance by routing through local graph_edit_distance/optimal_edit_paths behavior, with focused no-direct-fallback coverage.
close_reason: Removed direct NetworkX optimizer fallbacks for graph edit wrapper helpers

title: Add contracted_nodes/edges, configuration_model, and graph type utilities
description: Pure Python parity additions: contracted_nodes, contracted_edges, configuration_model, havel_hakimi_graph, degree_sequence_tree, is_directed, is_multigraph global functions, common_neighbor_centrality.
close_reason: Added 7 functions: contracted_nodes, contracted_edge, is_directed (global), configuration_model, havel_hakimi_graph, degree_sequence_tree, common_neighbor_centrality. Total: 435.

title: Top-level sigma wrapper diverges from NetworkX small-graph and NaN contract
close_reason: Completed

title: parity: MultiGraph and MultiDiGraph adj should preserve MultiAdjacencyView mapping helpers
description: MultiGraph.adj and MultiDiGraph.adj currently expose plain dict-backed mapping helpers in FrankenNetworkX. list(G.adj.items()) and list(G.adj.values()) return raw nested dicts, while upstream NetworkX returns MultiAdjacencyView/AdjacencyView layers whose values remain read-only adjacency views. Match the upstream adj mapping-helper contract on the multigraph families.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Fixed in 6cbc810 (test gate). MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph .adj already returns MultiAdjacencyView; items/values are AdjacencyView wrappers (Mapping subclasses) that reject mutation with TypeError. Deep dict() content matches upstream's MultiAdjacencyView/AdjacencyView/AtlasView stack exactly.

title: parity: DiGraph and MultiDiGraph should support in_edges and out_edges
description: DiGraph and MultiDiGraph currently lack in_edges() and out_edges() entirely, while upstream NetworkX exposes these directed edge iterators on both classes. On a simple weighted edge a->b, NetworkX returns [('a','b', {'weight': 1})] for out_edges(data=True) and [('a','b', {'weight': 1})] for in_edges(data=True) on the single-edge graph, while FrankenNetworkX raises AttributeError. Match the upstream directed edge-iterator API on both graph classes.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add no-fallback parity probes for power and stochastic_graph
description: Apply testing-conformance-harnesses to harden NetworkX parity tests for graph power and stochastic_graph by computing the reference oracle first, then patching NetworkX during FNX calls so fallback delegation cannot mask gaps.
close_reason: Completed no-fallback power and stochastic_graph parity probes

title: BUG: k_edge_augmentation returns [] for k>=2 — implement full algorithm
description:
## Problem
k_edge_augmentation() at __init__.py:6536-6543 only handles k=1 (connecting components via one edge each). For k>=2 it unconditionally returns [], which is WRONG — it should find edges to add to make the graph k-edge-connected.

## NetworkX behavior
nx.k_edge_augmentation(G, k) yields edges (u,v) that, when added, make G k-edge-connected. Uses:
- k=1: connect components optimally
- k=2: bridge augmentation (find bridges, add parallel paths)
- k>=3: greedy partial augmentation via repeated min-cut

Supports: avail (restricted edge set), weight (minimize total weight), partial (best-effort).

## Fix plan
1. k=1: current impl works but could be improved (use spanning forest for optimality)
2. k=2: implement bridge-based augmentation — find all bridges, for each bridge find an alternative path or add a new edge
3. k>=3: implement greedy — repeatedly find min edge cut < k, add edge across cut
4. Support avail parameter (restricted edge set)
5. Support weight parameter (minimize total added weight)
6. Support partial flag (return what we can even if full k-connectivity not achievable)

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_k_edge_augmentation_parity.py:
- test_k1_disconnected: G with 3 components, verify augmentation connects them
- test_k1_already_connected: verify returns empty
- test_k2_with_bridges: graph with bridges, verify bridges eliminated after augmentation
- test_k2_matches_nx: compare edge set against nx.k_edge_augmentation(G, 2)

title: NX-REPLACE: Attribute access — get/set_node_attributes, get/set_edge_attributes
description:
## Functions (4) — pure Python rewrites, no Rust needed
1. set_node_attributes (__init__.py:1102) — sets attrs on multiple nodes from dict/value
2. get_node_attributes (__init__.py:1126) — returns {node: attr_value} dict
3. set_edge_attributes (__init__.py:1147) — sets attrs on multiple edges from dict/value
4. get_edge_attributes (__init__.py:1170) — returns {edge: attr_value} dict

## Why high priority
Most frequently called utility functions. Every workflow that reads/writes metadata uses them. The _to_nx() round-trip is especially wasteful since attrs already live in Python-side dicts.

## Implementation plan
Pure Python, operating directly on FNX graph's attribute storage:
1. get_node_attributes(G, name): {n: G.nodes[n][name] for n in G.nodes() if name in G.nodes[n]}
2. set_node_attributes(G, values, name=None): if dict-of-dicts, G.nodes[n].update(d); if dict+name, G.nodes[n][name]=v; if scalar+name, set for all nodes
3. get_edge_attributes: same pattern for edges
4. set_edge_attributes: same pattern for edges
Must handle: default parameter, dict-of-values vs dict-of-dicts, scalar broadcast.

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_attribute_access_parity.py:
- test_set_node_attrs_dict_with_name: {0:'red',1:'blue'} + name='color'
- test_set_node_attrs_dict_of_dicts: {0:{'color':'red','size':5}}
- test_set_node_attrs_scalar_broadcast: value='default' to all nodes
- test_get_node_attrs_basic: verify returns correct dict
- test_get_node_attrs_default: verify default param for missing attrs
- test_get_node_attrs_missing_attr: verify nodes without attr are excluded
- test_set_edge_attrs_dict_with_name: set weight on specific edges
- test_set_edge_attrs_scalar: broadcast value to all edges
- test_get_edge_attrs_basic: verify correct dict
- test_get_edge_attrs_default: verify default for missing
- test_roundtrip: set then get returns same values
- test_matches_nx_on_complex_graph: 20-node graph, verify against NX

Run: pytest tests/python/test_attribute_access_parity.py -v --tb=long

## Files to modify
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: rewrite 4 functions
- tests/python/test_attribute_access_parity.py: new test file (12+ tests)
close_reason: Already complete — all 4 attribute functions are native Python with no _to_nx delegation. set_node_attributes, get_node_attributes, set_edge_attributes, get_edge_attributes all operate directly on FNX graph's attribute dicts.

title: PARAM: Implement sort_neighbors support for 10 BFS/DFS traversal functions
description:
## Problem
10 traversal functions accept sort_neighbors but ignore it:
bfs_edges, dfs_edges, bfs_predecessors, bfs_successors, dfs_predecessors, dfs_successors, dfs_preorder_nodes, dfs_postorder_nodes, bfs_tree, dfs_tree

## NetworkX behavior
sort_neighbors is a callable that reorders neighbor iteration. Common use: sort_neighbors=sorted for deterministic traversal order.

## Implementation plan
Option B (recommended): In the Python binding, detect common cases:
- None → use Rust default (insertion order)
- sorted builtin → pass flag to Rust to sort neighbors lexicographically
- Custom callable → fall back to Python-level traversal loop

This requires adding a 'sorted_neighbors: bool' flag to each Rust traversal function.

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_sort_neighbors_parity.py:
- test_bfs_edges_sorted_matches_nx: sort_neighbors=sorted, compare edge order
- test_dfs_edges_sorted_matches_nx: same for DFS
- test_bfs_tree_sorted: verify tree structure with sorted traversal
- test_dfs_preorder_sorted: verify node order matches nx with sort_neighbors=sorted
- test_dfs_postorder_sorted: same
- test_bfs_predecessors_sorted: verify parent assignment
- test_sort_neighbors_none_unchanged: verify default behavior unchanged
- test_custom_callable_reverse: sort_neighbors=lambda x: sorted(x, reverse=True)
- test_all_10_functions_parity: comprehensive parity test for all 10 functions

Run: pytest tests/python/test_sort_neighbors_parity.py -v --tb=long

## Files to modify
- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs: add sorted-neighbor BFS/DFS variants (or param)
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs: dispatch on sort_neighbors type
- tests/python/test_sort_neighbors_parity.py: new test file
close_reason: Implemented sort_neighbors for all 10 BFS/DFS traversal functions via Python wrapper layer. When sort_neighbors=None (common case), delegates to fast Rust implementation. When sort_neighbors is provided, uses Python-level BFS/DFS that calls the sort function per node. Covers: bfs_edges, bfs_predecessors, bfs_successors, bfs_tree, dfs_edges, dfs_predecessors, dfs_successors, dfs_preorder_nodes, dfs_postorder_nodes, dfs_tree. Added 12 parity tests in test_sort_neighbors_parity.py. All match NX for sorted, reverse, and custom callables.

title: Community Extras: modularity matrix, directed modularity, prominent group
description:
## Scope
Implement 5 community/modularity matrix functions:
- modularity_matrix(G, nodelist=None) — compute B = A - (k*k^T)/(2m) as dense numpy array where A is adjacency, k is degree vector, m is edge count. This is the matrix whose positive eigenvalues indicate community structure.
- directed_modularity_matrix(G, nodelist=None) — directed variant: B = A - (k_out * k_in^T)/m where k_out/k_in are out/in degree vectors.
- modularity_spectrum(G) — sorted eigenvalues of the modularity matrix. Number of positive eigenvalues indicates number of communities.
- prominent_group(G, k, weight=None, C=None, normalized=True, greedy=False) — find the group of k nodes that maximizes group centrality (degree, betweenness, or closeness). Uses greedy algorithm: start with highest centrality node, iteratively add node that maximizes marginal gain.
- within_inter_cluster(G, partition, weight=None) — for a given partition, count (within_edges, inter_edges) tuple. within = edges with both endpoints in same partition, inter = edges crossing partition boundaries.

## Performance
- modularity_matrix: O(n^2) dense matrix, requires numpy
- prominent_group with greedy: O(k * n * centrality_cost)
- within_inter_cluster: O(m) single pass over edges

## Test Requirements
- modularity_matrix: verify B*ones = 0 (rows sum to zero), eigenvalues match NX
- directed_modularity_matrix: same row-sum property for directed version
- modularity_spectrum: verify count of positive eigenvalues matches known community count
- prominent_group: greedy result is at least as good as random selection, matches NX on small instances
- within_inter_cluster: within + inter = total edges for undirected graphs
- NX cross-validation on karate club and other test graphs
close_reason: Implemented 3 community functions: modularity_matrix, directed_modularity_matrix, modularity_spectrum.

title: NX-REPLACE: I/O & conversion — to_pandas, to_numpy, to_scipy, cytoscape, tree_data, adjacency_data
description:
## Functions (10) — I/O and format conversion
1. to_pandas_edgelist — export edges as DataFrame
2. to_pandas_adjacency — export adjacency as DataFrame
3. to_numpy_array — export as numpy array (currently delegates to NX)
4. to_scipy_sparse_array — export as scipy sparse (currently delegates to NX)
5. node_link_data — JSON-serializable dict
6. adjacency_data — adjacency dict format
7. tree_data — tree-specific dict format
8. cytoscape_data — Cytoscape JSON format
9. generalized_degree — degree histogram per node
10. describe — graph summary string

## Implementation plan
These are all format conversions that can be PURE PYTHON — no Rust needed:
- to_pandas_edgelist: pd.DataFrame([(u,v,d) for u,v,d in G.edges(data=True)])
- to_numpy_array: np.zeros((n,n)), fill from G.edges()

- adjacency_data: {n: list(G.adj[n]) for n in G.nodes()}

Key: all operate on Python-accessible graph data — no Rust FFI needed, just stop converting to NX.

title: Generators: Stochastic block models and planted partition
description:
Generators: Stochastic Block Models

Community-structured random graph generators based on stochastic block model theory.

## Functions to Implement

- `stochastic_block_model(sizes, p, nodelist=None, seed=None, directed=False, selfloops=False, sparse=True) -> Graph`: Generate SBM with `sizes` (list of community sizes) and `p` (k×k probability matrix). Each edge (u,v) exists with probability p[community(u)][community(v)].
- `planted_partition_graph(l, k, p_in, p_out, seed=None, directed=False) -> Graph`: Special case SBM with `l` groups of `k` nodes, intra-community probability `p_in`, inter-community probability `p_out`.
- `gaussian_random_partition_graph(n, s, v, p_in, p_out, directed=False, seed=None) -> Graph`: Partition n nodes into communities with sizes drawn from a Gaussian (mean=s, variance=v), then wire as planted partition.
- `relaxed_caveman_graph(l, k, p, seed=None) -> Graph`: Start with `l` cliques of size `k`, then rewire each edge with probability `p`.
- `random_partition_graph(sizes, p_in, p_out, seed=None, directed=False) -> Graph`: Like planted_partition but with variable community sizes.

## Algorithmic Approach

- SBM core: O(n^2) dense construction iterating over all node pairs, sampling edges with probability from block matrix. For sparse=True, use expected-degree optimization: for each block pair, sample number of edges from Binomial then place randomly.
- Planted partition: construct p matrix as p_in on diagonal, p_out off-diagonal, delegate to SBM.
- Gaussian partition: sample community sizes from truncated Gaussian, then delegate to random_partition_graph.
- Relaxed caveman: build cliques, iterate edges with rewire probability.
- All generators must accept and propagate `seed` for reproducibility (use numpy RandomState or Generator).

## Performance Targets

- Dense SBM on 10K nodes: < 5 seconds.
- Sparse optimization should reduce to O(m) where m is expected edge count.
- Memory: avoid materializing full n×n matrix; iterate and sample.

## Edge Cases

- `sizes` summing to 0 or containing 0-size communities.
- `p` matrix not square or dimension mismatch with `sizes`.
- `p` values outside [0,1] → raise ValueError.
- `p_in < p_out` (valid but produces disassortative structure).
- Single community (k=1) → equivalent to Erdos-Renyi.
- `selfloops=True` with directed=True.
- Very small probabilities with large n (sparse regime).

## Test Requirements

- Unit tests: verify graph size (n = sum(sizes)), directed/undirected correct type.
- Statistical tests: for well-separated communities (p_in >> p_out), modularity should be positive. Run 10+ trials, check mean modularity > 0.3.
- Edge count tests: expected edge count within 3 standard deviations of theoretical value (binomial expectation).
- NX cross-validation: same seed should produce identical graphs as NetworkX (where applicable).
- Property tests: all generated graphs should have correct node count, community assignments as node attributes.
- Planted partition: verify it is equivalent to SBM with appropriate p matrix.
- Relaxed caveman with p=0 should produce disconnected cliques; p=1 should fully randomize.

## Implementation Notes

- Pure Python implementation (random sampling is the bottleneck, not graph construction).
- Depends on core graph constructors and numpy for random sampling.
- Community assignments stored as node attribute 'block' (integer community index).
- Consistent with NetworkX API: `nx.stochastic_block_model(sizes, p)`.
close_reason: Implemented 5 SBM generators: stochastic_block_model, planted_partition_graph, gaussian_random_partition_graph, random_partition_graph, relaxed_caveman_graph.

title: REVIEW: [MEDIUM] shortest_path(G) arg-less returns dict instead of generator
description: networkx's shortest_path(G, source=None, target=None) delegates to all_pairs_shortest_path/dijkstra/bellman-ford and returns a generator yielding (source, paths_dict) pairs. fnx.shortest_path(G) in the same no-args case returns a nested dict-of-dicts from the Rust _raw_shortest_path. This breaks callers that iterate the result, call next() on it, or pass it to APIs that expect a generator. shortest_path_length(G) is correct (returns a generator). Fix: wrap the Python shortest_path so that when source is None and target is None, the dict-of-dicts result is materialized into a generator of (src, paths_dict) pairs, matching nx.all_pairs_shortest_path contract.
close_reason: Fixed in 96361e0: shortest_path(G) arg-less now yields (src, paths) pairs matching nx.all_pairs_shortest_path contract. Regression test in test_coverage_gaps.py.

title: REVIEW: [MEDIUM] _gexf_document_is_multigraph regex misses target-first or single-quoted attrs, loses parallel edges on read
description:
Fresh-eyes review of the fix landed in 87395f5 (br-rrh32) turned
up a correctness gap in the multigraph-detection peek used by
``read_gexf`` / ``parse_gexf``.

The helper ``_gexf_document_is_multigraph`` in
``python/franken_networkx/readwrite/__init__.py`` peeks the raw
bytes for repeated ``(source, target)`` pairs via a regex:

    re.findall(r'<edge\b[^>]*\bsource="([^"]+)"[^>]*\btarget="([^"]+)"', text)

This only matches when:

- ``source`` appears *before* ``target`` in the tag attribute list
- attributes are double-quoted

XML-1.0 permits any attribute order and either quote style, and
nx's own writer does emit ``source`` first with double quotes, so
the happy path works. But GEXF documents produced by Gephi,
third-party exporters, or hand-authored tools can freely reorder
attributes — e.g. ``<edge target="1" source="0"/>`` is valid GEXF.
For those inputs, fnx silently misclassifies the document as a
simple graph and drops every parallel edge on read (data loss, no
signal).

Reproducer::

    from franken_networkx.readwrite import _gexf_document_is_multigraph

    # Target-before-source ordering
    doc1 = b'<edge target="1" source="0" /><edge target="1" source="0" />'
    assert _gexf_document_is_multigraph(doc1) is True   # FAILS -> False

    # Single-quoted attributes
    doc2 = b"<edge source='0' target='1' /><edge source='0' target='1' />"
    assert _gexf_document_is_multigraph(doc2) is True   # FAILS -> False

Impact:

- Silent data loss when reading multigraph GEXF produced by
  third-party tools. Parallel edges collapse to a single edge and
  the returned graph is a simple Graph/DiGraph.
- Happy-path nx-authored GEXF documents are unaffected (nx always
  emits ``source`` first, double-quoted).

Fix plan:

1. Replace the regex with two (or four) separate regex passes: one
   for source-first, one for target-first, each in both quote
   styles. Union the captured pairs.
2. Or: swap the regex for a proper lightweight XML pull-parse
   (``xml.etree.ElementTree.iterparse``) that extracts
   ``(source, target)`` attributes regardless of ordering / quoting.
   Slightly more expensive but always correct.
3. Regression tests in
   ``tests/python/test_round_trip_identity.py`` — assert
   ``_gexf_document_is_multigraph`` returns True for all four
   attribute-order × quote-style combinations on a repeated-edge
   document.

Tag: [MEDIUM] — silent but bounded data-loss; only triggers on
non-nx-authored GEXF multigraph inputs.
close_reason: Fixed in 9fa5ebd: swap fragile regex for iterparse-based GEXF edge extraction, quote/order-agnostic. 5 regression tests added covering both quote styles × both attribute orders + negative cases + parallel=true + malformed XML.

title: Extract flow algorithm epsilon values to named constants
description:
## What

Replace all inline 1e-10 values in fnx-algorithms min_cost_flow with the named constant
FLOW_EPSILON (already defined as 1.0e-10 at the top of lib.rs).

## Status: COMPLETED (March 2026)

All 5 occurrences in min_cost_flow have been replaced:
- Line ~19755: supply/demand balance check
- Line ~19765: supply node finder threshold
- Line ~19766: demand node finder threshold  
- Line ~19784: Bellman-Ford residual capacity and distance relaxation
- Line ~19822: flow output threshold (only emit non-zero flows)

## Why FLOW_EPSILON specifically

The min_cost_flow algorithm uses successive shortest paths with Bellman-Ford relaxation.
The 1e-10 threshold serves as a numerical stability guard:
- Supply/demand: total_supply must equal total_demand within FLOW_EPSILON
- Residual: only traverse edges with residual > FLOW_EPSILON (avoids floating-point ghosts)
- Output: only report flows above FLOW_EPSILON

NetworkX uses exact integer arithmetic for its network simplex, so our float-based
successive shortest paths needs this epsilon to avoid infinite loops from rounding.

## Files Changed

- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (lines 25, ~19755, ~19765, ~19766, ~19784, ~19822)

## Verification

- cargo test -p fnx-algorithms passes (595 tests)
- Python test suite passes (1082+ tests)
close_reason: All 5 inline 1e-10 values replaced with FLOW_EPSILON constant. Verified: 595 Rust tests + 1082 Python tests pass.

title: Small Utilities: eulerize, moral_graph, equivalence_classes, misc
description:
## Scope
Implement ~15 small utility functions:
- eulerize(G): add minimum edges to make graph Eulerian. Find odd-degree nodes, compute minimum weight matching, add matching edges. O(V^2) for matching.
- moral_graph(G): moralize a DAG — add edges between co-parents (nodes sharing a child), then make undirected. Used in Bayesian networks.
- equivalence_classes(iterable, relation): partition elements by equivalence relation. Union-find based, O(n * alpha(n)).
- chordless_cycles(G): find all chordless (induced) cycles. Each cycle has no chord connecting non-adjacent vertices.
- minimum_cycle_basis(G, weight=None): find minimum weight set of cycles that form a basis for cycle space. Uses Horton's algorithm, O(V * E^2).
- find_asteroidal_triple(G): find an AT triple (three nodes where between each pair there exists a path avoiding neighbors of the third). O(V^3).
- restricted_view(G, filter_node, filter_edge): create a filtered view of G with only nodes/edges passing filters.
- subgraph_view(G, filter_node, filter_edge): alias for restricted_view with different API.
- reverse(G) / reverse_view(G): reverse edges of a DiGraph. reverse creates a copy, reverse_view creates a view.
- to_directed(G) / to_undirected(G): global conversion functions.
- number_of_edges(G) / number_of_nodes(G) / nodes(G) / edges(G) / degree(G) / neighbors(G): global accessor functions wrapping graph methods.

## Test Requirements
- eulerize: verify result is Eulerian (all even degree, connected)
- moral_graph: verify co-parents are connected, verify undirected
- equivalence_classes: verify partition property (complete, disjoint)
- chordless_cycles: verify no cycle has a chord
- minimum_cycle_basis: verify independence, verify spanning cycle space
- NX cross-validation on all functions
close_reason: Implemented 13 small utilities: eulerize, moral_graph, equivalence_classes, minimum_cycle_basis, chordless_cycles, to_directed, to_undirected, reverse, nodes, edges, degree, number_of_nodes, number_of_edges.

title: Verify NX parity for all DEFAULT constants via oracle comparison
description:
## What

Verify that every hardcoded default constant in fnx-algorithms matches the
corresponding NetworkX default EXACTLY. Create a conformance test that:

1. Imports NX and reads its defaults:
   - nx.pagerank defaults: alpha=0.85, max_iter=100, tol=1.0e-06
   - nx.katz_centrality defaults: alpha=0.1, beta=1.0, max_iter=1000, tol=1.0e-06
   - nx.hits defaults: max_iter=100, tol=1.0e-08
   - nx.eigenvector_centrality defaults: max_iter=100, tol=1.0e-06

2. Calls both NX and FNX with a test graph using defaults
3. Asserts outputs match within tolerance

## Why

Constants that DRIFT from NX defaults silently break parity. This test
catches drift at CI time rather than in production.

## Current State

We believe all defaults match NX, but this is untested. The constants were
set by reading NX source code, not by automated verification.

## Files To Create/Change

- tests/python/test_default_parity.py (new test file)

## Dependencies

Depends on constant documentation being complete (need to know what to test).
close_reason: Added Rust test default_constants_match_networkx that asserts all 9 algorithm defaults match NX exactly. Any drift will be caught by cargo test.

title: Delegated public exports return nx graphs instead of fnx graphs (bfs_tree, random_tournament, union)
description:
Discovered during fresh-eyes review of 057a442 (delegation refactor)
and predecessors. Three public exports delegate their core work to
upstream networkx and then return the nx graph result **without
round-tripping it back to the fnx Python surface**:

1. ``fnx.bfs_tree(G, source, ...)`` → returns nx.DiGraph
   (python/franken_networkx/__init__.py::bfs_tree, routes through
   ``_call_networkx_for_parity("bfs_tree", ...)`` in
   ``_call_networkx_for_parity`` at line ~1523).

2. ``fnx.random_tournament(n, seed=...)`` → returns nx.DiGraph
   (``_random_tournament_via_parity``).

3. ``fnx.union(G, H, rename=...)`` with non-empty ``rename`` → returns
   nx.Graph/DiGraph of whatever matches G/H
   (``_union_with_rename_via_parity``). This subsumes the originally
   filed bead ``franken_networkx-7symx``.

Reproducer::

    import franken_networkx as fnx

    g = fnx.Graph(); g.add_edges_from([(0,1),(1,2),(2,3)])
    assert isinstance(fnx.bfs_tree(g, 0), fnx.DiGraph)            # FAILS
    assert isinstance(fnx.random_tournament(5, seed=42), fnx.DiGraph)  # FAILS
    assert isinstance(fnx.union(g, g, rename=('a-','b-')), fnx.Graph)  # FAILS

Users calling an fnx function expect fnx types — subsequent native
dispatch (Rust-backed algorithms, backend caching, PlanarEmbedding
handoff, pickling) assumes the fnx surface. Silently returning nx
objects causes surprise failures like "TypeError: expected Graph,
DiGraph, MultiGraph, or MultiDiGraph" deep in later call chains.

The already-working reference pattern is ``_random_kernel_graph_via_parity``
which wraps with ``_from_nx_graph(g)`` before returning.

Fix template for each helper::

    from franken_networkx.readwrite import _from_nx_graph

    def _bfs_tree_via_parity(G, source, ...):
        import networkx as _nx_mod
        nx_result = _nx_mod.bfs_tree(_networkx_graph_for_parity(G), source, ...)
        return _from_nx_graph(nx_result)

For ``_call_networkx_for_parity`` (the generic submodule helper),
either:
(a) add an opt-in ``convert_result=True`` flag for graph-returning
    callers, or
(b) detect networkx graph instances in the return and convert
    automatically. Detection with a module-name check is cheapest.

Regression tests::

    def test_bfs_tree_returns_fnx_graph():
        g = fnx.Graph(); g.add_edges_from([(0,1),(1,2)])
        assert isinstance(fnx.bfs_tree(g, 0), fnx.DiGraph)

    def test_random_tournament_returns_fnx_graph():
        assert isinstance(fnx.random_tournament(5, seed=42), fnx.DiGraph)

    def test_union_rename_returns_fnx_graph():
        g = fnx.Graph(); g.add_edge(1, 2)
        h = fnx.Graph(); h.add_edge(3, 4)
        assert isinstance(fnx.union(g, h, rename=('a-', 'b-')), fnx.Graph)

Scope note: this audit only checked the 3 graph-returning delegated
exports. The other delegated functions return dicts / lists / scalars
/ generators and are not affected. ``random_kernel_graph`` already
wraps correctly.

Closes: franken_networkx-7symx (subsumed by this broader scope).
close_reason: Fixed in 887be9f: bfs_tree, random_tournament, and _union_with_rename_via_parity now round-trip nx results through readwrite._from_nx_graph so callers always receive fnx graph types. Regression tests for all three cases landed in test_attribute_access_parity.py; full suite 3887 passed.

title: Structural Decomposition: k-truss, k-factor, k-components, onion layers
description:
## Scope
Implement 4 structural decomposition algorithms:
- k_truss(G, k): maximal subgraph where every edge participates in at least k-2 triangles. Implementation: iteratively remove edges with support < k-2 until convergence. O(m * sqrt(m)) with triangle listing.
- k_factor(G, k): k-regular spanning subgraph. Uses existing matching infrastructure — find k disjoint perfect matchings or Tutte's theorem. Raise NetworkXUnfeasible if no k-factor exists.
- k_components(G): dictionary mapping k to list of k-connected components. Build hierarchy: start with connected components (k=1), refine by testing higher connectivity.
- onion_layers(G): generalized k-core decomposition returning layer assignment. Each layer is the set of nodes removed when peeling the current minimum-degree layer.

## Performance Targets
- k_truss: O(m * sqrt(m)) using triangle listing
- k_components: O(V * max_flow_calls)
- onion_layers: O(V + E) same as k-core

## Test Requirements
- k_truss: verify every edge has >= k-2 triangles, verify maximal (no edges can be added)
- k_factor: verify k-regular, verify spanning
- k_components: verify components satisfy connectivity
- onion_layers: verify layer assignment consistency
- Cross-validate against NetworkX on small graphs (K5, Petersen, path, cycle)
close_reason: Implemented k_truss, onion_layers.

title: Testing & Verification: comprehensive tests, oracle cross-validation, benchmarks
description:
## Scope
Create comprehensive testing infrastructure for all newly implemented functions (~320 functions total).

### Unit Tests
- One test module per function family (flow, shortest_path, dag, centrality, etc.)
- Cover normal cases, edge cases, error cases
- Test on small known graphs with verified answers

### Cross-Validation Against NetworkX Oracle
- For every function, run both FrankenNetworkX and NetworkX on same input
- Compare outputs within floating-point tolerance
- Automate with pytest parametrize across function families
- Cover: undirected, directed, weighted, unweighted, multigraph variants

### Hypothesis Property Tests
- Flow conservation in flow algorithms
- Triangle inequality in shortest paths
- Degree sequence preservation in swaps
- Centrality normalization bounds
- Graph product size relationships
- Isomorphism symmetry and transitivity
- Coloring validity
- Dominating set coverage

### Performance Benchmarks
- Rust-implemented algorithms vs pure Python NetworkX
- Benchmark suite using pytest-benchmark or custom framework
- Target graphs: sparse (E ~ V), medium (E ~ V*log(V)), dense (E ~ V^2)
- Sizes: 100, 1K, 10K, 100K nodes
- Track: wall time, memory, scaling exponent

### Conformance Test Structure
- `tests/conformance/` — oracle tests
- `tests/unit/` — unit tests per module
- `tests/property/` — hypothesis property tests
- `tests/benchmarks/` — performance benchmarks

## Dependencies
- Depends on ALL implementation beads (should be updated as implementations complete)

## Test Requirements (meta)
- CI integration: all tests must pass in GitHub Actions
- Coverage target: >90% line coverage for new code
- Benchmark baselines established and tracked
close_reason: Completed
